name: 102 House
address: 506 The House of Roosevelt, No.27 Zhongshan East 1st Road, Shanghai
history:
Going up in the world: 102 House was originally opened in 2006 by chef-owner Xu Jingye as a small private kitchen in Foshan, Guangdong – but in 2021, after years of building a cult following through word-of-mouth recommendations, the restaurant upped sticks to a location worthy of its stellar reputation: the fifth floor of the House of Roosevelt Building on Shanghai's most famous waterfront promenade, the Bund.

Ancient traditions, preserved: Chef Jingye is dedicated to reviving Cantonese banquet cuisine – a formal, multi-course style of cooking historically served at weddings and other grand celebrations – and refining it to appeal to the modern palate. Seasonality lies at the heart of his philosophy, so his menus constantly evolve to showcase ingredients at their peak.

What can I expect? Sweet-and-sour pork is one of Jingye's signature dishes and a mainstay of the 102 House menu. Of course, this being an eatery with a seasonal focus, the sauce changes throughout the year: it's made with pineapple in spring, lychee in summer, peach in autumn and strawberry in winter. Guests can also enjoy steamed dim sum and dumplings, meat courses like roast duck and braised pork belly, and double-boiled soups, slow cooked to draw out rich flavours from Chinese herbs, chicken and fish.

An intimate affair: With just a few tables in the main dining area and three private rooms, 102 House exudes understated elegance, blending refined decorative touches like crystal chandeliers and linen tablecloths with subtle nods to Cantonese heritage. The best part? Large windows frame twinkling views of the Huangpu River and the Bund skyline, throwing the restaurant's serene interior into stark contrast with the vibrant city beyond.
verdict: Ancient Cantonese culinary traditions revived with passion
chefName: Jingye is dedicated to reviving Cantonese banquet cuisine

address: Via Edmondo de Amicis, 22, 20123 Milan, Italy
history: Ranked in The World's 50 Best Bars list since 2019, Milan's clandestine cocktail haven, 1930, moved to a new location this year in the Colonne di San Lorenzo neighbourhood. It's now in the basement of Mag La Pusterla, which used to be a historical Milanese cafe. The bar remains an exclusive club, with only its 193 members able to book in advance. Other hopeful patrons can have a drink at Mag (it shares ownership with 1930) and ask if there's a spot for them downstairs. If space allows, a host will lead them to the secret entrance. The new location greets guests with exposed brick vaults and low lighting, a fitting backdrop for the Prohibition-era mystique that surrounds 1930. While classics from a bygone age are flawlessly executed by the team, 1930 is rightly famous for cocktails that are truly from the 21st century. Created under the supervision of co-owner and head bartender Benjamin Cavagna, the latest menu 1930 à la carte blurs the line between food and mixology. Divided into five sections mimicking a restaurant menu – from appetizers to desserts – it features iconoclastic drinks such as Tortellini in Brodo, a reinterpretation of the boulevardier made with nutmeg-infused whiskey and served, much like the pasta dish it's named after, in a hot chicken broth. Another standout is the Parmigiano Colada, where the tropical classic is enhanced with parmesan cheese foam, truffle oil and pepper.
verdict: Where classic Italian dishes are transformed into wondrous cocktails

name: 7th Door
address: Seoul, Gangnam-gu, Hakdong-ro 97-gil, 41 4층
history:
The mastermind: Kim Dae-chun is well known to followers of Asia's 50 Best Restaurants, with his first Seoul restaurant, Toc Toc, winning the One To Watch Award in 2017 and ranking No.41 Asia's 50 Best Restaurants in 2019. After leaving behind a career in music for the kitchen, Kim has been cooking for more than 20 years.

Culinary philosophy: The DNA of the restaurant is built on the fundamentals of traditional Korean cuisine: ageing and fermentation. To eat here is to experience the intersection where Kim's deep appreciation for ancient, classic cooking collides with his thirst for innovative ingredients and modern methods. Kim aims to take diners through 'the seven tastes of food', including the basic five senses alongside the taste of fermentation, with the seventh being the chef's vision for drawing out the essence of all the ingredients in Korea's larder.

Are there really seven doors? Symbolically speaking, yes! On arrival, you'll be guided down a hallway that is supposedly six doorways-long and lined with glass cabinets that display animal and vegetable matter in varying stages of fermentation, some of which has been aged for up to 10 years. At the end of the corridor, you'll find the seventh door, which is the gateway to a dining room with only 14 seats and a clear view of the open kitchen that hints at the theatrical performance guests will experience over the course of the three-hour-long tasting menu.

Playful and delicious: Despite its grandeur, 7th Door has a surprisingly playful sense of humour based around a concept of refined fried finger-food that Chef Kim weaves effortlessly into his take on traditional dishes. Expect classic Korean tteokbokki to arrive swimming in a slick of aromatic black truffle sauce or modern dishes, such as delicate slices of aged fish paired with pickled jalapeño ssamjang.
verdict: A playful guided tour through the seven tastes of Korea
chefName: Kim weaves effortlessly into his take on traditional dishes. Expect classic Korean tteokbokki to arrive swimming in a sl

name: A Casa do Porco
address: Rua Araújo, nº 124 Centro, São Paulo, Brazil
history:
Homage to the hog: Meaning 'House of the Pig' in Portuguese, A Casa do Porco is named for its focus on pork in all its forms, with a tasting menu and a la carte offering that uses every part of the pig. In recent years, it has adapted to changing dietary requirements, introducing a vegetarian menu that tastes as good as the original. The quirkily decorated restaurant in the heart of downtown São Paulo is owned by chef Jefferson Rueda.

From farm to city: The tasting menu, Porco D.O.C., features pork and vegetables from Sítio Rueda, the farm Rueda bought in his countryside hometown of São José do Rio Pardo in 2020. Diners begin with small bites including raw pork soufflé with curd, radish and horseradish. There's a fun take on burger, fries and milkshake – yes, all made with pork, before the main course, Porco San Zé, a house classic, which comes with pork sausage, beans, banana tartare, farofa and 'whatever came from our garden'. Dessert arrives to refresh, with soursop, vanilla, cupuaçu and sapodilla (Amazonian fruits).

A democratic approach: A Casa do Porco is committed to offering something for everyone, with a democratic and inclusive approach sparked by Rueda's own humble upbringings.

Country-inspired cocktails: As well-thought-out as the food, the drinks programme includes alcoholic or non-alcoholic cocktails and wines. Try a Cachaça juice box with lemon and jam, or Porco Veio, made with Cachaça, Jack Daniel's Single Barrel, Jack Daniel's Old No.7 corn infused with bacon, honey, orange bitters and sparkling water.
verdict: A field-to-table funhouse from a powerhouse Brazilian chef

name: Acamaya
address: 3070 Dauphine St. New Orleans, LA 70117
history:
Gulf state of mind: A love letter to Mexican mariscos – vibrant hot and cold seafood – Acamaya is the vision of sisters Lydia and Ana Castro, the latter of whom first made her mark as head chef of Lengua Madre, the now-closed New Orleans restaurant famed for its inventive modern Mexican tasting menus. Opened in 2024, the venture treats Mexican takes on the Gulf's bounty with the same sense of reverence as New Orleans' own storied seafood traditions, carving out a distinctive place within the city's dining scene.

Heavy on heritage: This is not your stereotypical Tex-Mex spot. Contemporary and inviting, Acamaya channels the spirit of Mexico City in every detail, from its terracotta lattice walls, warm wood ceilings and pastel tiling to its Mexico-sourced cups, plates, tables and chairs. An upbeat soundtrack of Central and South American music sets the tone, while low lighting draws the eye towards the open kitchen, where diners can watch the chefs at work.

Treats from the deep: Expect a mixture of time-honoured recipes and modern twists – for example, classic Gulf shrimp aguachile sits alongside a hamachi tostada inspired by tacos al pastor. Make sure to try the arroz negro, a rice dish teeming with mussels, squid and huitlacoche, a prized fungus that's also known as 'Mexican truffle'. If you need help translating the dishes, don't worry; there's a glossary on the back of every menu, and the staff are always happy to guide you. The team sources from small fisheries, local fishmongers and purveyors as much as possible, and works with local and regional farmers to acquire – and sometimes co-grow – the best produce.

Finish with: A rich tonka bean flan infused with the flavours of almond and vanilla, or a masa tres leches cake with toasty corn undertones. Alternatively, linger a little longer over a nightcap – margaritas are never a bad idea, especially in a setting that feels so effortlessly south of the border.
verdict: A taste of the Gulf through a Mexican lens

name: Addison by William Bradley
address: 5200 Grand Del Mar Way, San Diego, CA 92130, United States
history:
A pastoral powerhouse: Take a short drive north of San Diego into the rolling hills of picturesque Carmel Valley and you'll soon stumble on Addison by William Bradley, a restaurant that's considered by many to be the benchmark for contemporary Californian fine dining. Part of the Mediterranean-inspired Fairmont Grand Del Mar resort, this celebrated eatery oozes old-world luxury – picture high ceilings, stone flooring, limestone fireplaces and arched windows that frame soul-stealing views of shallow canyons.

Chef-driven cuisine: William Bradley has held the position of chef-director of Addison by William Bradley since its inception in 2006, shaping a menu built entirely around his culinary philosophy of California Gastronomy: an approach emphasising seasonality, precision, global techniques and locally sourced ingredients. Each dish is carefully conceived to reflect California's unique climate, cultural diversity and coastal proximity, resulting in a style of cooking that feels firmly rooted in place – and quite unlike anything found elsewhere.

Quality and restraint: Choreographed to run like clockwork, the multi-course tasting experience is highly sought after, with reservations filling up well in advance. Menus constantly evolve according to the time of year, but luxurious produce and clean, refined flavours always take centre stage; signature offerings have included the likes of koshihikari rice with smoked sabayon sauce and caviar, chicken liver 'churros' and chawanmushi (Japanese egg custard) with shellfish.

The perfect pairing: Addison by William Bradley takes its beverage programme just as seriously as its cooking – just take the glass-shrouded wine cellar at the centre of the dining room as proof. More than just a quirky design feature, the room serves to keep the restaurant's extensive wine list at the forefront of diners' minds, from champagnes to exciting discoveries from small producers around the world.
verdict: The home of California Gastronomy

name: Afluente
address: Kr 3a 57-35 Chapinero Alto, Bogotá, Colombia
history:
What's in a name? Afluente, meaning 'tributary' in Spanish, wouldn't exist without Colombia's páramos: biodiverse, high-altitude ecosystems, set along the Andean mountain range. The páramos are a vital source of clean water for city-dwellers in Bogotá; for centuries, they have also provided Indigenous people with land for agriculture and grazing, plus abundant plants for medicinal use. More recently, they have become a fertile muse for Afluente's head chef, Jeferson García.

Chef CV: García's culinary background is varied and impressive. Before opening Afluente, he worked in some of Latin America's best kitchens, including Boragó in Santiago, and travelled extensively around the world, honing his craft in Thailand (at Gaggan and GA) and in Denmark (at Jordnær and Kadeau, among others).

What's on the menu? Dishes at Afluente use a carefully considered selection of ingredients from the páramos and other connected ecosystems, drawing attention to these natural environments, their rich offerings and the workers who cultivate this produce. Fittingly, the tasting menu is called Conectividad (Connectivity), but if you opt for a la carte, dishes include a starter of prawns, plantains, Andean tubers and pumpkin and a main of rice, pork, insulso (steamed or baked sweet cornmeal dough) and local potatoes.

Style and substance: This connection to the natural world extends through the design of the dining room, too: the rough plastered walls, muted tones and wooden accents bring an organic feel to the space, while the beautifully considered lighting and crockery keep things chic.
verdict: A culinary celebration of Colombia’s unique Andean ecosystems

name: Albi
address: 1346 4th St. SE. Washington, DC 20003, United States
history:
Fired up: Chef-owner Michael Rafidi opened Albi in 2020 with a live-fire, wood-burning hearth as its centrepiece. The restaurant, located in DC's Navy Yard neighbourhood, offers Rafidi's alternate take on traditional Palestinian cuisine using modern techniques. Every dish is evocative of his grandparents' ancestry in Ramallah, Palestine and their home cooking. The hearth isn't just for show – it's a functioning fire that touches almost every dish before it hits the plate.

Labour of love: Albi not only means 'my heart' in Arabic – the restaurant's name also reflects chef Rafidi's conviction that food has always been personal and a powerful connection to family and tradition. Before he launched Albi, the Maryland native spent more than a year scouring cookbooks, digging up old family recipes and holding multiple pop-ups to further sharpen his culinary direction. The result? Dishes bursting with flavour, colour and memory.

On the menu: The chef's choice five-course tasting menu highlights seasonal mid-Atlantic produce reimagined with Rafidi's signature Palestinian style, including dishes like Maryland blue crab with sweetcorn and serrano oil and a wood-fired potato pita with foraged mushrooms and hummus. For those dining a la carte, the menu features crab rice maqluba, Palestine's national dish.

Kitchen credentials: Before he lit a fire under DC's dining scene, Rafidi studied at Le Cordon Bleu in Pennsylvania before spending three years at Blue Duck Tavern, working shifts that – as a prelude of things to come – included managing the restaurant's wood-burning oven. Eventually, Rafidi moved to California and worked shoulder to shoulder with Michael Mina as executive chef of the celebrated, now-shuttered RN74 in San Francisco. Rafidi returned to DC five years later, and with a renewed focus on the food from his childhood, opened Albi.
verdict: Modern Palestinian cooking straight from the hearth
chefName: Rafidi's conviction that food has always been personal and a powerful connection to family and tradition. Before he laun

name: Alcalde
address: Av. México 2903, Vallarta Nte., 44690 Guadalajara, Jal., Mexico
history:
In a nutshell: Meaning 'mayor' in Spanish, Alcalde's fare is based on chef Francisco 'Paco' Ruano's simple, 'frank' Mexican cooking. Set in a stylish, welcoming setting in Guadalajara, Alcalde makes diners want to return again and again with its menu, which is deeply connected to the natural rhythms of the land.

What's on the menu? The menu has worldwide influences but is rooted in the Jalisco region. Local ingredients dominate, with dishes such as aguachile verde with shrimp, apple, cucumber and seaweed; fish of the day with chintextle (Mexican smoked chilli paste), yellow curry and mushroom ceviche; and rice pudding with cinnamon ice cream, soybean skin and toasted chocolate. The restaurant offers a full tasting menu, as well as a la carte.

Chef CV: Ruano honed his skills on cruise ships in Alaska and the Caribbean before enrolling at the Luis Irizar cooking school in Spain. He went on to work at esteemed restaurants such as Mugaritz and Best of the Best restaurants El Celler de Can Roca in Spain and Noma in Denmark, bringing back contemporary global techniques he now employs with Mexican ingredient at Alcalde.

Special recognition: Alcalde has long been a favourite in the Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants rankings and featured at No.51 on The World's 50 Best Restaurants 2025 list.
verdict: Stylishly humble Mexican gastronomy

name: Alchemist
address: Refshalevej 173C, 1432 Copenhagen, Denmark
history:
Getting there: Alchemist is located in Refshaleøen, a relatively remote part of Copenhagen known for its industrial buildings and a famous former shipyard. You have to go to the very end of the stretch before you get to the two-tonne-heavy bronze doors with decorations reminiscent of Narnia or Middle-Earth, which lead you into the mystical world of Alchemist. Wait for a few moments and they automatically swing open. Let the show begin!

What should I expect? Be prepared for a journey unlike any meal you’ve had before. There are no courses, only ‘impressions’ – 50 of them, actually – divided into several ‘acts’ that takes guests through different locations, types of art and extraordinary craftsmanship over a seven-hour, multi-sensory odyssey. It’s the realisation of head chef and mastermind Rasmus Munk’s dream of a holistic and meaningful dining experience.

Tell me more: You will be dining under a dome-shaped roof with various graphic sceneries that change throughout the dinner, but the prime reason it all works is because Alchemist’s cuisine is rooted in a strong foundation of classic techniques, modern research and Chef Munk’s obsessive search for the best ingredients. Expect ‘Space Bread’ canapés topped with caviar, the best seafood and pigeon aged in beeswax, just to mention a few delicacies – always presented in creative ways that will impress, as well as bring out your inner child.

Designed to challenge: No guests are forced to attend lectures from staff during a dinner at Alchemist, but if you’re curious to dig deeper, there’s plenty to be enthralled and educated by. How about a freeze-dried butterfly to bring as a sustainable source of protein? Or some ‘Food for Thought’ where guests must scoop the ‘brains’ (cherry meringue filled with a lamb’s brain mousse) from a realistic mould of a human head.

Other projects: Despite reservations selling out within minutes and five-digit waiting lists, Munk is not resting on his laurels. Instead, he is using the attention to raise funds to feed homeless people in Copenhagen and create clean water solutions for people in Africa. He is a man on a mission.
verdict: Genre-bending, world-building gastronomy of the highest order

name: Alice
address: 84-20 Dosan-daero 55-gil, Gangnam-gu, Seoul
history: Locating this trendy spot inspired by the classic Lewis Carroll tale is an adventure in itself as it involves following white rabbit signs, descending a narrow flight of stairs and finding a secret door at the back of a flower shop in Seoul's Gangnam district. Once inside, you'll discover a sophisticated, wood-panelled sanctum where the cocktails will transport you to a wonderland all of their own. There are no sleight-of-hand tricks to be found in the cocktail craft here, just innovative sips served with a touch of fairytale magic. Debonair owner Terry Kim and joint head bartenders Mason Park and Leo Kim lead the bar team, which serves up suitably imaginative and seasonally varied creations inspired by Carroll's timeless masterpiece, such as Make Me "Bigger" (rum, amaro, banana juice, tonka) and Unbirthday Tea Party (whiskey, oloroso sherry, black tea, coconut). The latest menu, Millenium Cocktail packs a flavour punch with drinks crafted from fresh fruits and homemade syrups, bitters and jellies. If playful cocktails are not what you are after, the back bar is overflowing with top quality sipping spirits and premium local soju.
verdict: Locating this trendy spot inspired by the classic Lewis Carroll tale is an adventure in itself as it involves following white rabbit signs, descending a narrow flight of stairs and finding a secret door at the back of a flower shop in Seoul's Gangnam district. Once inside, you'll discover a sophisticated, wood-panelled sanctum where the cocktails will transport you to a wonderland all of their own. There are no sleight-of-hand tricks to be found in the cocktail craft here, just innovative sips served with a touch of fairytale magic. Debonair owner Terry Kim and joint head bartenders Mason Park and Leo Kim lead the bar team, which serves up suitably imaginative and seasonally varied creations inspired by Carroll's timeless masterpiece, such as Make Me "Bigger" (rum, amaro, banana juice, tonka) and Unbirthday Tea Party (whiskey, oloroso sherry, black tea, coconut). The latest menu, Millenium Cocktail packs a flavour punch with drinks crafted from fresh fruits and homemade syrups, bitters and jellies. If playful cocktails are not what you are after, the back bar is overflowing with top quality sipping spirits and premium local soju.

name: Alquímico
address: Cl. del Colegio #34-24, Cartagena de Indias, Colombia
history: Set in a 19th-century mansion house in the pulsating heart of Cartagena on the Caribbean coast of Colombia, Alquímico is three floors of full-on fiesta. The party is orchestrated by the ground-floor courtyard bar, its all-female team of bartenders throwing out expertly crafted cocktails while also spontaneously throwing out dance moves. Climb the centrepiece staircase and the tempo slows a little, with more classic drinks starring, while the roof bar is all-out Reggaetón club. If you come late, when the place is packed out – up to 1,200 people – every floor is bouncing. But Alquímico is a party with purpose – Colombian culture and community runs like a rich seam through everything that happens here. Bar owner and philanthropist Jean Trinh runs many community-led projects, with proceeds from the new three-part menu at Alquímico going towards his causes. The first floor menu celebrates his Comunidad project, which supports farmers in a previously war-torn region whose rare regional ingredients underpin the menu. In 2026 Alquímico will fund the construction of buildings there, including a school. If there's one cocktail to try – all though in truth they're all delicious – it's Mango, a blend of tequila, homemade mango vermouth and hop maceration. Up on the second floor the drinks fund a bartender academy and a music foundation for young people from disadvantaged backgrounds, while the third floor's offering draws on ingredients from Alquímico's own coffee district farm and will help to underwrite the region's reforesting. Wherever you find yourself, make sure you try some of the bar's flavoured meads – their honey base produced by the farm's own bees. As you explore each floor, you'll discover that there is provenance and meaning behind everything at Alquímico.
verdict: Latin party vibes, with drinks that showcase Colombia

name: Angel's Share
address: 45 Grove St, New York, NY 10014, United States
history: In 1993, Tony Yoshida opened Angel's Share and ushered in a new era for New York. The bar introduced a generation to then-forgotten craft cocktails, as well as to Japan's rich drinks heritage. In 2022, the upstairs lounge was forced out of its iconic downtown location; two years later, Tony's daughter Erina Yoshida reopened Angel's Share in new West Village quarters. Transplanted from the original spot's walls, a mural featuring a cherub-studded cloudscape keeps watch over the 17-seat bar. The legendary cocktail programme – delicately balanced elevations of traditional Japanese flavour profiles – also made the move seamlessly. Stalwarts of the institution's early years are represented through classics like Smoke Gets In Your Eyes (bourbon, aromatised wine, herbal liqueur, bitters, smoke), while newer creations, such as the Dirty Dancing (gin, shiso-infused shochu, house-made pickle juice, Chinese onion essence), have earned a fanbase of their own. The drinks may be complex, but the atmosphere is refined and minimalist, with just enough hardwood features to represent NYC. Likewise, the smartly clad staff practice an elegant service, seemingly conjuring libations – as well as some izakaya standard snacks – from the ether. The new Angel's Share delights in the ethos that propelled its forebearer to fame: the graceful pursuit of excellence.
verdict: In 1993, Tony Yoshida opened Angel's Share and ushered in a new era for New York. The bar introduced a generation to then-forgotten craft cocktails, as well as to Japan's rich drinks heritage. In 2022, the upstairs lounge was forced out of its iconic downtown location; two years later, Tony's daughter Erina Yoshida reopened Angel's Share in new West Village quarters. Transplanted from the original spot's walls, a mural featuring a cherub-studded cloudscape keeps watch over the 17-seat bar. The legendary cocktail programme – delicately balanced elevations of traditional Japanese flavour profiles – also made the move seamlessly. Stalwarts of the institution's early years are represented through classics like Smoke Gets In Your Eyes (bourbon, aromatised wine, herbal liqueur, bitters, smoke), while newer creations, such as the Dirty Dancing (gin, shiso-infused shochu, house-made pickle juice, Chinese onion essence), have earned a fanbase of their own. The drinks may be complex, but the atmosphere is refined and minimalist, with just enough hardwood features to represent NYC. Likewise, the smartly clad staff practice an elegant service, seemingly conjuring libations – as well as some izakaya standard snacks – from the ether. The new Angel's Share delights in the ethos that propelled its forebearer to fame: the graceful pursuit of excellence.

name: AnnaLena
address: 1809 W 1st Ave. Vancouver, BC V6J 4M6, Canada
history:
Substance and style: A Kitsilano stalwart since 2015, AnnaLena's interiors may have had a dusky makeover over the past year or so, but you can still depend on it to deliver the same contemporary Canadian cooking and special-occasion spirit as ever. Headed by owner and executive chef Mike Robbins (and named after his two grandmothers), the informal fine-dining spot oozes warmth, whimsy and a sense of comforting nostalgia, from the narrative-driven plates it serves to the Lego Star Wars figurines strewn about the shelves.

Precision? Yes. Pretence? No: Known for combining creative concepts with familiar flavours, the restaurant's multi-course tasting menu evolves with the seasons to highlight local ingredients at their peak, while drawing on global techniques to ensure each dish is presented with precision and artistry. However, AnnaLena doesn't take itself too seriously; each imaginative course is listed on the bill of fare in bright, handwritten crayon, a playful detail that reflects Robbins' personality and his intent to create an experience that lingers long in the memory.

What can I expect? Previous tasting menus have included the likes of Cheeseburger 2050, a deconstructed starter of elk tartare, tallow-caramelised onion and Red Storm and mimolette cheeses; Off the Coast, a seafood dish comprising a juicy Argentinian prawn, Salt Spring mussels, cabbage and a smoked lobster bisque; and Far From the Tree, a dessert combining a roast apple financier with sunchoke and miso apple caramel.

Genuine hospitality: With attentive, knowledgeable staff on hand to explain each course and the story behind it, the service is as much part of the dining experience at AnnaLena as the food is. The aim is to make diners feel engaged rather than intimidated – a philosophy that clearly resonates in the consistently positive reviews the restaurant receives.
verdict: Accessible and personable fine dining

name: Aramburu
address: Pasaje del Correo, Vicente López 1661, C1103ACY Cdad. Autónoma de Buenos Aires, Argentina
history:
About the chef: After training around the world with the likes of Daniel Boulud, Charlie Trotter and Martín Berasategui, Gonzalo Aramburu returned to Argentina in 2007 and opened his eponymous restaurant. With a creative vision anchored in research and technique, he designs an artistic 16-course tasting menu with seasonal products, reimagining classic Argentine cuisine. Aramburu is also the only two-Michelin-starred restaurant in the country.

The dining experience: A regular on Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants list since its inception, in 2019 Aramburu relocated from the edgy Constitución neighbourhood to upscale Recoleta. Located at the end of a charming cobbled passage, diners can enjoy the flawless culinary execution that takes place behind floor-to-ceiling windows. In 2020, the restaurant expanded to the first floor, offering a change of pace for dessert. There's also a private dining room for 14.

What's on the menu: Owing to the hyper-seasonal philosophy to which Aramburu adheres, you'll find the likes of prawns and blood orange, or passion fruit and pork – mix and match when appropriate. One signature snack is Patagonian spider crab and squash, assembled to resemble the crustacean's leg with uncanny accuracy.

What's in store? The aesthetically exciting menu often puts seafood first, making full use of the Atlantic waters to source sea snail, crab, scallop, clams and langoustines. If you opt for the wine pairings, rest assured that labels come from across Argentina.
verdict: Impeccable creativity behind a seasonal menu

name: Arami
address: Av. Aviador 77, La Paz, Bolivia
history:
Dream team: Arami – meaning 'little sky' in Guaraní – was opened in November 2024 by chef Marsia Taha Mohamed and sociologist and sommelier Andrea Moscoso Weise. Taha Mohamed trained in Bolivia and Spain and worked in renowned restaurants in Europe before landing at Gustu in La Paz in 2013, where she spent ten years as head chef before moving into an executive chef role. In 2024, she was named Latin America's Best Female Chef as part of Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants 2024. Meanwhile, Moscoso Weise has specialised in identity, gastronomy and restaurant development. As a sommelier, she celebrates the unique heritage of Bolivian wine.

Making connections: The menu at Arami explores the connection between the Amazon River and the Andes, considering both how these landscapes and ecosystems intersect and the cultural exchange that takes place between these regions. The resulting dishes – such as a fig tart with sacha inchi (mountain peanut) and goat's cheese or a dish of coconut, tucupi (a sauce made from manioc root) and edible flowers – are thoughtful, creative, and almost too pretty to eat.

Set the scene: Located in the Achumani neighborhood of La Paz, Arami keeps things casual. Largely decorated in pale wood and grey, the décor is minimal, allowing those perfectly composed dishes to steal the show.

Doing good through food: Mohamed is more than a chef. She is also the co-founder and driving force behind Sabores Silvestres, a research and educational initiative that promotes the conservation of biodiversity, the preservation of food heritage and acts as a showcase for Bolivian gastronomic culture. By visiting and working with more than 50 Indigenous communities in the Bolivian Amazonia since 2018, the group helps Bolivian consumers and fellow chefs to delve, explore and integrate native ingredients into dishes.
verdict: A female-led culinary exploration of the Andes and the Amazon
chefName: Marsia Taha Mohamed and sociologist and sommelier Andrea Moscoso Weise. Taha Mohamed trained in Bolivia and Spain and wo

name: Arca
address: Carr. Tulum-Boca Paila km 7.6, Tulum Beach, 77780 Tulum, Q.R., Mexico
history:
Jose Luis Hinostroza Buenrostro

Welcome to the jungle: Arca elevates the culinary experience typically found in touristic beaches all over Mexico by bringing sophistication to popular dishes and creating a sustainable spot where nature rules.

What's the setting? An elevated and contemporary take on a palapa-style dining room built on sand, the dining area is elegant yet simple. Whether sitting at a table or at the minimalist bar in the back, you'll be surrounded by palm trees and colourful plants. Arca makes you feel like you are in the middle of nowhere.

Fire, street food and nostalgia: Chef José Luis Hinostroza Buenrostro, hailing from Tijuana in Baja California, reinvents Mexican street food dishes with local ingredients and modern techniques. He brings the explosion of flavours of his hometown's street food scene to the Caribbean jungle, creating unique recipes with sophisticated Mexican taste. Alongside the nostalgia and inventiveness, fire is an important element in the kitchen. A perfect example is the roasted bone marrow, with tamarind and pasilla glaze. Other standouts include the Mexican Wagyu tlayuda (grilled flank steak with oxtail carnitas, smoked yuca purée and huitlacoche mole) and the strped seabass, which is seared in coconut oil and served with a green mole, local basil and coconut and jicama slaw.

Lauded cocktails: Arca doesn't skimp on its cocktail offering: there's a good reason why it was ranked No.27 in North America's 50 Best Bars 2025. It works in symbiosis with the kitchen, crafting cocktails with all-natural ingredients and a lot of personality, alongside a wide selection of Mexican spirits.
verdict: Jungle-based culinary wizardry with cocktails to match

name: Argo
address: 8 Finance St, Central, Hong Kong
history: Led by the unstoppable Four Seasons beverage manager Federico Balzarini, this groundbreaking bar is named after Argo – the ship that sailed Jason and the Argonauts to the Golden Fleece in Greek mythology. Argo provides a taste of the future of drinking – and poses the question: 'What will we be sipping on when ingredients run out or demand for spirits can't be met?' Argo's menu uses Asian-sourced ingredients which risk becoming a luxury due to scarcity caused by climate. Its latest menu, titled Combinations, is split into categories of one, two, three or four core ingredients, ranging from cacao to kumquat. From its single-element drinks, expect highballs of pineapple honey, jasmine-infused pineapple, and pineapple tomato shrub. Alternatively, the four-element Dr. J Negroni supercharges the classic negroni with pineapple, bergamot, cacao and grapes. In 2025, the bar celebrates its fourth birthday, with no signs of slowing down any time soon.
verdict: Led by the unstoppable Four Seasons beverage manager Federico Balzarini, this groundbreaking bar is named after Argo – the ship that sailed Jason and the Argonauts to the Golden Fleece in Greek mythology. Argo provides a taste of the future of drinking – and poses the question: 'What will we be sipping on when ingredients run out or demand for spirits can't be met?' Argo's menu uses Asian-sourced ingredients which risk becoming a luxury due to scarcity caused by climate. Its latest menu, titled Combinations, is split into categories of one, two, three or four core ingredients, ranging from cacao to kumquat. From its single-element drinks, expect highballs of pineapple honey, jasmine-infused pineapple, and pineapple tomato shrub. Alternatively, the four-element Dr. J Negroni supercharges the classic negroni with pineapple, bergamot, cacao and grapes. In 2025, the bar celebrates its fourth birthday, with no signs of slowing down any time soon.

name: Arpège
address: 84 Rue de Varenne, 75007 Paris, France
history:
Expect the unexpected: Though most restaurants of its ilk lean into all that is predictable and precise, chef Alain Passard and the team at Arpège conspire to create an experience that is anything but. Ruled by seasonality, and taking advantage of ingredients at their best, this is an off-the-cuff approach to fine dining, that sometimes even deviates from the menu. The result is a distinct, immersive and engaging experience with every visit.

Elevating veg: Famously removing meat from the menu before it was popular and fondly referred to as the 'maestro' of vegetarian cuisine, Passard caused a small revolution while making non-meat eaters very happy. While some protein has since crept back into some dishes since his 2001 announcement, the stars of the menu are undoubtedly vegetables and plant-based ingredients.

What’s on the plate?: Think remarkable French ingredients delivered with layered delicacy: cauliflower of three colours paired with shallots and herb sauce; white asparagus, named The Belle of the Sarthe Valley, topped with slices of kiwi and radish; and a deceptively simple yet memorable crème brûlée. The restaurant’s mostly animal-free dishes also lend themselves to adaptations to accommodate dietary requirements, which still isn’t as common as it should be in the realm of fine dining.

Home grown: The fresh produce that forms the backbone of the dishes served at Arpège is harvested daily and delivered from Passard’s three biodynamic and organic farms and kitchen gardens just outside of Paris. Not only do they supply the restaurant and cut down on food miles, they also support a rich ecosystem of local wildlife. Nothing is refrigerated for long periods of time. The produce is picked by a team of gardeners and served or sold that day in order to preserve the true integrity of each ingredient.
verdict: A bastion of Parisian fine dining where vegetables reign supreme
chefName: Alain Passard and the team at Arpège conspire to create an experience that is anything but. Ruled by seasonality

name: Asador Etxebarri
address: San Juan Plaza, 1, 48291 Axpe, Bizkaia, Spain
history:
What’s the deal? Set in a peaceful Basque village surrounded by mountains and lush greenery somewhere between Bilbao and San Sebastian, Asador Etxebarri is the platonic ideal of a destination restaurant. Food enthusiasts travel from all over the world to experience the culinary masterpieces created by chef Bittor Arguinzoniz. With the help of a little fire, he transforms simple ingredients like milk and beef into extraordinary and unforgettable dishes.

Kissed by fire: In a small kitchen filled with custom-made grills designed by the chef, Arguinzoniz prepares a series of elegant dishes where the ingredient is king. There is no molecular gastronomy here, as the chef prefers to let the natural flavours of the high-quality produce take centre stage. Everything on the menu is touched by the grill, from anchovy on toast to the milk ice cream with beetroot that ends the meal. Take for instance, a succulent red Palamós prawn simply grilled and served on its own, or the fresh mozzarella from the chef's buffalo served with a juicy tomato slice.

What should I order? While locals may plump for the a la carte, the tasting menu is the best way to enjoy everything Etxebarri has to offer. Over 14 courses eaten in the spacious dining room overlooking the village square, guests enjoy Arguinzoniz’s homemade chorizo and seasonal delicacies like baby eels and sea cucumber with green beans. The star of the show is a charcoal-charred beef chop – it may look impossibly large, but its impossibly tender texture and extraordinary flavour mean the plate is almost always sent back clean.

Hometown hero: One of the best-loved chefs in the business, Arguinzoniz grew up around the corner from where the restaurant now stands, in a house without electricity and gas where his mother and grandmother would cook on the hearth. He has been passionate about cooking on fire for more than 30 years. Despite gaining recognition, including the Chefs' Choice Award and consistently ranking high in The World's 50 Best Restaurants list, he remains humble. Asador Etxebarri has only been off the list once since 2009 and currently holds the title of The Best Restaurant in Europe 2025.
verdict: The Basque master of the barbecue
chefName: Bittor Arguinzoniz. With the help of a little fire

name: Aska
address: 47 S. 5th St. Brooklyn, NY 11249, United States
history:
The backstory: Stockholm-born chef Fredrik Berselius stumbled into cooking in London before moving to New York, where he trained at some of the city's top kitchens, including Per Se. In 2012, he launched Aska (Swedish for 'ashes') in a modest Williamsburg spot. Within a year, it earned a Michelin star. By 2016, Berselius relocated Aska to a restored 1860s warehouse under the Williamsburg Bridge. The dimly-lit space – with its main dining room and seasonal garden patio – earned a second Michelin star shortly after.

What's on the plate: Expect a procession of 12-14 dishes, each expressing the rhythms of Northeastern US seasonality and the purity of Scandinavian terroir. Signature dishes include sea oak with blue mussel emulsion, grilled langoustine with red gooseberry and live scallop with white turnip and blackcurrant leaf.

To drink: Aska doesn't have a sit-down bar, however guests can order classic cocktails on demand, or choose from pours of 70-year-old Cognac or a nip of Japanese whisky. The restaurant's French-forward wine list spans from grand cru Burgundy and grower champagne to cult California pinot noir and artisanal sake, highlighting producers which embrace exceptional craftsmanship.

The vibe: Set in an intimate, candlelit space of exposed brick and wooden tables draped in black tablecloths, Aska's naturalist aesthetic frames the rear open kitchen, which glows as the creative heart of the dining room.
verdict: Scandi-style cooking meets Northeast US seasonality
chefName: Fredrik Berselius stumbled into cooking in London before moving to New York

name: Atelier Crenn
address: 3127 Fillmore St. San Francisco, CA 94123, United States
history:
Top chef: There's no doubt France-born Dominique Crenn, who helms Atelier Crenn, is at the top of her game. She was the first female chef in the US to be awarded a Michelin star for Atelier Crenn in 2011, then subsequently the first US-based female chef to achieve three Michelin stars at the same restaurant in 2018. In between that, she was named The World's Best Female Chef in 2016, then crowned the winner of The World's 50 Best Restaurants Icon Award in 2021. While also finding time to be an activist, champion of women's rights and sustainability trailblazer, it's safe to say Crenn is a force majeure.

What's in a name? 'Atelier' – meaning an artist's workshop or studio – is an apt way to describe Crenn's self-titled restaurant. Based on the concept of 'Poetic Culinaria', an eight-table space adorned with family photos and her father's artwork envelops guests as they experience an artistic menu, presented in the form of a poem written by Crenn before ample tableside theatrics begin.

Making memories: The 17-course pescatarian menu is designed to represent memories from Crenn's summers spent in Brittany, or to spark new memorable moments for diners. One prime example is Crenn's signature dish, Kir Breton. A surprising play on the traditional Kir aperitif of Crenn's beloved Brittany, it is made with a white chocolate shell topped with creme de cassis, which bursts upon eating to reveal an apple cider filling.

Ethical sourcing and sustainability: Crenn has always prioritised ethical sourcing – the restaurant works with two small dairies to supply butter and cream. The table butter from Animal Farm Creamery is produced by one cow, called Timothy, and San Martin Milk Company supplies butter for the famous brioche. Atelier Crenn is also certified plastic free, with no single use plastics on site – it's the first restaurant in the US to achieve the certification. In 2026, Atelier Crenn was recognised with the Sustainable Restaurant Award, as part of North America's 50 Best Restaurants.
verdict: Poetic French-Californian cuisine with impeccable sourcing
chefName: in the US to be awarded a Michelin star for Atelier Crenn in

name: Atelier Moessmer Norbert Niederkofler
address: Via Walther von der Vogelweide, Brunico, 39031
history:
Scene setting: The small town of Brunico sits in a lush valley beneath the meeting point of the Alps and the Dolomites. In 2023, local chef Norbert Niederkofler relocated from his previous St Hubertus hotel-restaurant in a neighbouring valley to new premises in a 19th century villa, formerly part of the Moessmer textile production complex. The space has been redesigned with an emphasis on discreet luxury, including the addition of a modernist glass-walled extension housing the open kitchen and counter seating area.

Chef as nurturer: Niederkofler has forged an enviable reputation over many decades, both within and beyond the kitchen. While his benign presence permeates Atelier Moessmer, executive chef Mauro Siega and sommelier-manager Lukas Gerges lead a talented and youthful team in daily operations. At the same time, Niederkofler’s established ‘cook the mountain’ philosophy – which permits only the use of hyper-local Tyrolean terroir ingredients – drives every aspect of the restaurant’s sustainability-forward culinary narrative.

The dining experience: Guests first ring a doorbell before being invited into the villa, emphasising the private family feel. Snacks are served in the bar-lounge area, before different courses are presented in a variety of rooms, on the garden terrace and at the kitchen counter. The charming team judge how much detail to deliver on the dishes and wines. The simply named dishes – Spring Salad, Tyrolean Beef, Mountain Cheesecake – belie serious complexity, cutting-edge techniques and, most importantly, fascinating flavour combinations. Long-time signatures include singular versions of beetroot gnocchi and tarte tatin that can be added to the super-seasonal 12-course tasting menu.

Above and beyond: Niederkofler oversees numerous projects outside the walls of Atelier Moessmer, including AlpiNN by Norbert Niederkofler – a stunning three-in-one restaurant (lounge-bar area; a la carte dining room; tasting menu space) on the slopes of Plan de Corones ski area, perched at an altitude of 2,235m. Furthermore, he founded Care, an annual event centred around ethical cooking, helped curate a degree course in gastronomy at the University of Bolzan, heads an academy scheme to train young, mostly female chefs in Saudi Arabia and is campaigning for the reform of school meals in Italy.
verdict: Nature-focused Tyrolean chef bringing his ‘cook the mountain’ philosophy to exquisite life

name: Atomix
address: 104 E. 30th St. New York, NY 10016, USA
history:
K-wave: If food is the ultimate window into culture, and Korean culture is increasingly prevalent and valued internationally, then Atomix in New York provides the ultimate gastronomic manifestation of the K-wave phenomenon. This is Korean dining at its very finest: sophisticated and flavoursome food, with dishes grounded in heritage, but distinct and innovative.

Unlikely location: Arrive outside Atomix and you might be forgiven for thinking you were in the wrong spot – until other diners gather on the sidewalk alongside you. The unprepossessing exterior in the still rough-round-the-edges NoMad district of Manhattan belies a beautiful, if minimal, restaurant within. The ground floor houses a bar serving exceptional cocktails, with the main 14-seat U-shaped counter in the basement.

On the menu: Diners can expect a well-paced 12-course tasting menu of Korean-based plates, featuring world-class flavour and texture combinations. Each dish is served in beautiful bespoke ceramics accompanied by an explanatory card providing accessible information on the ingredients, origin and inspiration. Dishes range from lamb with deodeok [a Korean root vegetable] to cherry blossom trout with Korean mustard and rhubarb; sea cucumber might be served with shrimp and egg over rice, while just-grilled wagyu is contrasted with cold noodles.

Couple goals: Atomix is the flagship restaurant of Ellia Park, who runs the business, and her husband, chef Junghyun ‘JP’ Park. The Korean-born duo also oversee three further New York dining spots – the more casual Atoboy (which predates Atomix), Naro (focusing on more traditional dishes) and the drinks-led Seoul Salon – making them arguably the city’s ultimate gastronomic power couple. Yet the Parks have retained the hospitality, humility and sincerity, combined with immense skill, that has been the bedrock of their business and creative success.

Major impact: Atomix is providing a fresh and more international perspective on Korean cuisine, outside the boundaries of hansik (traditional Korean food) or New Korean gastronomy.
verdict: Elevated Korean counter dining in Manhattan

name: Attaboy
address: 134 Eldridge St, New York, NY 10002, United States
history: Attaboy continues to stake its place in New York cocktail history. Housed in the former space of Milk & Honey, its unmarked metal door is a trademark of this iconic, cavernous and low-lit speakeasy owned by Sam Ross and Michael McIlroy. Enter for a bespoke experience, with no menus being a feature of the bar. Simply describe your drink preferences, perhaps answer a few questions, and the bartenders will create a cocktail tailored to your liking. Or, order one of its signatures, as it's the birthplace of famous classics such as the Paper Plane, Penicillin and Greenpoint. The space has fortunately expanded beyond its notoriously trim capacity of 25, having doubled in size in December of 2025 and now featuring two bar tops instead of one. This allows for limited reservations, pop-ups, private events and cocktail classes – all while keeping the original feel of the bar intact, thanks to the second bar top space being a mirror of the first. Combined with the history of Milk & Honey, this cocktail space is entering its 25th year, making it a bonafide setting of the American craft cocktail renaissance.
verdict: Re-Entry

name: Au Jardin
address: The Warehouse @ Hin Bus Depot 125, Jalan Timah, 10150, Georgetown, Penang, Malaysia
history:
A portal to another world: A hidden oasis contained within a warehouse at a former bus depot, Au Jardin is French for 'in the garden' – and, once you've transcended the corrugated metal panels of its utilitarian exterior, you'll immediately notice why. Swathed in greenery, with plants rising up from pots around its perimeter, the restaurant's courtyard-like atrium hints at the earth-loving nature of chef Su Kim Hock's fine-dining menu. Giving this secret sanctuary even more of an exclusive air, the refined dining room beyond seats just 18 guests.

What's the vibe? This may be a garden, but that doesn't mean you shouldn't dress to impress. A far cry from George Town's famously no-frills street-food stalls, Au Jardin is all refinement, from its minimalist design to its classic white tablecloths. The softly lit upstairs bar is a sophisticated spot to sip on a cocktail or Scotch whisky before you descend for the main event.

And the philosophy? Chef Kim Hock is a champion of innovative, sustainable gastronomy, blending traditional Malaysian ingredients with French culinary flair to produce dishes that sing with flavour and texture. His multi-course tasting menus change monthly, flowing with the seasons to capture produce at its very finest – and often, locally sourced vegetables are the focus, backed by a supporting cast of proteins from land and sea.

Give us the highlights: Crispy on the outside, meltingly moist in the middle, the cognac and hay-aged duck is widely regarded as one of Au Jardin's standout dishes, as is the river prawn: a seafood course noted for its plump, juicy texture. The lacto-fermented bread with tomato chutney butter – a mouthwatering medley of salt, tang and spice – is also the subject of much praise from diners.
verdict: Capturing the beauty and tranquillity of nature on a plate
chefName: Su Kim Hock's fine-dining menu. Giving this secret sanctuary even more of an exclusive air

name: August
address: Sequis Tower - Ground Floor #03-02, Jl. Jenderal Sudirman No.Kav. 71, Jakarta, Jakarta 12190
history:
Hans Christian and Budi Cahyadi

Rising star: Opened in late 2021, August was the winner of the American Express One To Watch Award 2023 and made its debut on the Asia's 50 Best Restaurants list in 2024. Chef Hans Christian and seasoned hotelier and F&B professional Budi Cahyadi joined forces to create this labour of love that offers local Indonesian flavours showcasing the chef's culinary journey, which includes references to his childhood memories and snacks. In 2026, August is named The Best Restaurant in Indonesia for the third consecutive year.

Why August? Christian and Cahyadi, who share the same culinary and hospitality vision, named August after the month they in which they were both born.

What's the vibe? The 50-seat dining destination, located in the teeming metropolis of Jakarta, is warm and chic and, despite its size, still feels cosy. With a large kitchen dominating the restaurant, diners can enjoy their meal with a full view of the action. For solo diners or couples who want a more involved experience, the Chef's Counter offers guests front-row seats overlooking August's pastry section, which is overseen by Ardika Dwitama, winner of the Asia's Best Pastry Chef Award 2026, sponsored by Valrhona. The excellent bar also serves an array of non-alcoholic drinks to pair with the menu, including a delicious fermented black tea stout.

On the menu: Enjoy Indonesian dishes reinvented by Chef Christian using French techniques. A delicate mini milk-bread using spent grain with seaweed butter kicks off proceedings. Other courses include grilled octopus with a three-layered laksa sauce and topped with Thai basil, or kalamansi-marinated tomato and cubes of cured ruby snapper on a bed of black tea jelly and cultured cream. Other fan favourites, such as his interpretation of the popular peanut butter sandwich, renamed the Foie PB&J, are available as add-ons for the tasting menu.
verdict: Bold, contemporary Indonesian flavours paired with timeless technique
chefName: Hans Christian and seasoned hotelier and F

name: Avize
address: 956 Brady Ave. NW Atlanta, GA 30318
history:
Bringing the Alps to Atlanta: The name offers something of a hint as to what this much-lauded restaurant is all about; 'aviser' is a French word that translates poetically as 'to take notice'. However, it's not just French cuisine that's prepared with great attention to detail here – an eclectic array of Alpine-inspired Italian, Swiss, Austrian and German dishes are also executed with precision and flair, overseen by chef Karl Gorline.

What's cooking? The menu is a showcase of hyper-seasonal, farm-to-table cooking, each dish the product of European imagination, meticulous technique and Southern ingredients. Though the menu changes regularly to capture produce at its best, the lemon-pepper wet frog legs (inspired by Atlanta legend J R Crickets' signature wings) are a standout starter, and mains often include the likes of fermented carrot bolognese, wagyu strip steak with a pastrami-spiced beef tongue jus, and polenta and ricotta–filled agnolotti pasta in a Madeira and mushroom broth.

And the drinks? Avize's sumptuous beverage offering is curated by co-founder Taurean Philpott, a doyen of hospitality with years of experience in the fine-dining industry. Every pour is designed to flatter the food, from Old World wines and champagne to creative cocktails such as the Old Man of the Woods (rye whiskey, bay laurel gomme syrup, maple and pine) and the Easy Rider (gin, melon, vanilla, clarified milk and juniper).

Mountain motifs: Elegant yet approachable, the space is a little like an ultra-chic Alpine lodge – a mural depicting stately fir trees and towering peaks spans banquette seating, and a taxidermy mountain goat is a candescent focal point amid the wood and dim lighting. The dead giveaway that this is still North America? The soundtrack of indie electronic and hip-hop classics playing softly in the background.
verdict: Alpine cuisine with a Southern twist

name: Baba au Rum
address: Klitiou 6, 105 60 Athens, Greece
history: There's an undeniable charm to Baba au Rum, a bar that, after more than 15 years, has become an Athenian stalwart. Billing itself as a "rum and cocktail society," the bar, under the guidance of owner Thanos Prunarus, has a clear, unpretentious philosophy: come for the exceptional drinks and stay for the warm company. For Prunarus, it is more than just a place to grab a drink; it's a cultural hub where people connect and exchange ideas. This ethos has earned it a devoted following and a consistent presence on the global stage, marking its 11th consecutive year on the list of The World's 50 Best Bars – a testament to its enduring quality. Baba au Rum's reverence for sugarcane spirits is obvious – it's all in the name – with a back bar boasting more than 400 rums. The bar honours the Tiki tradition, and classic rum cocktails are springboards for innovation. For example, the Supremus n°58 is an inventive twist on the Ti Punch, featuring a rum blend, falernum, tea and summer fruits. The team also leverages its expertise with exotic ingredients to create wonderful cocktails with other spirits. The Beatnik Paloma, its unique take on one of the world's most popular long drinks, is made vibrant by a mix of tequila, mezcal, beetroot and black cardamom, and is a perfect example of the bar's creative range. The bar's modernist-inspired design and carefully curated music create a sophisticated yet welcoming atmosphere, where high-concept ideas are made accessible through genuine, joyful hospitality.
verdict: An Athenian institution with exceptional drinks and joyful hospitality

name: Baltra Bar
address: Iztaccihuatl 36D, Colonia Condesa, Cuauhtémoc, Mexico City, 06100
history: On any given Tuesday night, you can find much of Mexico City's craft cocktail community gathered at Baltra Bar for martinis. The bartenders' seal of approval is warranted: Baltra's space creates the feel of a neighbourhood café coupled with a cosmopolitan drinks menu. Beverage director José Luis León is also responsible for the celebrated and experimental Licorería Limantour in Roma Norte, but here the drinks maestro employs a softer touch with the menus. León's lists are as elegant as they are playful; last year, a menu titled Beber es un Viaje (Drinking is a Journey) was themed around the various frustrations of modern air travel, from delayed flights (reinterpreted as the perfect opportunity to squeeze in one more drink – namely the Plan Z, featuring gin, cassis, chai, fig and mascarpone) to lost luggage (the pain of which can be quickly eased by the anaesthetic effects of Modo Avión, a concoction of tequila, vermouth, pickles and watermelon sangrita). The nuanced flavours offset a lively atmosphere with friends gathered in banquettes around low tables or in the covered bar at the front of the building. With the low lighting and constant crowd of visitors, you'd be forgiven for missing the Charles Darwin memorabilia decorating the space. Baltra was the second Galapagos island visited by the venerable biologist. In the spirit of his work and travels, this chic Condessa bar provides both an evolution in mixology and a safe harbour to celebrate in.
verdict: On any given Tuesday night, you can find much of Mexico City's craft cocktail community gathered at Baltra Bar for martinis. The bartenders' seal of approval is warranted: Baltra's space creates the feel of a neighbourhood café coupled with a cosmopolitan drinks menu. Beverage director José Luis León is also responsible for the celebrated and experimental Licorería Limantour in Roma Norte, but here the drinks maestro employs a softer touch with the menus. León's lists are as elegant as they are playful; last year, a menu titled Beber es un Viaje (Drinking is a Journey) was themed around the various frustrations of modern air travel, from delayed flights (reinterpreted as the perfect opportunity to squeeze in one more drink – namely the Plan Z, featuring gin, cassis, chai, fig and mascarpone) to lost luggage (the pain of which can be quickly eased by the anaesthetic effects of Modo Avión, a concoction of tequila, vermouth, pickles and watermelon sangrita). The nuanced flavours offset a lively atmosphere with friends gathered in banquettes around low tables or in the covered bar at the front of the building. With the low lighting and constant crowd of visitors, you'd be forgiven for missing the Charles Darwin memorabilia decorating the space. Baltra was the second Galapagos island visited by the venerable biologist. In the spirit of his work and travels, this chic Condessa bar provides both an evolution in mixology and a safe harbour to celebrate in.

name: Bandista
address: 1300 Lamar St, Houston, TX 77010, United States
history: Scientific laboratory tech meets classic southern hospitality at Houston's hot spot Bandista. Deep within the downtown Four Seasons Hotel, the chic lounge is hidden behind a nondescript bookcase. Inside, a mix of local cocktailians and visiting VIPs experience a unique take on the classic speakeasy. Yes, touches of Art Deco invoke the expected 1920's vibes and the exclusive space oozes Gatsby. However, the focus here is on tequileros and Prohibition's oft overlooked smuggling routes along the US-Mexican border. Despite the historical influences and a bevy of vintage glasses, the drinks are undeniably contemporary. Beverage director Johnathon Jones leans into advanced lab techniques to conjure creations like made-to-order 'dipping dots' that accompany the Menage a Trois (cognac, creme de cacao, coffee liqueur, local cream, cacao butter). True to concept, the list also provides a host of agave forward options like the Almost Famous (mezcal, roasted pineapple, Mexican tea-infused génépy, Japanese amaro); the final ingredient, paparazzi, manifests as a bartender wielding a Polaroid camera. Bandista's enthusiastic team carefully balance a serious programme with such lighter fun touches. Whether guests are perusing their extensive spirits list or learning about vacuum distillation, the Bandista experience is grounded in deep knowledge, but ultimately offers an expression of joy.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bar Benfiddich
address: 9F, 1 Chome-13-7, Nishishinjuku, Shinjuku City, 160-0023 Tokyo, Japan
history: No two visits to Bar Benfiddich are ever the same. Since your last, Hiroyasu Kayama might have tasted an indigenous drink in some new, remote country, unearthed a new recipe from a dusty book, or planted something on his farm that's just ready to harvest. Most drinks contain something of that farm in Chichibu, an hour north west of Tokyo. Twigs snapped fresh from mizunara or juniper trees might be used to stir a drink; fennel, grown alongside intensely aromatic mint, finds its way into a farm-fresh Julep, sipped through a century-old pewter straw. Kayama might take a splash of honey water from one of the absinthe drippers hanging above the bar, or he might reach for one of his many bottles whose labels fell off decades ago. Guests sit at a walnut counter facing those bottles – some of which are global classics, others his own work, including a farm-distilled absinthe – as well as fresh fruit and herbs, inventive infusions and a telling painting of an illicit Highland distilling operation. The room has just 17 seats, the atmosphere a mix of quiet focus and curiosity. Guests lean in to watch Kayama work, and often ask questions – about the plant in their drink, the antique tool in his hand, or the old book open on the bar. Bar Benfiddich follows its own style – one that weaves a path between nature, history and your palate.
verdict: Inventive farm-driven cocktails in an ever-changing Shinjuku hideaway

name: Bar Leone
address: 15 Bridges St, Central, Hong Kong
history: Opened in June 2023 by multi award-winning Italian bartender Lorenzo Antinori, Bar Leone's meteoric rise to success can be attributed to two things: a great bar programme and an even greater sense of humour. Rome-born Antinori comes from serious pedigree, having worked at the finest bars in London, Seoul and Hong Kong. Passionate about cocktail history, Italy and football, all roads eventually led Lorenzo to open Bar Leone – a bar designed to pay tribute to cocktail popolari, Italian pop-culture and the art of classic bartending. Bar Leone channels the soul of Rome's traditional neighbourhood bars – unpretentious, convivial spots that anchor the city's 21 districts, and Trastevere in particular. Its namesake, Leone the lion was once the emblem of Rome before the Renaissance and remains the symbol of Trastevere to this day. The bar's beverage programme follows a low-intervention approach, reviving classic cocktails with a focus on craftsmanship, quality ingredients and seasonality, avoiding re-distillation and any complex equipment. The bar takes a deliberate step away from current trends, embracing simplicity. This pared-back approach celebrates the classic art of bartending and allows guests to truly understand and appreciate what goes into each drink. Lorenzo is also determined to revive the bellini, served in a small tumbler just as at the iconic Harry's Bar in Venice, where it originated. Featuring almond eau de vie and peach, it is topped with a fluffy cream, in stark contrast with the fruit-forward base. At Bar Leone, while the cocktails are what draw guests in, it's the warm service, the welcoming neighbourhood atmosphere and the near-viral mortadella sandwiches that keep them coming back.
verdict: A tribute to timeless Italian drinking culture in the heart of Hong Kong

name: Bar Libre
address: 3 Chome−25−8 相馬屋ビル B1F, Nishiikebukuro, Toshima City, Tokyo, Japan
history: Look for the Vietnamese grocery store sign – you can't miss it. But Bar Libre, which is in the same building? Their sign is all but invisible. However, it's no speakeasy schtick – Bar Libre simply doesn't need to shout. Open for 14 years, it's a fixture of Tokyo's cocktail scene – unassuming from the street, but beloved by industry insiders and devoted regulars alike – drawing drinkers to the unpretentious Ikebukuro neighbourhood, far from the established tourist trail. Found the building? Head to the basement and enter a 17-seat room where the bartending is laid-back but polished. The soundtrack is mellow jazz, the colour tone sepia, the atmosphere effortlessly convivial. Owner Yujiro Kiyosaki and manager Kazuaki Nagao bring real warmth, quick wit, and serious skill to proceedings. Libre has been championing modern, creative cocktails, setting the tone for technique-driven drinks well before it was fashionable in the Japanese capital. The cocktails often incorporate Japanese ingredients. A toasty twist on the Screwdriver employs soba tea; the Fake White mimics a white wine using gin, tomato water, Earl Grey, citruses and a yoghurt wash. There are thoughtful riffs on the classics, a rotating cast of seasonal fruit cocktails, and a standout selection of vintage Japanese malts. The presentation skews playful, think wilting wine glasses, artisanal ceramics, and even flower vases even, but it never distracts from the drink. Libre may be a New Entry to Asia's 50 Best Bars 2025 list, but in Tokyo, it's been hiding in plain sight all along.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bar Madonna
address: 367 Metropolitan Ave, Brooklyn, NY 11211, United States
history: Contrary to what you might expect, Bar Madonna does not take its name from religion or the pop music icon. Instead, this spot in trendy Williamsburg is the self-titled brainchild of seasoned New York bartender Eric Madonna. Together with former Carbone manager Ray Rando, they debuted Bar Madonna in April 2024 with the aim of opening a new chapter in modern Italian-American hospitality. The interiors of Bar Madonna feel slick and modern, with the centrepiece painting from artist KidSuper proudly perched above the long bar counter. But nods to the classic Italian-American neighbourhood hangouts of old are woven into the aesthetic through the prevalence of stained wood, dark green banquette seating, exposed bricks and candlelight. Its cocktails also aim to bridge the historic and contemporary. Take the Nonna's Half & Half, for example, a martini-adjacent concoction of both vodka and gin. In the Limoncello Milk Punch, the traditional digestif gets a makeover as a delectable, clarified creation with the addition of gin, tequila, genmaicha and shiso. Food from chef Chris Caliso trades simple bar bites for a line-up of the greatest hits of heart-warming Italian-American cuisine. Think smashed meatball parms with oozy provolone cheese, parmigiano crisp and garlic fries, or grilled calabrese wings dressed in smoked Calabrian hot sauce and buttermilk vinaigrette. Make sure to arrive both thirsty and hungry.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bar Mauro
address: Tabasco 149, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México, CDMX, Mexico
history: Opening in late 2024, Bar Mauro might still be a relatively new addition to the beating heart of Mexico City's hip La Roma neighbourhood, but it's already made waves – the biggest of which saw it named the Campari One To Watch Award winner at The World's 50 Best Bars 2025. Wander inside this bijou space to discover a slice of nostalgic 1970s Italy, replete with mirrors, subway tiles and sleek, modernist touches that remind the bar's vogueish guests of Milan's status as fashion capital. The bar itself is named after Mauro Mendoza and crafted around his love for an afternoon aperitif. Today, his grandsons Ricardo and Eduardo Nava honour his legacy with their Milanese oasis. Black and white photos of Mauro in stylish, tailored suits keep watch over the space. For good measure, the surrounding shelves display dozens of vermouths and amari from the bar's Italian origins. Things get even more Italianate with the drinks menu: some creations honour the home nation's bountiful standards such as the Negroni 1929 (gin, red vermouth, Campari, cacao nibs, strawberry), while others employ regional favourites to blend originals like the Basilico (tequila, rose vermouth, basil, peach). Likewise, Mauro's kitchen offers all of Italy's iconic accoutrements including an impressive array of charcuterie, cheese and olives. However, locals will cite the humble cheeseburger as a must-try. The culinary dichotomy is simply one example of the lounge's concept: 'where golden age meets modern nostalgia.'
verdict: Opening in late 2024, Bar Mauro might still be a relatively new addition to the beating heart of Mexico City's hip La Roma neighbourhood, but it's already made waves – the biggest of which saw it named the Campari One To Watch Award winner at The World's 50 Best Bars 2025. Wander inside this bijou space to discover a slice of nostalgic 1970s Italy, replete with mirrors, subway tiles and sleek, modernist touches that remind the bar's vogueish guests of Milan's status as fashion capital. The bar itself is named after Mauro Mendoza and crafted around his love for an afternoon aperitif. Today, his grandsons Ricardo and Eduardo Nava honour his legacy with their Milanese oasis. Things get even more Italianate with the drinks menu: some creations honour the home nation's bountiful standards such as the Negroni 1929 (gin, red vermouth, Campari, cacao nibs, strawberry), while others employ regional favourites to blend originals like the Basilico (tequila, rose vermouth, basil, peach). Likewise, Mauro's kitchen offers all of Italy's iconic accoutrements including an impressive array of charcuterie, cheese and olives. However, locals will cite the humble cheeseburger as a must-try. The culinary dichotomy is simply one example of the lounge's concept: 'where golden age meets modern nostalgia.'

name: Bar Nouveau
address: 5 Rue des Haudriettes, 75003 Paris, France
history: Tucked away in Paris's vibrant Marais district, Bar Nouveau was co-founded by twins Sara and Hadrien Moudoulaud, Marc Puzzuoli, and the recipient of the Industry Icon Award in 2021 Rémy Savage. Passionate about the arts, Savage and the team built their bar around the famous Art Nouveau movement. The decor is meticulously crafted to transport guests to a bygone era. The bar is home to the world's largest private collection of antique Bimini glasses, and custom light fixtures echo the movement's embrace of natural forms. The space is split between two distinct levels: the ground floor reimagines an Art Nouveau bar from 130 years ago with a bright, airy feel, while the darker, more intimate downstairs area with its exposed brick brings a futuristic twist, exploring the relationship between nature and the post-technological world. This duality extends to the cocktail programme. The tightly curated menu of six cocktails is a masterclass in elegant simplicity, offering modern interpretations of classic drinks, including lesser-known French bistro classics. The upstairs menu focuses on pre-industrial techniques, while the downstairs menu utilises modern technology to create drinks with a different personality. This unique approach, combined with the bar's stunning aesthetic, has turned Bar Nouveau into an in-demand destination. It's a diminutive space and doesn't take reservations, but the bar opens at 3pm so it's a good idea to arrive early to score one of the city's most coveted seats.
verdict: Art-inspired bar and elegant cocktails by an Industry Icon

name: Bar Pompette
address: 607 College St, Toronto, ON M6G 1B5, Canada
history: Despite the classic French character and neighbourhood bistro feel, the backbone of the drinks programme at Bar Pompette is an enthusiastic and exhaustive commitment to locavorism. Tight partnerships with regional farmers make it possible for the team at this cherished cocktail destination to showcase Ontario terroir in painstakingly perfected cocktails served up with grace and warmth in a cosy, walk-in-only space that's something of a sanctuary in Toronto's busy Little Italy. Given its dedication to seasonal ingredients farmed and foraged in Toronto's greenbelt, many of the cocktails on the menu at Pompette are ephemeral, but you can always count on its five staple signatures. House drinks include the Cornichon, a riff on a martini with a splash of pickle distillate, as well as the bar's greatest hits, such as a scrumptious silky and nutty Nitro Colada as well as a legendary hot serve: the smoky Paloma Quemada. Everything else is pretty much only available while supplies last, so don't put off ordering drinks like the spicy Breakfast at Tommy's, a margarita variant supercharged with birria distillate and bitter orange, finished with coriander dust. It's worth noting that people who prefer a glass of wine are equally spoiled for choice at this charming bar.
verdict: Despite the classic French character and neighbourhood bistro feel, the backbone of the drinks programme at Bar Pompette is an enthusiastic and exhaustive commitment to locavorism. Tight partnerships with regional farmers make it possible for the team at this cherished cocktail destination to showcase Ontario terroir in painstakingly perfected cocktails served up with grace and warmth in a cosy, walk-in-only space that's something of a sanctuary in Toronto's busy Little Italy.
