House drinks include the Cornichon, a riff on a martini with a splash of pickle distillate, as well as the bar's greatest hits, such as a scrumptious silky and nutty Nitro Colada as well as a legendary hot serve: the smoky Paloma Quemada. Everything else is pretty much only available while supplies last, so don't put off ordering drinks like the spicy Breakfast at Tommy's, a margarita variant supercharged with birria distillate and bitter orange, finished with coriander dust. It's worth noting that people who prefer a glass of wine are equally spoiled for choice at this charming bar.

name: Bar Sathorn
address: The House on Sathorn, 106 N Sathon Rd, Si Lom, Khet Bang Rak, Bangkok 10500, Thailand
history: The mansion at 106 North Sathorn Road has, at various points over the past 136 years, served as a tycoon's private residence, a luxury hotel, and a Soviet embassy. Today, it's home to Bar Sathorn – an atmospheric cocktail lounge and part of W Bangkok where heritage architecture meets contemporary cocktail craft. Marco Dognini, a Milan-born bartender whose résumé spans eight countries and four continents, leads a programme that raids the archives, twisting local and historical references into brilliantly unorthodox serves. A mango sticky rice riff on the ramos gin fizz blurs the line between dessert and drink. A reinvention of the bullshot – served hot, with tom kha broth – nods to the mansion's Soviet embassy years. Most famously and virally, the bar's agave-and-bacon Bloody Mary is chilled not with ice, but with a whole frozen tomato. High rollers, meanwhile, can try a Siam Royal, where cognac meets kaffir lime and Thai basil bitters. Dognini embraces his adopted home further through a monthly specials menu that spotlights local artisans. Past themes have included Thai cacao, wild honey, pomelo and hilltribe coffee. Many of the bar's herbs and greens come from W Bangkok's rooftop garden, where the hotel's food waste is composted into nutrient-rich soil to nourish kale, Thai basil, and edible flowers among many other ingredients. Furnished with plush leather sofas and grand Corinthian columns, and soundtracked by a sexagenarian bon vivant spinning vinyl, Bar Sathorn is a Bangkok original – a bar that honours the past but isn't beholden to it.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bar Snack
address: 92 2nd Ave, New York, NY 10003, United States
history: Created by bartender Iain Griffiths, this welcoming neighbourhood hangout comes with a slick, seventies-inspired vibe and a menu designed to surprise and delight in equal measure. Built on the premise of creating "dressed-up classics for dressed-down people", this is not the place to come if you want highfalutin serves poured by white-jacketed bartenders; this is the place to be for unpretentious service, inventive drinks in all shapes and sizes – think mini snaquiris all the way up to pitchers of seasonal house daiquiris for the table – and bar food so good it's become a thing of local legend. Claim a burnt orange vinyl chair at the curved, neon-laced lacquered bar and order yourself a signature frozen starter such as the Mama Tried (strawberry, yuzu and matcha blitzed with mezcal into a smack-you-in-the-face refreshing sorbetto), and follow it up with something equally cryptic or quirkily named such as the Salad Negroni, which is confusing nothing like a salad, unless you make them with nectarines, basil and gin. The most popular bar snack to accompany your drink? The bar's Spice Bag, which combines fried chicken, fries and peppers, all served in (you guessed it) a branded bag with curry sauce.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bar Spirit Forward
address: Ground Floor, Hotel Southern Star, 40/2, Lavelle Road, Shanthala Nagar, Ashok Nagar, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560001, India
history: A bustling bar with a fun, boisterous crew manning the counters, Bar Spirit Forward is built for cocktail enthusiasts who appreciate craft, clarity, and context in their drinks. With a rich backstory led by industry veterans including Arijit Bose, formerly of Countertop India and the team behind Goa's Tesouro, ranked No.4 in Asia's 50 Best Bars 2022, the bar focuses on creating a buzzy atmosphere that's punctuated by technically sound cocktails. Sink into a plush leather chair and soak in the warm, lounge-like vibe as you browse an inventive cocktail menu replete with thoughtful ingredients. Sample the Vesper Martini, for which premium spirits are prediluted, frozen, and poured straight from icy bottles, delivering a frost-fresh sip. The Southern Star, a tequila and fermented fruit blend with jalapeño and guava, offers a gentle mix of spice and sweetness, while classic inspiration meets inventive execution in the Old Cuban (rum, mint, citrus, prosecco) combining the best of a mojito and French 75. Complementing the drinks are small plates inspired by pan-Indian and global flavours. Expect elevated bar bites that change seasonally such as yams infused into both cocktails and bites. Bar Spirit Forward isn't just a bar; it's a place where like-minded folk can congregate over standout drinks.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bar Trigona
address: Four Seasons Place, 145, Jalan Ampang, 50450 Kuala Lumpur
history: Bar Trigona speaks to its setting within the gilded walls of its host, the Four Seasons Hotel Kuala Lumpur. Head bartender Rohan Matmary, who took the reins from Ashish Sharma in October 2022, has used his tenure to solidify the bar's relationship with the Malaysian farmers that supply the ingredients for its patriotic drinks programme. Its latest menu, titled Colour Me Curious 2.0, elevates this mission to new heights, with proceeds from every drink going towards the revitalisation of local bee habitats. It follows a lineage of menus that put the country's terroir into its cocktails, such as 2023's Foraged Malaysia, which focused on indigenous and sustainable ingredients, as well as Herb Garden in Your Glass, former winner of the Siete Misterios Best Cocktail Menu Award. Drinks across the menu pique interest through vibrancy and experimentation, as seen with the Wagyu, made with cognac, all spice vermouth, kulim (jungle garlic) bitters and A5 beef tallow, and the Zen, comprising gin, lychee, sherry and house-pickled ginger. Some of its creations are also available bottled, in either one-, two- or five-person servings. Don't miss the signature Trigona Honey Flight, where guests can experience three classic cocktails infused with the namesake produce at their core.
verdict: Bar Trigona speaks to its setting within the gilded walls of its host, the Four Seasons Hotel Kuala Lumpur. Head bartender Rohan Matmary, who took the reins from Ashish Sharma in October 2022, has used his tenure to solidify the bar's relationship with the Malaysian farmers that supply the ingredients for its patriotic drinks programme. Its latest menu, titled Colour Me Curious 2.0, elevates this mission to new heights, with proceeds from every drink going towards the revitalisation of local bee habitats. Drinks across the menu pique interest through vibrancy and experimentation, as seen with the Wagyu, made with cognac, all spice vermouth, kulim (jungle garlic) bitters and A5 beef tallow, and the Zen, comprising gin, lychee, sherry and house-pickled ginger. Some of its creations are also available bottled, in either one-, two- or five-person servings. Don't miss the signature Trigona Honey Flight, where guests can experience three classic cocktails infused with the namesake produce at their core.

name: Bar Us
address: 1/37 Floor, 1 Soi Sukhumvit 26, Khlong Tan, Khlong Toei, Bangkok 10110, Thailand
history: Founded in September 2023 by Sudarat 'Taln' Rojanavanich and Veerach 'Aum' Sawaengsupt, Bar Us reflects their shared vision rooted in hospitality, identity, and experience. Both partners have backgrounds in art direction and a deep love for spatial storytelling, which has shaped the concept of Bar Us in terms of design, service flow, and the overall visual language. Every cocktail at Bar Us is a lesson in precision – layered, balanced and rooted in Thai flavours. Crafted with fresh ingredients and exacting technique, each drink reimagines familiar dishes as a new sensory experience told through taste, texture, and aroma. Bar Us reimagines the 'Drinking Room' as an intimate, thoughtful space where the focus isn't just on what's in the glass, but the entire experience that surrounds it. The menu reads like a tasting menu – structured yet playful. The 'Starter' section sets the tone with light, refreshing serves that wake up the palate. The 'Mains' dive into bolder, food-inspired cocktails that are savoury, layered and sometimes disarmingly familiar. Think guava leaf, green chilli, makrut lime and an ensuing explosion of Thai flavours in every sip. Then it's time for 'After', where nostalgia meets indulgence: soft, comforting drinks that linger, while 'All Night' is a selection of easy-going classics: refined, modernised, and never over the top. That's the menu of bar that has wowed the world since launching in 2023 – not least landing it the No.4 spot on Asia's 50 Best Bars 2025.
verdict: A tasting menu in your glass, rooted in progressive flavours

name: Barc
address: Bluestar complex, Kathmandu 44600, Nepal
history: Perhaps the last place you'd expect to find the chic and intimate Barc, with its forest green walls, minimalist furniture and calming jazz soundtrack, is in the nondescript Bluestar Complex office block in the heart of Kathmandu. Hidden away, it feels like a true speakeasy. Founder Abhishek Tuladhar started making cocktails while living in Singapore, before training as a mixologist in London, and eventually returning to Nepal with a plan to bring innovative and quality drinks to the local market. He opened Barc in 2021 with his friend, Adarsha Tamrakar, after hosting a series of pop-up events. Each page of the creative cocktail menu is headed by either a key local ingredient – such as titaura (hog plum), khattu (liquorice) and aila (a Newari spirit made from fermented rice, grains and millet) – a flavour profile like 'spirit-forward', or a drink type like 'highball' or 'margarita'. The signature Bloody Nepali, served in a martini glass, is a bold mix of vodka, Barc's titaura mix and sugar, while the Peat Repeat is a smoky blend of whisky, cherry liqueur, dry vermouth and apple juice. The comprehensive bar food menu takes influence from all over Asia, including Japan, Thailand, Korea and China, with dishes including hot honey chicken, teriyaki tofu, basil chicken nachos and steamed momos. This year, Barc's success reaches new heights as it takes home a double of special accolades: the Michter's Art of Hospitality Award 2025, thanks to its impeccable service, as well as the title of The Best Bar in Nepal 2025, sponsored by Michter's.
verdict: Perhaps the last place you'd expect to find the chic and intimate Barc, with its forest green walls, minimalist furniture and calming jazz soundtrack, is in the nondescript Bluestar Complex office block in the heart of Kathmandu. name: Beba
address: 3900 Rue Éthel, Verdun, QC H4G 1S4, Canada
history:
The family ties: In 2019, brothers Ariel and Pablo Schor opened their restaurant as a tribute to their grandmother Vera, who the family lovingly knew as 'Beba'. Both siblings gained valuable experience within Montreal's prestigious Joe Beef restaurant group with Ariel spending six years as chef de cuisine at Liverpool House, while Pablo mastered wine service and dining room management. Their joint venture is an inviting neighbourhood restaurant that features sophisticated dishes rooted in their Argentine-Jewish heritage. Obviously hungry for new flavours, Montrealers have flocked to Beba since day one.

Cuisine and concept: Tucked into a residential corner of Verdun, this intimate dining room celebrates the Spanish and Italian immigrants who influenced Argentina's food culture. Chef Schor draws from his multicultural background, weaving together culinary memories from his childhood, his experience working for Jewish caterers in Winnipeg and spending time in fine-dining kitchens in Montreal. The result is deeply personal cooking crafted with pristine local ingredients, including vegetables from the farm of his former Liverpool House boss, David McMillan.

On the plate: This compact restaurant offers a focused menu beginning with signature dishes like potato knish with imperial Oscietra caviar and montadito of mackerel with sesame and horseradish. Seasonal standouts may include beef tongue with green tomato relish, and guinea fowl with maitake mushrooms, fingerling potatoes and pancetta.

A no-fuss philosophy: Chef Schor champions traditional preparations without artifice or overcomplication. This is epitomised by his love of boiled meats, a rudimentary cooking technique that may lack pizzaz, yet perfectly represents his commitment to honest cooking and his rejection of excessive styling and fine-dining fussiness.
verdict: A family tribute where tradition and pristine ingredients rule

name: Bekeb
address: Calz. De La Presa No. 85, Zona Centro, 37700 San Miguel de Allende, Gto., Mexico
history: Previously located on the terrace of a colonial building with one of the best views of San Miguel de Allende's downtown district before its 2025 move to a new location inside the luxurious Live Aqua hotel, Bekeb is at the forefront of this city's wave of innovative new cocktail bars. Named after the Tzotzil culture's word "bek," meaning "seed", owner and director Fabiola Padilla (former bartender of Enrique Olvera's Cosme at New York) designs drinks that utilise Mexican fruits, plants, flowers, seeds, roots and traditional elixirs. Cocktails featured on Bekeb's menu, titled Herbolaria Mexicana, look to Mexico's long-held history of herbalism for inspiration, with commonly found ingredients forming the backbone of each drink. From cacao flowers to lemon verbena, expect a showcase and exploration of uniquely Mexican flavours across Padilla's drinks programme. As well as the natural ingredients of Mexico, the menu also favours Mexican spirits at the base of each of its cocktails. As a Jalisco native, Padilla loves working with agave distillates. Take the Maguey, for example, where reposado tequila is combined with cooked blue agave must, pepper liqueur and bitters. From its classics section, try Tepache, a fermented, pineapple-based ancient drink that been revisited with a mix of spices, piloncillo and a base of raicilla, a wild agave distillate from the coast of Jalisco. Bekeb is the spot for locals and visitors who really want to learn more about Mexico and its hidden treasures.
verdict: Previously located on the terrace of a colonial building with one of the best views of San Miguel de Allende's downtown district before its 2025 move to a new location inside the luxurious Live Aqua hotel, Bekeb is at the forefront of this city's wave of innovative new cocktail bars. Named after the Tzotzil culture's word "bek," meaning "seed", owner and director Fabiola Padilla (former bartender of Enrique Olvera's Cosme at New York) designs drinks that utilise Mexican fruits, plants, flowers, seeds, roots and traditional elixirs. Take the Maguey, for example, where reposado tequila is combined with cooked blue agave must, pepper liqueur and bitters. From its classics section, try Tepache, a fermented, pineapple-based ancient drink that been revisited with a mix of spices, piloncillo and a base of raicilla, a wild agave distillate from the coast of Jalisco. Bekeb is the spot for locals and visitors who really want to learn more about Mexico and its hidden treasures.

name: Belcanto
address: Rua Serpa Pinto, 10 A, 1200-445 Lisbon, Portugal
history:
Origin story: A legendary Lisbon establishment that opened its doors as a men’s club in 1958, Belcanto took on a new life when chef José Avillez took the helm in 2012. In 2019, Avillez moved the restaurant to bigger and better premises – next door. Under his culinary navigation, Belcanto has progressed to become the finest in Portugal.

The low-down: The intimate restaurant lies under vaulted ceilings and minimalist chandeliers, while light streams through the high windows. Belcanto’s home offers its 45 guests two tasting menus, which take diners on a gastronomic journey of contemporary Portuguese cuisine, as well as an a la carte for those who prefer the luxury of choice.

Highlight dishes? Standout plates include cured and smoked red mullet with Algarve carrot and a parsley emulsion; matured beef sirloin paired with an onion tart, spinach and almond spread; and the whimsically named, The Garden of the Goose that Laid the Golden Eggs, which features egg with crunchy bread and mushrooms. A taste of the seas comes from scarlet shrimp with green curry, sprouts and apple.

More on the chef: A well-known face on Portuguese TV for his cooking show Improbabilicious, Avillez has also published four cookbooks and his food empire harbours a cluster of restaurants in Lisbon and Porto, including Bairro do Avillez, a food hall housing four distinctive eateries under one roof. A wine aficionado, the chef has developed several vintages under the JA line with Quinta do Monte D’Oiro winery.

Worth noting: Avillez fans should book seats at Belcanto’s chef’s table for ringside culinary action in the kitchen.
verdict: An exploration of Portuguese flavours from the country’s landmark eatery
chefName: José Avillez took the helm in

name: Benu
address: 22 Hawthorne St. San Francisco, CA 94105, United States
history:
From the beginning: Quietly located off an alley in San Francisco's SoMa district, Benu has earned countless awards since opening in 2010. Korean-American chef Corey Lee has racked up more than 25 years in the business, including many years as chef de cuisine working with legendary chef Thomas Keller at The French Laundry. Since breaking into The World's 50 Best Restaurants in 2019, Benu has shown up on the list multiple times, with Chef Lee being voted by his peers as the winner of the Estrella Damm Chefs' Choice Award, as part of North America's 50 Best Restaurants 2026.

It's really about SF: Lee's Seoul roots show up in his inspired East-meet-West cooking, where innovation and bold flavours are met with restraint. It's easy to glorify California's incomparable produce. And he does. But as Lee says, his tasting menus are centred around San Francisco and its rich cosmopolitanism, all in only 7x7-sq. miles.

What's on the plate and in the glass? Though the set, ever-changing menus feature meat, Benu goes heavy on seafood and vegetables, reflecting the best of local bounty. Lee's iconic faux shark's fin soup has been known to fool even food legends like Cecilia Chang. Benu has more than 300 wine labels focused on France, California, Germany and Austria, alongside sake and beers, curated by general manager Sinéad Quach.

If you like this, you'll love: Lee's other restaurant, San Ho Won, remains one of SF's tough-to-snag reservations since opening in 2021. His casual-but-upscale take on Korean BBQ – using lychee wood charcoal produced just for the restaurant – has gained an impressive following.
verdict: Creative dining representing San Francisco’s dense diversity
chefName: Corey Lee has racked up more than

name: Best Intentions
address: 3281 W Armitage Ave, Chicago, IL 60647, United States
history: Sometimes it is the most unassuming of neighbourhood bars that leave the biggest mark on the drinking culture of a city, and that's certainly the case with Best Intentions: a beloved neo-dive with a thoroughly Midwestern vibe. A true community gathering place that has more than its fair share of Logan Square regulars, on any given night the space is packed with people of all ages and from all walks of life, mingling in the glow of the backbar beer fridge bearing a tongue-in-cheek 'fancy cocktail bar' sign. One could easily spend an hour perusing all the quirky photographs and artwork lining the bar's wood-panelled walls – an aesthetic nod to owners Calvin and Chris Marty's Wisconsin childhood. A few arcade games in the space adjacent to the back patio solidify the unpretentious ambiance and the drink offering is just as eclectic and egalitarian, with barkeeps pulling cheap Coors Banquet drafts with the same skill they use to assemble manhattans, gimlets and old fashioneds. In the kitchen you'll find Bryan McClaran cooking up staples like steaming bolls of matzo ball soup, towering steak sandwiches and the self-proclaimed 'Huge Reuben'. When visiting, don't miss their liquid calling card: the Wondermint Malted, a modern take on the classic Grasshopper. The ice-cold Midwestern dessert drink just might be the best you'll ever have.
verdict: Sometimes it is the most unassuming of neighbourhood bars that leave the biggest mark on the drinking culture of a city, and that's certainly the case with Best Intentions: a beloved neo-dive with a thoroughly Midwestern vibe. A true community gathering place that has more than its fair share of Logan Square regulars, on any given night the space is packed with people of all ages and from all walks of life, mingling in the glow of the backbar beer fridge bearing a tongue-in-cheek 'fancy cocktail bar' sign. When visiting, don't miss their liquid calling card: the Wondermint Malted, a modern take on the classic Grasshopper. The ice-cold Midwestern dessert drink just might be the best you'll ever have.

name: Bisous
address: 938 W Fulton Market, Chicago, IL 60607, United States
history: From the Footman Hospitality group (Spilt Milk, Bangers & Lace and Emporium Arcade Bar) together with veteran barman Peter Vestinos – who opened the sultry Cuban-inspired drinking den Sparrow – Bisous is a Fulton Market gem with drinks and decor inspired by 1960s Paris. It is one of the only standalone cocktail bars in Chicago's famous Restaurant Row, making it an ideal pitstop for a first date, pre-prandial Martini, or quiet nightcap after dinner. From the romantic globe lighting to the weighty marble bar, cozy velvet booths and lavishly patterned wallpaper, no detail was overlooked in creating an atmosphere that feels sophisticated with a touch of French joie de vivre. The cocktail menu matches the mood with a rich collection of French wines, ciders and spirits and upscale bar snacks (think caviar and chips, marinated Manchego and olives, rillettes and an array of premium tinned fish). The highlight of the drinks programming is Bisous's magnificent martini menu, which serves as a love letter to sometimes overlooked iterations like the alaska, gibson, martinez and vesper, each made with subtle updates to help ferry the vintage drinks into the modern age with grace and elegance. For something ultra-savoury, there's the house Olivette, made with olive leaf-infused gin, vermouth, and saline solution; for something more refreshing, look to the restrained list of house highballs, including one with whisky, Pommeau de Normandie and tonic, and another with strawberry and pomegranate aperitifs, absinthe and Sicilian lemonade soda.
verdict: From the Footman Hospitality group (Spilt Milk, Bangers & Lace and Emporium Arcade Bar) together with veteran barman Peter Vestinos – who opened the sultry Cuban-inspired drinking den Sparrow – Bisous is a Fulton Market gem with drinks and decor inspired by 1960s Paris. For something ultra-savoury, there's the house Olivette, made with olive leaf-infused gin, vermouth, and saline solution; for something more refreshing, look to the restrained list of house highballs, including one with whisky, Pommeau de Normandie and tonic, and another with strawberry and pomegranate aperitifs, absinthe and Sicilian lemonade soda.

name: Bium
address: 1F ,41, Hakdong-ro 97-gil, Gangnam-gu, Seoul
history:
Meals rooted in mindfulness: At Bium, a shrine-like space reimagining ancient Korean temple cuisine through a fine-dining lens, food is thoughtful, intentional and grounded in simplicity – an antidote to the bells and whistles of modern, overly processed, overly hyped dining. The name translates into English as 'emptiness', which may initially sound strange; that is, until you remember that sometimes, you need to be empty to feel full again.

An epicurean epiphany: The restaurant's deliberate, wholesome philosophy is the brainchild of Kim Dae-chun, best known as the chef-owner of Seoul's fermentation-driven 7th Door. When his son fell ill, Kim sought comfort at Jingwansa Temple, where he was served straightforward food that nurtured both his health and his spirit. From that experience grew the idea for Bium, a place where cooking is designed to nourish not just the body, but the mind, too.

Humble ingredients, elevated with intent: Crafted with meticulous care from local, seasonal produce, Bium's entirely plant-based menu pays homage to the time-honoured traditions of temple fare, rejecting pungent additions like onions and garlic. Every dish served within the restaurant's serene, spiritual space – all wood, handmade hanji paper and artisan ceramics – embodies one of the four Buddhist elements: earth, air, fire and water, evoking a sense of harmony and inner balance.

What you might encounter: Previous tasting-menu dishes have included soyabean custard with mountain greens and toasted buckwheat (earth), a palate-cleansing radish broth with pine mushrooms (water), charred lotus root with barley soy sauce and perilla oil (fire) and crispy tofu skin with chrysanthemum and puffed rice (air).
verdict: Food that recalibrates body and soul

name: BKK Social Club
address: 300, 1 Charoen Krung Rd, Khwaeng Yan Nawa, Khet Sathon, Bangkok 10120, Thailand
history: BKK Social Club, inside the Four Seasons Hotel Bangkok at Chao Phraya River, is a glowing, glamorous destination bar that could easily be a Golden Era movie set. Soaring arches, a towering mirrored back bar, sensuous golden lighting and tumbling greenery furnish a room that feels impossibly luxe – even by five-star hotel standards. The al fresco smoking terraces alone are big enough to be dazzling bars in their own right. Service from bar-world veteran Philip Bischoff, manager Dheeradon 'Gotji' Dissara and head bartender Chunyanuch 'Ning' Yodsuwan is seamless and intuitive – no surprise from a bar that won the Michter's Art of Hospitality Award as part of Asia's 50 Best Bars 2022. The current menu draws inspiration from the other side of the world – Mexico – and is broken into sections called People, Places and Parties. It features elegant riffs on Michelada, tepache and horchata, a liquid tribute to the oldest bar in Tequila, and an ingenious Mezcal Negroni made with pineapple Campari, coffee vermouth and olive saline. Groups can order past favourites like the tequila-malbec-Campari gem Hand of God – a nod to football's most infamous handball – in 750ml or three-litre bottled format. Even the classics list gets clever upgrades: a peat-kissed highball and the most convincing case yet for a wet martini. For all the drama and dazzle, BKK Social Club never forgets that most fundamental thing: it's a bar. You come to drink and you leave wanting to come back.
verdict: Glitz, glamour and oodles of agave in downtown Bangkok

name: Boilermaker
address: Church, Vaddi Siolim . Enroute Thalassa from, opposite Vailanka Wine store, Siolim, Goa, 403517, India
history: What happens when beer meets whisky? You get a boilermaker. Inspired by the simple, fuss-free whisky-and-beer shot, this neighbourhood bar celebrates the region's spirit through craft and community. The story began when Nakul Bhonsle, a craft beer entrepreneur, met Pankaj Balachandran, a renowned mixologist who co-founded Bar Tesouro, ranked No.4 in Asia's 50 Best Bars in 2022. Since the pandemic, Goa's F&B scene has exploded, but many complained that it was losing its soul. Boilermaker, founded in late 2024, embodies the "susegad" mindset – a Konkani word describing a laid-back, unpretentious, and friendly temperament. Hanging at the reimagined dive bar where the decor includes grunge elements like aluminium pendant lights and a corrugated steel bar front feels like being at a house party, albeit one with exceptional food and drinks. The menu features craft beers from Bhonsle's Great State Aleworks, including IPAs, lagers, draught ales, and Goa's first Scotch ale brewed with smoked malt. Innovative cocktails shaken by Balachandran's bar consultancy Countertop include complex and clean sips like Siolim Salsa (tequila, jalapeño, gochujang, citrus) and Biso-Manana Karamel, an old-fashioned twist with overripe Moira bananas and local feni. The food menu reimagines Goan bar bites, such as Prawn Chorizo Gambas with garlic and chili, Kokum Buttered Fish, Pork Belly Rice Cake in Coconut Milk, and Pickled Cucumbers in Balchão (spicy, vinegary sauce), crafted to complement the drinks. Everything about Boilermaker is carefully considered, but the feeling you get is one of a remarkably relaxed place that's made to hang out with your gang. It's a refreshing change as well as a reminder of that local flavour that defines Goa.
verdict: New Entry

name: Bon Vivants
address: 401 Seaskye Lane, Nassau, Bahamas
history: Bon Vivants is a French term given to those who truly enjoy the heady pleasures of life, so therefore makes a very fitting moniker for this debonair Nassau drinkery. After opening its doors in 2019, the vintage-vibed space soon positioned itself at the vanguard of the region's drinking landscape through a strong focus on mixology, quality ingredients and historical inspiration. The bar itself is often referred to (most frequently by the bar's founders themselves) as the first true craft cocktail bar in The Bahamas, with the drinks menu and décor to back it up under the watchful eye of managing director and co-founder Kyle Jones. Inside, the space is awash with eye-catching patterns, vibrant colours and gold-accented vintage touches, from the tropical-printed wallpaper to the palm-frond lamps. But it's in the glass (flamingo or tiki-shaped) where the real magic happens. Come during the day for espresso-based drinks, light bites or a cup of the bar's signature hot chocolate made with fresh Bootleg Chocolate ganache. Visit in the evening to work your way through a cocktail list that draws inspiration from Bahamian culture as well as the adventurous lifestyle of writer Ernest Hemingway in the form of creative house signatures such as The Long Surrender (tequila, curaçao, Scrappy's Firewater Tincture, passion fruit liqueur, lime, agave) and tiki-style sips. Bartenders go the extra mile here to champion fresh, whether citrus or coconut milk, making for drinks that maximise tropical flavours through modern techniques. When you've exhausted the cocktail list, turn to the spirit selection, which, at more than 400 bottles, is the largest on the island.
verdict: New Entry

name: Boragó
address: San José María Escrivá de Balaguer 5970, Región Metropolitana, Santiago, 7640804, Chile
history:
Who’s the chef? Rodolfo Guzmán toured restaurants across Europe and Chile, learning new techniques and experimenting with flavours, before resettling in Santiago to open Boragó in 2006. Since then, the restaurant has become a haven for Guzmán’s discovery of, and research into, native Chilean produce.

What to expect: The menu, named Endémica, showcases the bounty Chile has to offer. Guzmán takes diners through the country’s regions with creatively executed dishes such as mussels, fava beans and unripe peaches, onion and pink tomato tarte tatin and Patagonian lamb with fig leaf and Maule pink tomato.

What’s the inspiration? Boragó is deeply rooted in Mapuche culture. Inspired by the Indigenous hunter-gatherers of Southern Chile and Argentina, Guzmán strives to incorporate the autochthonous plants of the nation into the restaurant’s vast pantry. He works with more than 200 people, including foraging communities and small producers all over the country, to bring seasonal and fresh goods to the plate. The ingredients come from coastlines, mountain tops or even the restaurant’s nearby orchard.

Second home: Boragó is in Vitacura, a suburb of Santiago at the foot of the city’s tallest mountain, Cerro Manquehue. The landmark makes both a stunning backdrop for the 54-cover restaurant and a home for Guzmán’s food research centre, CIB, that delves into the untapped possibilities of Chilean ingredients.

Sustainability is key: Since its inception, the team at Boragó has placed high importance on sustainability, maintaining local ecosystems and responsible sourcing. Its efforts earned it the inaugural Sustainable Restaurant Award at Latin America’s 50 Best Restaurants in 2018. With its focus on zero-mile cooking, a biodynamic farm and decades of research, Boragó went on to win the same accolade at The World’s 50 Best Restaurants 2021.
verdict: Beautiful Chilean produce from farm to fork

name: Born
address: 1 Neil Rd, #01-01, Singapore 088804
history:
An architectural icon: Contemporary fine-dining restaurant Born can be found on Neil Road, a Chinatown street famous for its beautifully preserved colonial shophouses – and even in such a colourful neighbourhood as this, it stands out from the crowd. Housed inside the historic Jinrikisha Station (which, constructed in 1903, originally served as a rickshaw depot), the striking triangular building occupies a position at a junction, giving it a landmark quality.

Heritage bones, 21st-century accents: With high glass ceilings, expansive arches and a large suspended paper sculpture at its core, Born's main dining room is as luminous as it is spacious, evoking the atmosphere of a cosmopolitan art gallery. The bar and lounge area is a little more relaxed, with low chairs and sofas providing the perfect setting to linger over pre-dinner drinks and amuse-bouches.

Honouring life's journey: Malaysian-Chinese chef Zor Tan served under the renowned André Chiang at three of his prestigious restaurants before founding Born in July 2022. A scintillating showcase of Chinese and Asian flavours and exacting French technique, his multi-course tasting menu is based on the circle of life, celebrating nostalgia, growth and the passage of time. Each heartfelt course reflects one of nine core principles: Birth, Roots, Memories, Craft, Relationship, Vicissitudes, Time, Progress and Legacy.

What does that mean, exactly? Expect courses such as Jerusalem artichoke with lily bulbs and caviar; representing Roots, this dish reflects Tan's dedication to wholesome ingredients and ancestral culinary traditions. Under the banner of Memories, you'll discover creations like monkfish with fermented chilli and chicken fat – a dish that draws on Tan's past experiences with Sichuan cuisine. Sweet potato with oolong and sea salt, on the other hand, is a dessert symbolising Birth via a medley of childhood flavours.
verdict: A life story conveyed in nine courses
chefName: Zor Tan served under the renowned André Chiang at three of his prestigious restaurants before founding Born in July

name: Botanist Bar
address: 1038 Canada Pl, Vancouver, BC V6C 0B9, Canada
history: Science meets nature at the Botanist Bar and cocktail lab at the Fairmont Pacific Rim hotel in downtown Vancouver. In a sophisticated space that feels as bright and airy as the surrounding restaurant, the tight-knit bar team led by beverage director Grant Sceney draws inspiration from the natural bounty of the Pacific Northwest, sourcing and foraging local ingredients to mix into drinks that are equal parts showpiece and storytelling. Take the Botanist Marine Martini, for example. Made with a dry gin blend, house vermouth, kombu, chive oil and sea asparagus, its creation was inspired by the city's reputation for precipitation, with the drink's extra dry profile offering escape from the notorious wet weather. Thanks to its superlative service programme, Botanist Bar was recognised as the winner of the Michter's Art of Hospitality Award in 2023. As the seasons change, so does the cocktail menu, and the featured spirit-free options are just as inventive as the booze-fuelled ones, employing some of the same technologies (think rotary evaporator and centrifuge) to extract the best flavour from ingredients like cherry blossom, douglas fir and pumpkin pecan cordial. If cocktails aren't the plan, stop by the champagne lounge for an engaging selection of sparkling wines from around the world, and don't miss out on the highly seasonal cuisine at the restaurant, which has been awarded its own array of accolades.
verdict: Science meets nature at the Botanist Bar and cocktail lab at the Fairmont Pacific Rim hotel in downtown Vancouver. In a sophisticated space that feels as bright and airy as the surrounding restaurant, the tight-knit bar team led by beverage director Grant Sceney draws inspiration from the natural bounty of the Pacific Northwest, sourcing and foraging local ingredients to mix into drinks that are equal parts showpiece and storytelling. Take the Botanist Marine Martini, for example. Thanks to its superlative service programme, Botanist Bar was recognised as the winner of the Michter's Art of Hospitality Award in 2023. As the seasons change, so does the cocktail menu, and the featured spirit-free options are just as inventive as the booze-fuelled ones, employing some of the same technologies (think rotary evaporator and centrifuge) to extract the best flavour from ingredients like cherry blossom, douglas fir and pumpkin pecan cordial. If cocktails aren't the plan, stop by the champagne lounge for an engaging selection of sparkling wines from around the world, and don't miss out on the highly seasonal cuisine at the restaurant, which has been awarded its own array of accolades.

name: Café La Trova
address: 971 SW 8th St, Miami, Florida, 33130
history: Julio Cabrera is a celebrated ambassador for Cuban culture in the cocktail world – both him and his signature fedora seem present wherever fine rum or cigars are to be found. In 2019, the ex-pat opened Café La Trova, a virtual embassy for his native island's culinary culture in Miami's aptly named Little Havana neighbourhood. Thanks to his lifelong contribution to the bar sector, Cabrera took home the Roku Industry Icon Award in 2023. A collaboration with James Beard Award-winning chef Michelle Bernstein, Café La Trova is a joyous celebration of Cuban hospitality. Stepping into the space, you're instantly transported to the Caribbean through sight and taste. The bar team adheres to the traditional (and theatrical) cantinero style created by Constantine Ribalaigua at Havana's legendary El Floridita. Typically, the bartenders are busy throwing Hotel Nacionals (rum, apricot liqueur, pineapple juice, lime juice) through the air or dramatically shaking Daiquiri Clásico (rum, lime juice, sugar). Yet, the dinner jacket-uniformed staff also find time to pick up maracas and play along with the house Son Cubano band. Bernstein's food is equally harmonious. Authentic buñuelos de bacalao (salt cod fritters) and croquetas provide accompaniment for the rum-forward drinks. Full entrées are also available, like the crispy whole boneless snapper served with avocado, or the divine mojo-marinated lechon (pork shoulder) with steamed yuca and pickled onions. Vegans are also well-catered to – the fricassee vegano sees tofu drenched in mojo, Cuba's signature citrus-forward sauce, while the mushroom ropa vieja brings the heat with warming spices. Be sure to leave some time – and room – to dance after dinner.
verdict: Julio Cabrera is a celebrated ambassador for Cuban culture in the cocktail world – both him and his signature fedora seem present wherever fine rum or cigars are to be found. In 2019, the ex-pat opened Café La Trova, a virtual embassy for his native island's culinary culture in Miami's aptly named Little Havana neighbourhood. Vegans are also well-catered to – the fricassee vegano sees tofu drenched in mojo, Cuba's signature citrus-forward sauce, while the mushroom ropa vieja brings the heat with warming spices. Be sure to leave some time – and room – to dance after dinner.

name: Cantina del Tigre
address: Plaza Centenario, C. 68 Este, Panamá, Panama
history:
Origin story: In 2021, chef Fulvio Miranda decided to do what no one else was doing in Panama City: he created a culinary space focused on ceviche, a near-forgotten dish in his country, drawing inspiration from regional recipes. In 2023, Cantina del Tigre (translation: the tiger's canteen) debuted on Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants, winning the Highest New Entry Award.

What's the concept? The restaurant serves a range of traditional, but reimagined Panamanian dishes. Miranda's boldness and creativity are showcased in colourful preparations that are gaining increasing popularity across the region.

Who's in charge? Born in the Chiriquí Province, Miranda founded his first gastronomic venture after seven short years in the industry – a food truck called Esa Flaca Rica serving hamburgers inspired by the flavours of his homeland. His art is not limited to the kitchen: a bass player and composer with a long career in the local scene, he launched a solo record in 2022 called Vieto Sound System.

Have a taste: The original dishes on the menu would be hard to find anywhere else and are matched only by the drinks offer. Cebiche de mamón chino (rambutan ceviche), red rice, mondongo (a hearty traditional Latin American stew made with tripe) and rice tamales with prawns are only some of the delicious signatures on the a la carte menu. Vegetarians and vegans are well catered for, while the staff also serves up generous rounds of papaya negronis and pepino (cucumber) mezcal sours.

The ambience: The project began in a modest backyard but, in October 2022, moved to a larger space in front of the previous venue. The space also boasts a bar area, a spacious garden, a wine cellar and a private dining room. Each is adorned with warm lighting which evokes the colour of a tiger's fur – and tigers are also painted in every room to help create a stylish and eccentric atmosphere.
verdict: The culinary roar of a uniquely Panamanian tiger
chefName: Fulvio Miranda decided to do what no one else was doing in Panama City

name: Caprice
address: Four Seasons Hotel, 8 Finance Street, Central, Hong Kong
history:
Opulence abounds: With its glass-floored catwalk, dazzling crystal chandeliers, panoramic harbour views and smooth service from a fleet of immaculately groomed staff, Caprice oozes opulence at every opportunity. Here, every meal is destined to be a memorable occasion that's well worth dressing up for.

The man at the pass: Guillaume Galliot worked with the Pourcel brothers at the lauded Le Jardin des Sens in Montpellier, France, before he set his sights on the wider world, with stints in New York, the Caribbean and Morocco, before landing in Asia. There, he made splashes at Raffles in Singapore and Beijing, then with The Tasting Room by Galliot in Macau, before taking the reins at Caprice.

And the food? Unabashedly luxurious – think Australian wagyu beef with Gillardeau oyster and caviar; daily wild caught fish with a light seafood consommé and Timut pepper; and a Brittany blue lobster, beetroot and chocolate with ruby sauce.Often, Galliot leans into his French gastronomic tendencies; think Challandais duck tart with foie gras. His signature dish, king crab with crustacean jelly and prestige caviar served in a spotless glass sphere, is a work of art.

A fine wine haven: Galliot a Château d'Yquem ambassador, with the iconic Sauternes available by the glass and poured from five-litre bottles.
verdict: Uninhibited French opulence overlooking Victoria Harbour

name: Caretaker's Cottage
address: 139-141 Little Lonsdale St, Melbourne VIC 3000, Australia
history: Beneath the skyscrapers in the middle of the Melbourne CBD, there's a small, humble-looking place that was once the residence of the caretaker of the church next door. It's here you'll find a small team taking care of the thirsty, the tired and those in need of a good time. Caretaker's Cottage opened here quietly in 2022; it has since become a must-visit for locals and travellers alike. The owners – Matt Stirling, Ryan Nordics and Rob Libecans – have some of the world's best bars on their resumes, and the skills to match. But at Caretaker's Cottage, they hang their hats on two distinct drinks that you can find anywhere, but nowhere as good as here: the martini and Guinness. Come in the afternoon, and you'll see guests with a Guinness in hand; the smart drinkers order the best boilermaker in the country, a pint of the black stuff and their signature martini: served bracingly cold from the freezer with a gin the bar has custom made, it appears regularly on lists of Australia's best cocktails. But to stop there would be to miss the rotating list of signature cocktails that change every month, on a newly designed menu from Noreiks. But the real feat they've achieved in a small place, is ensuring that – despite being Melbourne's busiest cocktail bar – every guest feels like they've found a place of their own.
verdict: Discover world-leading martinis in this Melbourne city refuge

name: Carrots Bar
address: Fairgrounds SCBD Jl. Jenderal Sudirman No.14 Lot 14, Basement Level, Jakarta 12190, Indonesia
history: Hidden in the basement of a nightlife complex in Jakarta's SCBD, Carrots is an intimate 14-seat cocktail bar defined by its sense of playfulness, community and craft. Co-founders Alvin Sung Jaya and Monica Jonan, alongside creative partner Anthony Luis, have built a low-lit, design-led space that feels part speakeasy, part social table. The centrepiece is an eight-seat communal bar surrounded by standing room, where guests are encouraged to mingle over drinks that blend innovative culinary thinking with classic cocktail structure. The current menu I Drink, Therefore I Am is a seasonal exploration of flavour designed to meet guests wherever their palate lies. From banana-daiquiri riffs to sarsaparilla-smoked highballs, cocktails here are inventive without being self-indulgent. Everything is rooted in careful R&D, with nods to Indo-Chinese ingredients and kitchen techniques. Serious about flavour but not too serious about itself, Carrots has quickly made a name for itself in an ever-evolving scene.
verdict: New Entry

name: Casa Las Cujas
address: Alonso de Córdova 2467, Vitacura, Santiago, 7630418
history:
Coastal concept: What began over a decade ago as a beachside kitchen in Cachagua, a small fishing village north of Valparaíso, has evolved into one of Santiago's most beloved seafood destinations. Founded by brothers Juan Pablo, Max and Domingo Raide, Casa Las Cujas brings the spirit of the Pacific Ocean to the capital, celebrating Chile's marine bounty like oysters, machas (a type of razor clams), and Patagonian king crab.

Ocean interiors: In Santiago's Vitacura district, a hub for dining and design, Casa Las Cujas mirrors the calm rhythm of its beachside origins, channeling the ease of the coast within the city. Sun-bleached tones, nautical accents and a looping projection of Chile's northern beaches lend a sense of salt, air and light, while a glass tank of live shellfish at the restaurant's centre serves as a living pantry; a reminder that every plate begins in the sea.

On the menu: The kitchen is guided by seasonality and respect for fresh ingredients. Dishes such as Caleta Las Cujas, a grand platter of the day's freshest catch, capture the Pacific at its most elemental. Other highlights include Patagonian king crab served two ways, locos (Chilean abalone) in salsa verde, and a smoky rice simmered in crab stock. The menu balances tradition with lightness and ease, from empanadas filled with local shellfish to clam chowder perfumed with merkén, a smoky indigenous Mapuche spice blend of chilli.

Beyond beach cooking: For the Raide family, Casa Las Cujas represents a deep sense of place and community. Their approach, cocina de playa (beach cooking), goes beyond ingredients. It's about honouring the coast, the people who work its waters and the shared pleasure of gathering over simple, excellent food. Each service invites diners to slow down, breathe in the echoes of the waves, and experience the spirit of the ocean from the heart of the city.

Culinary family: In May 2025, Casa Las Cujas marked its 11th anniversary by hosting La Ruta Trasandina, a collaborative event that brought together leading chefs from across Latin America. The gathering reaffirmed the restaurant's place at the heart of Chile's contemporary culinary movement, serving as a bridge between coastlines, cultures and cooks across the region.
verdict: The soul of the Chilean coast finds home in the city

name: Cat Bite Club
address: 75 Duxton Rd, Singapore 089534
history: After launching their bartending careers in San Francisco in 2007, operating partners Gabriel Lowe (previous bar programme curator of Paradise Lost) and Jesse Vida (former Atlas head bartender) opened Cat Bite Club's doors in June 2023 in Singapore's buzzing Duxton neighbourhood. Embracing the world of agave early on in their careers in the West, the duo recognised that same potential in Asian legacy liquors, like soju and shochu. Aptly, the drinks menu covers two regions – Mexico and Asia – sharing ingredient origin stories while celebrating the character of the corresponding base spirit. Each section boasts a signature serve: the Cat Bite Margarita (tequila, Siete Misterios Espadín mezcal, orange liqueur, citrus blend, agave nectar, sherry) for agave and the Soju Sprint (soju, peach, honey, mint, citrus blend, sherry, absinthe, soda) for rice. From there, each page sees one classic cocktail plus two separate playful renditions of said classic through the lens of agave and rice. For instance, the Old Cuban can be ordered with agave at the fore as the Corsair (Cointreau, mezcal, vermouth, malic acid, amaro, bitters, carbonation) or you could opt for the Grape Escape, a rice-forward interpretation of the classic, (shochu, gin, grape shrub, elderflower liqueur, gentian, amaro, citrus blend and sparkling sake). There's also a weekly milk punch for a Cause, with a portion of sales donated to local cat charities. The food menu also underscores the crossover between Mexican and Asian gastronomic traditions, with clever cultural collabs like Korean chicken al pastor quesadillas. Cat Bite Club's sophisticated drinking experience, thoughtful menu, curated bottle list and commitment to the craft made it the deserving winner of the Campari One To Watch Award 2024.
verdict: After launching their bartending careers in San Francisco in 2007, operating partners Gabriel Lowe (previous bar programme curator of Paradise Lost) and Jesse Vida (former Atlas head bartender) opened Cat Bite Club's doors in June 2023 in Singapore's buzzing Duxton neighbourhood. Embracing the world of agave early on in their careers in the West, the duo recognised that same potential in Asian legacy liquors, like soju and shochu. There's also a weekly milk punch for a Cause, with a portion of sales donated to local cat charities. The food menu also underscores the crossover between Mexican and Asian gastronomic traditions, with clever cultural collabs like Korean chicken al pastor quesadillas. Cat Bite Club's sophisticated drinking experience, thoughtful menu, curated bottle list and commitment to the craft made it the deserving winner of the Campari One To Watch Award 2024.

name: Celele
address: Calle del Espiritu Santo, cr 10c # 29 – 200, Getsemaní, Cartagena, Colombia
history:
Meet the chef: Chef Jaime Rodríguez spent years exploring the Caribbean coast of Colombia with former restaurant partner Sebastián Pinzón, meeting Indigenous people, discovering new flavours and ingredients and documenting recipes that were in danger of being lost. This research project, called Proyecto Caribe Lab, continues to inform the restaurant’s concept today, now led by Chef Rodríguez alone.

What’s the experience? Diners can choose from the a la carte menu that explores everything the Caribbean coast has to offer, accompanied by thoughtful drinks flights of regional craft beers, cocktails made with Colombian fruits, fermented spirits and wines. Around 90 per cent of the products on the menu are from the Caribbean, with 70 per cent from wild harvests. Expect to try rare ingredients like orejero (tree seeds often made into a sweet paste), guaimaro (maya nut), pomarrosa (malay apples that taste like rosewater) and jumbalee (a wild fruit).

What will I eat? Dishes include Caribbean flower salad with pickled cashews and passion fruit dressing; squid and mussels with a mussel broth reduction and plantain dumplings; the signature Celele de Cerdo of confit pork terrine, preserved sweet peppers, Caribbean beans, cabbage and pork broth; or fish salpicón, with yam and coconut bechamel and local cheese crumble.

For dessert: It’s hard to resist the creamy chocolate from La Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta dish, served with a tropical dry forest crumble and borojó gel (a sweet-tart fruit), or the yuca leaf and mambe (toasted coca leaf powder) cake served tiramisu style, with a hibiscus leaf sorbet.
verdict: Groundbreaking Colombian-Caribbean fusion
chefName: Jaime Rodríguez spent years exploring the Caribbean coast of Colombia with former restaurant partner Sebastián Pinzón

name: César
address: 333 Hudson St. New York, NY 10013, United States
history:
Speedy success: Opened in July 2024 by chef César Ramirez, this self-titled spot made big waves in a short time. Within a few months of opening, Ramirez's razor-sharp precision and globally influenced 13-course tasting menu earned him not only a spot on the inaugural North America's 50 Best Restaurants 2025 list, but two Michelin stars as well. A year on, César has climbed 28 spots to reach No.10 on the 2026 ranking.

Growing up global: Born in a small town in Mexico City and raised in Chicago, Ramirez draws, in part, on his culinary childhood memories and his great-grandmother's Spanish heritage for his seafood-focused menu. Coupled with French inflections from his travels in Europe and a passion for Japanese precision and ingredients from trips to the country during his time at New York's Bouley, César has a truly international flavour.

Icon status: Located in a former printing house in Soho, César's minimalist interiors will be home to some of Ramirez's iconic dishes from Bouley and Brooklyn Fare, where he ran the legendary chef's table for 14 years until his departure in 2023 – his legendary Hokkaido uni brioche toast and seasonal matcha and corn soufflés all make appearances on the pass.

Beyond the sea: While spoils of the sea are the main focal point, diners can expect to find a who's who of luxe and rare ingredients on the menu. Stand-outs include culinary rarities such as a Japanese breed of white quail sourced from the same Sacramento ranch that supplies Thomas Keller, live Norwegian langoustine with caviar and Hokkaido uni, and sturgeon rillettes.
verdict: Sleek seafood from chef’s table trailblazer

name: Bar Cham
address: 34 Jahamun-ro 7-gil, Tongin-dong, Jongno-gu, Seoul
history: Tucked away amongst the patchwork of intricately tiled rooftop dwellings in one of the oldest neighbourhoods in Seoul, Bar Cham is set in a hanok [traditional Korean house]. Slide open the wooden door and you'll be welcomed into a softly lit cocktail haven helmed by Byung-Jin 'BJ' Lim and his dynamic team. Cham was one of the first bars in the capital city to pioneer a Korean-culture-first ethos that truly puts a spotlight on traditional spirits and indigenous ingredients. On the menu you can expect to find a plethora in native alcohols, with products sourced from all over the provinces of South Korea. The team uses deodeok (a native root vegetable) and Korean bitters for its version of a subtly spiced negroni. Other cocktails, such as the Wonju, meld Korean drinks traditionalism with new-world vision, pairing mowallin – a distillate made using local organic rice – with mezcal, corn cream, corn silk, black pepper and egg yolk. Even if you weren't a fan of soju at the start of the night, by the end of it you're sure to be an aficionado thanks to the small shots in traditional clay cups handed out by BJ and crew – these fiery sips are made to be drunk quickly to the jubilant shout of 'geon bae!' The space is small and seating limited so be sure to book in advance or, alternatively, add your name to the waitlist and venture a few streets over to Pomme, another of BJ's ventures, to sip an apple-spirit-spiked cocktail while you wait for your perch at Cham.
verdict: Tucked away amongst the patchwork of intricately tiled rooftop dwellings in one of the oldest neighbourhoods in Seoul, Bar Cham is set in a hanok [traditional Korean house]. Slide open the wooden door and you'll be welcomed into a softly lit cocktail haven helmed by Byung-Jin 'BJ' Lim and his dynamic team. Cham was one of the first bars in the capital city to pioneer a Korean-culture-first ethos that truly puts a spotlight on traditional spirits and indigenous ingredients. On the menu you can expect to find a plethora in native alcohols, with products sourced from all over the provinces of South Korea. The team uses deodeok (a native root vegetable) and Korean bitters for its version of a subtly spiced negroni. Other cocktails, such as the Wonju, meld Korean drinks traditionalism with new-world vision, pairing mowallin – a distillate made using local organic rice – with mezcal, corn cream, corn silk, black pepper and egg yolk. Even if you weren't a fan of soju at the start of the night, by the end of it you're sure to be an aficionado thanks to the small shots in traditional clay cups handed out by BJ and crew – these fiery sips are made to be drunk quickly to the jubilant shout of 'geon bae!' The space is small and seating limited so be sure to book in advance or, alternatively, add your name to the waitlist and venture a few streets over to Pomme, another of BJ's ventures, to sip an apple-spirit-spiked cocktail while you wait for your perch at Cham.

name: Chef Tam's Seasons
address: Wynn Palace, Av. da Nave Desportiva, Cotai, Macau
history:
What's the hype? Inside the extravagant Wynn Palace complex, you'll find Chef Tam's Seasons (previously named Wing Lei Palace): the restaurant of the celebrated chef Tam Kwok Fung. The newly re-monikered restaurant showcases refined Cantonese cuisine, featuring the finest seasonal produce in the most opulent surroundings.

On the menu: With both a la carte and tasting menu offerings, Chef Tam's Seasons offers a wide selection of dishes, including signatures such as braised grouper fillet with sweet bean curd sticks, pork belly and garlic; wok-fried late flower Chinese cabbage with turnip and minced pork; fish maw and pork tripe in chicken and peppercorn broth with rice noodles; and chilled strawberry and sago sweet soup with superior bird's nest and deep-fried walnut custard. There's also a wide selection of live seafood, barbecued meats and dim sum.

Palatial by name, palatial by nature: The interior of Chef Tam's Seasons leans into the name and opulence of its host hotel. Gold, jewels and emerald jade line every surface, with a lush floral garden in the entrance featuring more than 5,000 roses, hydrangeas and exotic flowers. The pièce de résistance is the view onto the resort's Performance Lake, where graceful plumes of water spring into spectacular action at regular intervals.

About the chef: Earning his reputation at the renowned Jade Dragon in Macau, Chef Tam moved to the Wynn location in 2018 and earned Wing Lei Palace its No.36 position in the 2019 ranking of Asia's 50 Best Restaurants. He has cooked for politicians and royal families and won a gold medal in the World Champion of Chinese Cooking competition. In 2026, the restaurant has retained its title as The Best Restaurant in Macau.
verdict: Exquisite Cantonese cuisine in palatial surroundings

name: Chubby Fish
address: 252 Coming St. Charleston, SC 29403, United States
history:
Dock-to-table: Charleston knows its seafood. So for locals to line up 'round the block every night (more on that in a minute) to wait for fish, you know it has to be good. The kitchen coordinates with fishers to get the best, freshest catch every day. The menu changes as a result, but expect an assortment of raw bar options; oysters harvested along the East Coast from South Carolina all the way up to Maine; and dishes such as chilli garlic shrimp and bone marrow with shrimp tempura and spicy mayonnaise.

Chef CV: James London is a man of Charleston. He grew up, as South Carolinians do, cooking barbecue. He worked in fine-dining restaurants while attending the College of Charleston. From there he attended The French Culinary Institute in New York, and worked in kitchens in New York and San Francisco, racking up awards, until Charleston's siren song brought him home.

What's the secret: It's true, Chubby Fish does not take reservations. And there are only 40 seats in the restaurant. So, expect a queue. The best strategy is to skip lunch and get there for an early dinner. Or put your name on the list and stroll around Cannonborough, or have a drink at Seahorse next door. Be prepared to order when you sit though: tables have a 60-minute limit.

Drink to that: As you would expect from a place that is thoughtful about each ingredient and each purveyor, the wine and beer selection is similarly thoughtful, designed to pair well with seafood. Non-alcoholic options are also more than afterthoughts.

World recognition: London's advocacy for sustainable fishing and sourcing have put him on the international map. Well, that and the blue crab tagliatelle.
verdict: Expect to queue for this super popular fish and seafood hotspot

name: Civil Works
address: 50 Brant St, Toronto, ON M5V 3G9, Canada
history: Swanky bars with impressive cocktail programmes have been popping up in the world's best food halls of late, but few are as striking as this sprawling mezzanine with a grand hotel lobby vibe and a gleaming long bar that defines Civil Works. Luxe blue velvet armchairs in the lounge afford a first-rate view of the playful hanging art installation – a series of massive colourful globes that light up the brickwork in the post-industrial Waterworks Food Hall. The view from a barstool reveals just how tight this bar's ambitious cocktail programme truly is, as well as a commitment to pouring a smooth and creamy pint of Guinness that borders on obsession. Brought to you by the same folks responsible for the occasionally irreverent Civil Liberties, it's no surprise that the menu is full of cheek and double entendre, as well as earnest research into Toronto history. The drinks run the gamut from sturdy house drinks like the Woolnough (Islay single malt, aromatised wines, haskap, vetiver) that pays homage to the building's original architect, J.J. Woolnough, to the Playdate, a mix of cognac, madeira, carrot-coffee cream as well as foam and fizz. Better yet, there's no need to leave the table for snacks, thanks to chefs from the food hall who are only too happy to send up corn empanadas, deep-fried Brussel sprouts and a habit-forming Bao with pulled pork and pineapple.
verdict: New Entry

name: Clemente Bar
address: 11 Madison Ave, New York, NY 10010, United States
history: Daniel Humm needs little introduction in the world of hospitality. When the visionary chef behind New York fine dining institution Eleven Madison Park reached out to Italian artist Francesco Clemente, he simply wanted to collaborate on the creation of a single cocktail for the restaurant. What followed was a fast move into a full partnership and the opening of a whole new bar one floor above EMP's dining room. The first impression Clemente Bar makes is its crowning glory. The Neapolitan artist's masterful touch is manifest throughout the space, from the breath-taking ceiling fresco above the entrance stairwell – painted directly on-site by Clemente – to the two murals that sweep around the perimeter of the main bar area. Clemente has substance under the surface, too, with a drinks programme that pulls from its sister restaurant's penchant for explorative flavours. The result? Cocktails that effortlessly toe the line between layered complexity and elegant simplicity. Take the Clemente Martini, for example, where the team have delicately imbued the timeless classic with aromatics of green curry and saffron, or the Parakeet, made with gin, strawberry-infused Campari, red wine molasses and pineapple. In addition to the main bar room, Clemente Bar also offers an omakase-style five-course tasting menu experience in The Studio, where picture-perfect, plant-based bites, such as the agedashi tofu handroll and maitake mushroom skewer come paired with a curated selection of cocktails. Since opening its doors in late 2024, Clemente Bar quickly became one of New York's hottest cocktail destinations, earning it the Three Cents Best New Opening Award in 2025.
verdict: Daniel Humm needs little introduction in the world of hospitality. When the visionary chef behind New York fine dining institution Eleven Madison Park reached out to Italian artist Francesco Clemente, he simply wanted to collaborate on the creation of a single cocktail for the restaurant. What followed was a fast move into a full partnership and the opening of a whole new bar one floor above EMP's dining room. The first impression Clemente Bar makes is its crowning glory. The Neapolitan artist's masterful touch is manifest throughout the space, from the breath-taking ceiling fresco above the entrance stairwell – painted directly on-site by Clemente – to the two murals that sweep around the perimeter of the main bar area. Clemente has substance under the surface, too, with a drinks programme that pulls from its sister restaurant's penchant for explorative flavours. The result? Cocktails that effortlessly toe the line between layered complexity and elegant simplicity. Take the Clemente Martini, for example, where the team have delicately imbued the timeless classic with aromatics of green curry and saffron, or the Parakeet, made with gin, strawberry-infused Campari, red wine molasses and pineapple. In addition to the main bar room, Clemente Bar also offers an omakase-style five-course tasting menu experience in The Studio, where picture-perfect, plant-based bites, such as the agedashi tofu handroll and maitake mushroom skewer come paired with a curated selection of cocktails. Since opening its doors in late 2024, Clemente Bar quickly became one of New York's hottest cocktail destinations, earning it the Three Cents Best New Opening Award in 2025.

name: CMYK
address: 388 Zhongshan West Road, Changsha, Hunan, 452370
history: Named after both the acronym for the four primary colours used in the printing process – cyan, magenta, yellow and black (key) – and the hangout's tagline 'Countless Memories You Keep', CMYK sprouted from the idea that "the superposition of four shades can create countless new colours", says owner Ethan Liu. When applied to cocktails, the theory is a celebration of the myriad flavour combinations that a creative street-style bar team, like CMYK, can conjure. Liu – an award-winning bartender in his own right – drew inspiration from years of global travel, culminating in a high-energy, multi-room cosmopolitan bar built inside an old house along Changsha's historic Chaozong Street. Here, guests can expect to sip technically advanced cocktails delivered with colourful pizazz. The cocktail list is curated to offer libations that are 'easy to love but not too complicated to enjoy', with a sustainability-focused backdrop. Case in point, the Smooth Operator combines bourbon, salted egg, pineapple, peanut butter, honey, sesame oil and lemon. A host of easy sippers, such as the Yeah Coconut (aged rum, coconut, pineapple) are just as noteworthy. In fashionable Changsha – one of China's buzziest cities, recognised for its vibrant nightlife and spicy Hunan cuisine – there is no better place for young, adventurous drinkers to mix, while sipping mixed drinks.
verdict: Named after both the acronym for the four primary colours used in the printing process – cyan, magenta, yellow and black (key) – and the hangout's tagline 'Countless Memories You Keep', CMYK sprouted from the idea that "the superposition of four shades can create countless new colours", says owner Ethan Liu. A host of easy sippers, such as the Yeah Coconut (aged rum, coconut, pineapple) are just as noteworthy. In fashionable Changsha – one of China's buzziest cities, recognised for its vibrant nightlife and spicy Hunan cuisine – there is no better place for young, adventurous drinkers to mix, while sipping mixed drinks.

name: Coa
address: Shop A, LG/F Wah Shin House, 6-10 Shin Hing Street, Central, Hong Kong
history: Opened in 2017 by award-winning bartender Jay Khan, and named after the traditional tool for harvesting agave, Coa has established itself as the region's authority on all things tequila and mezcal. Inspired by Mexico's drinking dens or mezcalerias, Khan decided to bring a piece of that culture back to Hong Kong as a celebration of agave, a spirit he has long championed. This proverbial shrine to the Mexican spirits has since become a playground for some of the finest cocktail innovation in the city and the region, earning the 40-seater bar a loyal following and serpentine queues around the block. The drinks programme is designed as an ode to Mexican flavours with a new cocktail introduced every week crafted from seasonal hand-picked ingredients. Unsurprisingly, their agave spirits list is deep and considered, stretching across more than forty pages – making it a gathering place for global agave enthusiasts. This detailed list is categorised by agave species, mentioning details of terroir as well as trivia on factors like variety, soil, microclimate and how they all impact the final spirit. Stepping into Coa, the Mexican influence is evident throughout, from the textiles to the low seating, it transports guests to the streets of Oaxaca, where the only decision to be made is whether to order the Bloody "Beef" Maria, the Pepper Smash, or both. The food menu complements the drinks and has been carefully crafted in collaboration with award-winning Mexican restaurant Chicano.
verdict: An agave temple in the heart of Hong Kong

name: CoChinChina
address: Armenia 1540, C1414 Buenos Aires, Argentina
history: Whether you sink into a striped deckchair on the pavement or sit at the eye-catching U-shaped bar underneath the towering Asian street signs, the intent is clear: CoChinChina wants to carry you away to a brand-new destination. A theatrical cocktail bar and restaurant located in Palermo, Buenos Aires, whose name in Spanish translates as 'a faraway place', owner and bartender Inés De Los Santos unites the two diverse worlds of Vietnam and France and gives them an Argentine accent. Depending on your mood, an array of spaces have been conceived, from intimate booths for romantic dates to the low bar counter for a mixology close-up. If you're tempted by the tasting menu paired with cocktails, book a table upstairs at the more chilled-out Arriba. Music spun by the DJ keeps energy levels high, varying between soul, hip hop and tech house that often has revellers dancing in the aisles. While CoChinChina's interior design is show-stopping – take your phone to the bathroom for a photo with the goldfish wall – De Los Santos' cocktails are effortlessly elegant, tipping a nod to both France and Vietnam. Order the iconic La Vida Que Me Merezco (The Life I Deserve), a margarita balanced with pineapple, lemon and vanilla; another classic is Blend De Los Buenos, made with Santos' very own Cantieri Navali vermouth, soda water and capers. Dishes also have Franco-Vietnamese inspiration: a must is the delectable shrimp banh-mi and the legendary short ribs.
verdict: Fusing the flavours of France and Vietnam with an Argentine accent

name: Connaught Bar
address: Connaught, Carlos Pl, London W1K 2A, UK
history: It's not an exaggeration to say that when the Connaught Bar opened in 2008, it changed the game for hotel bars almost overnight. Before it opened its doors, five-star hotel bars were vestiges of old-world luxury – the Connaught brought that model into the 21st century, modernising both drinks and service without losing an ounce of elegance. The Connaught set the bar and hasn't fallen short since – its 16 appearances in The World's 50 Best Bars lists is unparalleled. Essentially, this Mayfair sanctuary has become the quintessential hotel bar. No small part of its magic is in the David Collins-designed Cubist décor that provides the perfect setting for the poise of Agostino Perrone, Giorgio Bargiani and their affable team. For its latest menu, Stream, the bar has taken a novel approach to seasonality – maintaining the same set of 10 drinks throughout the entire lifespan of the menu but tweaking their ingredients and reworking recipes with the changing seasons. Stem is a standout, currently made with cognac infused with argan oil, Michter's rye whiskey, Acqua Bianca, Persian cordial, new-make wine and finished with coriander bitters. The headline act is the bar's signature bespoke Dry Martini. Arriving by trolley, the serve becomes a performance as the bartender stirs the guest's spirit of choice with a house-blend of vermouths before raising the mixing glass aloft and pouring into a bitters-adorned glass from overhead. The effortlessness of the service betraying the high-level of technique at play – just like the bar itself.
verdict: Modernity meets classicism in this pinnacle of hotel drinking

name: Cordero
address: Av Principal de las Mercedes CC Tolon Fashion Mall, Las Mercedes, Caracas
history:
What makes it special? Since its 2022 opening, Cordero has been blazing a trail for its innovative approach in making one protein the protagonist of its 24 dishes: lamb. In 2023, the Caracas-based establishment picked up the American Express One To Watch Award and broke into the main Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants ranking at No.44 in 2024. This year, it rises once more to No.29.

What's in a name? Cordero means lamb in Spanish and all the sheep and lamb products – from meat to milk and cheese – are sourced from Murciano-Granadina and Assaf breeds, which are raised on partner farm Proyecto Ubre.

About the chef: Following a stint behind the burners in Europe and the Middle East, Issam Koteich returned to his native Venezuela and opened his first restaurant in the city of Mérida. He then set up shop in Dubai, where over the years, various awards were bestowed upon Latino House. On a visit to Caracas, he fell in love with Proyecto Ubre, a local sheep farm located a 30-minute drive from the Venezuelan capital, which propelled him to open Cordero.

On the menu: Grass-raised lamb is the star on each dish of the multi-course tasting menu, Koteich's delicate hand showcases it in a multitude of ways – from jerky to cheese and even lamb chops – ensuring the palate is continually surprised. One snack is ocumo (elephant ear tuber) with sheep's milk ricotta and lamb marrow and cinnamon oil, while the croqueta lamb sausage comes with roasted garlic aioli and smoked apple purée. Cuello (lamb's neck) served with silky gravy is also a must.
verdict: Celebrating grass-fed lamb in every dish

name: Corima
address: 3 Allen St. New York, NY 10002, United States
history:
The backstory: Having clocked time at nearby inventive bistro Contra (now Bar Contra) and at Spain's avant-garde Basque restaurant Martín Berasategui, Fidel Caballero opened Corima in 2024 – its name meaning 'circle of sharing' in northern Mexican culture. The intimate restaurant honours Caballero's heritage through a modern lens, drawing on inspiration from the Chihuahuan desert and the culinary traditions of Ciudad Juárez and El Paso, with nods to Asia and Europe. The menu showcases northern Mexican ingredients and techniques, reimagined with precision and creativity.

Dining design: This rustic Lower East Side-haunt blends clean lines with exposed brick, wooden accents and an overall unadorned, earthy look. A buzzy front cocktail bar and dining area leads to a rear dining room with wooden tables and a six-seat chef's counter that offers a direct view into the goings-on of the open kitchen.

Going global: Offering both a la carte dining and a 13-course tasting menu, Caballero's Mexican menu blends global touches with progressive technique. The abalone tostada is served with shiitake and finished with salted yuzu, a peashoot, ginger and sesame consommé offers a zingy palate cleansing moment, while the chef's wheat tortillas – served with recado negro butter – are made with sourdough.

On the bar: Naturally, northern Mexican agave spirits including tequila, mezcal, sotol and more, anchor the fruit-forward cocktail list, but much like the food menu, global influences weave their way in. The non-alcoholic Jamaica Cooler features hibiscus and liquorice, while the Puntilla pairs tequila reposado with red wine, cinnamon and grapefruit.
verdict: Thoroughly modern Mexican from a rising star

name: Cosme
address: Av. Tudela y Varela 162, San Isidro 15073, Peru
history:
In a nutshell: After gaining valuable haute cuisine experience in kitchens around the world, chef James Berckemeyer returned to his native Peru to unveil his first establishment in 2015. Taking a relaxed approach to cooking, Cosme serves up a short but diverse menu, capturing the aromas and flavours of Peruvian home cooking.

About the chef: After graduating from culinary school Le Cordon Bleu Peru in 2001, then taking a culinary master's degree in Italy, Berckemeyer's first job was at Astrid y Gastón, Lima's pioneering fine-dining restaurant. He then clocked up experience cooking at Spanish legends El Celler de Can Roca and Arzak as well as in other European kitchens. When opening Cosme, Berckemeyer decided to take a casual approach to cooking and has spent the last ten years mastering the art of Peruvian comfort food. The effort has certainly paid off: Cosme is this year's winner of the Highest Climber Award.

A lot of bottle: When you enter the restaurant, located in the Peruvian capital's upscale San Isidro district, the first thing to catch your eye is the ceiling. Berckemeyer commissioned an artistic and sustainable installation made of 5,000 recycled plastic bottles, while the back wall is decorated with 13,345 bottle caps.

On the menu: Order a la carte or put yourself in Berckemeyer's capable hands and savour the chef's choice nine-course tasting menu. A la carte highlights include grilled Angus sweetbreads with corn, yellow chili pepper and wine sauce; corn tacos with bbq ribs, beans, avocado and cabbage; and cau cau (a traditional Peruvian stew) with prawns, scallops and potato.
verdict: A contemporary approach to Peruvian comfort food
chefName: James Berckemeyer returned to his native Peru to unveil his first establishment in

name: Cosmo Pony
address: Grand Hyatt Jakarta, 4th Floor, Jl. M.H. Thamrin Kav. 28-30, Jakarta, Daerah Khusus Ibukota Jakarta 10350, Indonesia
history: Cosmo Pony is the first overseas bar from Singapore's Jigger & Pony Group and their Indonesian collaborators The Union Group – a partnership that unites two of Southeast Asia's most decorated F&B names. Located within the Grand Hyatt Jakarta, the space draws inspiration from mid-century lounges with marble, brass, and warm textures. It's sophisticated but not stiff, social but not showy, and feels more like an evening salon than a hotel bar. The vibe is shaped by General Manager Silvio Daniele, formerly of Gibson and Jigger & Pony Singapore.
