Bar director José Luis León delivers a menu that celebrates its Mexico City roots with now-signature Limantour classics like the venerated Margarita al Pastor (tequila, orange liqueur, taco mix, lime) always on the list. You'll find Limantour's commitment to exalting its Mexican roots and its keen sense of humour omnipresent, including in the most recent drinks list El Menú que nadie nunca pidió (The Menu You Didn't Know You Needed), which focuses on what the bar calls forgotten misfits and guilty-pleasure sips you swore you'd never revisit instead of "chasing the Holy Grail of mixology". Think tequila sunrise, sex on the beach and banana daiquiris made simply and made well. As recognition of its permanent presence on North America's 50 Best Bars list as one of Mexico's finest, Licorería Limantour was awarded the Rémy Martin Legend of the List Award in 2025.

name: Lido 84
address: Corso Zanardelli 196, 25083, Gardone Riviera (BS), Italy
history:
What makes it special? Lido 84 serves quintessentially Italian food – just not as you know it. Years of research into local ingredients, old recipe books and international cooking techniques, combined with a penchant for art in all its forms and a deep-held belief in putting the customer first, have turned Riccardo and Giancarlo Camanini’s restaurant into a masterpiece.

A view to remember: The restaurant is perched over Lake Garda in a charming old building that was once a lido – in Italy, a beach club for vacationing families. The Camaninis bought the building in 2013 and transformed it into a restaurant, with the dining room now dotted with eclectic pieces of art. When the weather is good, tables are set up on the terrace outside, directly overlooking the lake’s tranquil waters, with Giancarlo as the warm host ensuring each client feels looked after.

The signature dish? Riccardo, the chef half of the duo, is known for constantly adding new dishes to the tasting and a la carte menus based on his latest findings. However, a few recipes have gained semi-permanent status, the most famous of which is the Rigatoni Cacio e Pepe en Vessie. An almost poetic ode to craftmanship and the highest-quality products, it sees the pasta cooked in a pig’s bladder in a process that must be assessed blindly, touching the bladder to judge whether it may be ready. The bladders’ juices, gently shaken by specialist cooks, form an emulsion with the cheese, lending perfect creaminess to the final dish.

Swift rise: Lido 84 made its mark on the global culinary scene in 2019, when it was chosen to receive the One To Watch Award. It then debuted on The World’s 50 Best Restaurants list in 2021 at No.15, picking up the Highest New Entry Award. In 2025, it’s in the No.16 position.
verdict: Joyfully breaking the rules of Italian cuisine

name: Line
address: Agathodemonos 37, Orestou 1, 118 53 Athens, Greece
history: A short ride from Athens' city centre is a former art gallery that, with its ceiling-height and Mediterranean-industrial aesthetic, could just as easily pass as a mechanic's shop as it could one of the world's most forward-thinking bars. This is Line, Athens' most ambitious bartending project. Created by Dimitris Dafopoulos alongside Athens bartending royalty in Vasilis Kyritsis and Nikos Bakoulis, whose debut bar The Clumsies has been a regular in The World's 50 Best Bars ranking, Line is a cocktail bar with a conscience. The focus here is a circular economy – the bar ferments its own fruit wines, beers and sourdough breads on-site with waste from the production finding its way into to a list of classics-inspired cocktails. It's the fruit wines (called Why-ins), that are at the centre of the bar's drinks offering. Made in-house by the team, in collaboration with acclaimed Greek winemaker Thanos Georgilas, they are divided into two categories. Classic Why-ins take inspiration from traditional grape wine production, using nothing but fruit, yeast and time. The Fancy Why-ins take more influence from the Line team's bartending roots – here the wines are augmented with flavoured distillates, aromas or fresh fruits. The cocktail list doesn't disappoint either. Kyritsis and Bakoulis have built a reputation on their deep understanding of flavour and for an example at Line, look no further than the Delusional Margarita. A twist on the classic serve that combines reposado tequila with mustard, ketchup, potato water and spices, and somehow manages to be utterly delicious.
verdict: An airy former gallery bar with a farm to glass ethos

name: Ling Long
address: No.2 Zhong Shan Dong Yi Rd Huang Pu District Shanghai, 200002 China
history:
Fun, yet formal: There are white tablecloths, avant-garde Chinese art-decorated walls in crimson and onyx hues and gloved service, yet Ling Long diverges from the modern Chinese fine dining trend in that the meal follows a whimsical path, leading guests further down the (white) rabbit hole. The level of tongue-in-cheek eccentricities build throughout, resulting in each course more imaginative than the last.

Grounded in umami: The ever-changing set menu orbits around the concept of xian (umami). The aim is to re-create a cross-China journey over eight courses, pulling inspiration from regional tastes and their unique terroirs.

What's on a plate? A cohesively designed experience that is at once nostalgic and ingenious, the menu weaves through inventive dishes such as ultra-crisp fried tung feng chicken, served with preserved vegetables, or the signature fish maw with parmesan, where the bouncy maw arrives in a complex broth, topped with a crisp and umami-rich cheese wafer. Meals finish with a showcase of Chinese honey through the Bees and Butterflies Playing in Spring course, made with a combination of fruity varietals and fermented sour honey from numerous local bee species.

Who's the chef? Humble and soft-spoken, executive chef and partner Jason Liu may only be in his early thirties, but his culinary experience belies his relative youth. At 16, he began his career at Grand Hyatt Taipei, working up the ranks while simultaneously attending Taiwan Kai-Ping Culinary School. He cut his teeth at Taipei's Café Bellini Dunnan, Paris 1930 and at Bistro 3 as the chef and owner, before opening Ling Long Beijing in 2019 and then Ling Long Shanghai in March 2023.
verdict: Creative exploration of regional Chinese terroir in stylish surrounds
chefName: and partner Jason Liu may only be in his early thirties

name: Locale Firenze
address: Via delle Seggiole, 12r, 50122 Florence, Italy
history: Locale Firenze, housed within the historic Concini Palace, offers a unique blend of centuries-old Tuscan grandeur and cutting-edge mixology. The building, which dates back to the 1200s with elaborate Renaissance features added in the 1500s, was once the residence of a powerful diplomat during the time of the Medici family. Today, this historical backdrop is the setting for spectacular gastronomic experiences. The innovative cocktail programme is led by bar manager Fabio Fanni, who has positioned the bar as one of the leading venues in Italy. Fanni and his team use modernist techniques, traditional methods and laboratory technology to craft a low-waste menu that is both surprising and sustainable. Their approach is to reimagine classic flavours with a forward-thinking twist, often drawing inspiration from seasonal produce. The menu, which is short and to the point, covers a wide array of preferences. It's based on recognisable formulas given the Locale touch: the Foglia is a gin sour with herbaceous notes of mint, basil and hemp, while the Manhattan calls for marsala and a powerful combo of kombucha and cold-brew coffee. Culinary influences are never far away either, with the very Italian association of butter and sage playing a star role in the Green Fizz. Locale, after all, is also an award-winning restaurant. Since September, the dialogue between the kitchen and the bar lab has been strengthened with the launch of a unique pairing experience in Labo-ttega, the bar's lab, located in the spectacular, vaulted medieval basement.
verdict: Low-waste classics with a twist in a grand setting

name: Locavore NXT
address: Jl. A.A. Gede Rai Gang Pura Panti Bija, Lodtunduh, Kecamatan Ubud, Kabupaten Gianyar, Bali 80571, Ubud, Indonesia, Bali
history:
Ray Adriansyah and Eelke Plaismeijer

The science of sustainability: The next-generation incarnation of the original Locavore – once one of Bali's most celebrated fine-dining destinations – Locavore NXT may have only opened in late 2023, but it's made quite an impression on Asia's discerning dining community. A Willy Wonka-style wonder factory spanning a rooftop food forest, a fermentation lab and an underground mushroom chamber, it's a powerhouse of eco-conscious, plant-based cuisine. The awards are already rolling in: in 2025, it clinched the Sustainable Restaurant Award at Asia's 50 Best Restaurants ceremony, sponsored by Hibiki.

Nature-driven cuisine: Masterminded by Dutch-Indonesian duo Eelke Plasmeijer and Ray Adriansyah, the restaurant's immersive multi-course tasting menu, which often includes as many as 20 courses, spotlights Indonesia's extraordinary biodiversity alongside contemporary cooking techniques, with seasonal, hyper-local and foraged ingredients taking centre stage. The result of constant experimentation and refinement, each dish is meticulously crafted to look as captivating as it tastes.

Bringing the outside in: All exposed concrete and modular forms – but with an intricately carved Balinese door to usher you in – Locavore NXT's tropical brutalist shell is pierced by large windows that allow light, air and rice-field vistas to permeate the dining space. With nature woven into every detail, eating here is an incredibly grounding experience, linking guests and the environment in a way that almost feels spiritual.

Memorable courses from menus gone by: Mud crab paired with rich bonito garum, aromatic banana blossom in white curry, cassava noodles with sea urchin and zero-waste dashi, and raw lamb served with a selection of fermented, pickled and fruity condiments.
verdict: A pioneer of cutting-edge environmental gastronomy

name: Logy
address: 1F, No.39, Lane 258, Ruiguang Road., Neihu District, Taipei, Taiwan
history:
The story behind the name: The suffix 'logy' has many meanings in different languages. In Greek, it's attached to scientific disciplines like meteorology and geology; in Latin, to spoken word or discourse such as eulogy. In Japanese, it translates to alley, which is where the restaurant sits both geographically and culturally: at a cross section of culinary influences borrowed from across Asia served up in the alleyways of Taipei.

Strong connections: Logy is a cousin of Asia's 50 Best Restaurants mainstay Florilège, which secured the No.31 spot on the 2026 list. Logy celebrates local Taiwanese ingredients while remaining true to the roots of its Tokyo relative.

Who's the chef? Ryogo Tahara is at the helm of Logy. Born in Hokkaido, he got his start at an Italian restaurant in Sapporo before leaving for Italy in 2010 to train in some of the country's most lauded kitchens, including La Ciau del Tornavento. Tahara returned to Japan in 2015 as sous-chef at Florilège under Hiroyasu Kawate before moving to Taipei for the launch of Logy in 2018.

And the food? Expect complex, refined dishes that represent all corners of the Asian continent, such as the deceptively simple course of yellowtail, daikon radish and bergamot or the mugwort mousse with pumpkin seeds, served inside a carved stone.
verdict: Plating up the Taiwanese larder with pinpoint Japanese precision
chefName: at Florilège under Hiroyasu Kawate before moving to Taipei for the launch of Logy in

name: Máximo
address: Av. Álvaro Obregón 65 Bis, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México, CDMX, Mexico
history:
The backstory: Mexican-born chef Eduardo 'Lalo' Garcia began his culinary career washing dishes in Atlanta, Georgia. After a chef position opened up at a small restaurant, his kitchen journey rapidly spiralled, with Garcia going on to work across the city at Brasserie Le Coze under the leadership of Eric Ripert. A few years on, a misstep saw him deported to Mexico, where he met Gaby López while working under Enrique Olvera at Pujol. The couple quickly found themselves joined at the hip with a shared vision: to build a new frontier of Mexican gastronomy grounded in local, indigenous ingredients and hyper-seasonality. In 2011, their dream was realised as Máximo opened its doors to the people of Mexico City.

The philosophy: Mexican cuisine has always been deeply rooted in nature and tradition, and creating a kitchen guided by the seasons that worked in symbiosis with local producers required dedication. To hone their vision, Garcia and López made their way through the outskirts of Mexico City, meeting growers and identifying places where they could source and harvest their own ingredients. The result is a delightful fusion of refined French technique with locally sourced ingredients, Mexican elements and methodologies.

What's on the menu? Dishes at Máximo are a constant source of surprise, offering unexpected flavour combinations that reflect Garcia's creative vision. Think wagyu cross beef rib-eye with mole, porcini mushroom and poblano pepper soup, and citrus sorbet with prickly pear granita. Dishes can be experienced individually or as part of a full tasting menu experience.

A restaurant collective: Over the years, Garcia and López have built a dedicated team across multiple venues, nurturing the talents of young chefs and service professionals in Mexico. Today, the duo oversees 11 restaurants, setting an inspiring example around community and teamwork in Mexico's burgeoning restaurant scene.
verdict: Contemporary Mexican gastronomy from a powerhouse couple
chefName: Eduardo 'Lalo' Garcia began his culinary career washing dishes in Atlanta

name: Maido
address: San Martín 399, Miraflores, Lima, Peru
history:
Welcome! The elegant restaurant led by chef Mitsuharu ‘Micha’ Tsumura takes its name from a Japanese phrase used to greet people: maido. It’s the first thing diners will hear from Tsumura’s team when stepping into the space.

Japan meets Peru: The chef-restaurateur’s ancestors landed in Peru from Japan in 1889 and Tsumura was born in Lima. He then completed a culinary arts programme in the US and made the trip to Osaka to specialise in Japanese cuisine. In 2009, he opened Maido in Lima, where he combines Japanese techniques and Peruvian ingredients into Nikkei cuisine. Some 15 years later, the menu may have diversified, but the philosophy remains the same: serving the best local ingredients changing every season.

The Maido experience: The tasting menu features more than 10 courses and may include delicacies like The Triple, a combination of avocado, eggs, tomato and chashu (braised pork belly); caracoles al sillao (soy sauce), sea snails, with yellow chilli foam and Nikkei sauce; squid ramen with Amazonian chorizo, duck dumpling, nigiri and more. An a la carte menu is also available, filled with seasonal products and a catch of the day. In 2024, the restaurant celebrated 15 years with a Maido Experience menu which celebrated the past, present and future.

Into the Amazon: Maido increasingly references ingredients and traditions from the vast Peruvian Amazon, with courses like the miniature jaune parcel of pork jowl and palm heart that nods to a rainforest staple, snacks of ‘ham’ made from sustainable paiche fish, and yucca served with Amazonian beans.

More accolades: Aside from Maido winning the title of Latin America’s Best Restaurant four times since joining the list in 2013, plus achieving the title of The Best Restaurant in South America 2024, beloved cook Tsumura was voted by his peers as the winner of the Estrella Damm Chefs’ Choice Award 2024. This year, Maido has reached the pinnacle and has been named The World’s Best Restaurant 2025, sponsored by S.Pellegrino & Acqua Panna.
verdict: Amazonian-influenced degustation from the world’s premier Nikkei chef

name: Maison Premiere
address: 298 Bedford Ave, Brooklyn, NY 11249, United States
history: Maison Premiere offers an immersive trip into New Orleans' gilded age with a distinctly New York twist. The Brooklyn bar space evokes the Crescent City with ceiling fans, a large marble bar and a replica of a historic Bourbon Street absinthe fountain. The menu sticks to the theme featuring interpretations of NOLA classics like the New Orleans Buck (rum, ginger, cinnamon, lime) as well as many of the city's adopted Caribbean classics. Appropriately, Maison Premiere also touts one of New York's finest oyster menus along with a good selection of crudo and caviar. Plus, the absinthe programme is indisputably the city's finest – replete with vintage silver service. The impressive drinks list is bolstered by excellent service and presentation. Sharply dressed staff are always on hand to provide even the most obscure fact. Some drinks are presented tableside, such as the Old King Cole Martini, an elevation of gin, vermouth and orange bitters (caviar optional) transformed into grand theatre. Prime seats for the performance are located in Maison Premiere's picturesque garden space. Evocative of a French Quarter Courtyard, one will find a piece of the Big Easy in the Big Apple.
verdict: Maison Premiere offers an immersive trip into New Orleans' gilded age with a distinctly New York twist. The Brooklyn bar space evokes the Crescent City with ceiling fans, a large marble bar and a replica of a historic Bourbon Street absinthe fountain. The menu sticks to the theme featuring interpretations of NOLA classics like the New Orleans Buck (rum, ginger, cinnamon, lime) as well as many of the city's adopted Caribbean classics. Appropriately, Maison Premiere also touts one of New York's finest oyster menus along with a good selection of crudo and caviar. Plus, the absinthe programme is indisputably the city's finest – replete with vintage silver service. The impressive drinks list is bolstered by excellent service and presentation. Sharply dressed staff are always on hand to provide even the most obscure fact. Some drinks are presented tableside, such as the Old King Cole Martini, an elevation of gin, vermouth and orange bitters (caviar optional) transformed into grand theatre. Prime seats for the performance are located in Maison Premiere's picturesque garden space. Evocative of a French Quarter Courtyard, one will find a piece of the Big Easy in the Big Apple.

name: Maito
address: C. 50, Panamá, Provincia de Panamá, Panama
history:
What's the story? Mario Castrellón was among the earliest chefs to pioneer the vision of a new Panamanian contemporary cuisine. The first restaurant from Central America to appear in Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants in 2016, Maito is a meeting point for the diversity of cultures, flavours and ingredients of Panama. Its influence in the region is growing so fast that in 2022 it won the Highest Climber Award and this year it maintains its title of The Best Restaurant in Panama.

Food influences: At Maito, diners can enjoy the contrasts of Panamanian cuisine, taking a whirlwind tour through the Caribbean, Indigenous, Asian, Creole, Afro-Antillean and American influences present in Panama's multicultural culinary identity like nowhere else.

And the vibe? Located at the heart of Coco del Mar, the restaurant feels warm and inviting with a Caribbean flow – its design was inspired by the architecture of the Panama Canal. There's a lot of creative energy coming out of Maito, which gives it a dynamic and avant-garde atmosphere.

About the menu: Maito offers a tasting menu that can range from nine to 11 courses, alongside an à la carte featuring ingredients rarely used anywhere else in the city. This is thanks to the relationships Castrellón has built with local producers, whom he showcases at the restaurant. His love of the sea and tropical products, justified by Panama's double coastline, shines in all the dishes, including the shrimp wonton and the pesca chombasia, a fish dish celebrating the unique mix of cultural influences that are found in Panamanian cuisine.

More on the chef: Castrellón's professional background is in European cuisine, but – intuitively and without a road map – he invented his own culinary identity through interactions with producers and a love of Panamanian specialty coffee (he is a founder of coffee roaster Café Unido). He is also known for empowering young chefs and gastronomic talents across his kitchens.
verdict: Weaving Panama’s gastronomic identity into contemporary perfection

name: Manuel
address: Cra. 55 #74-125, Nte. Centro Historico, Barranquilla, Atlántico, Colombia
history:
Manuel Mendoza and Rafael Pedraza

What makes it special? The first restaurant located in this Caribbean city to rank in the extended list of Latin America's 50 Best Restaurants in 2023 at No.77, Manuel took matters up a notch to reach the main ranking in 2024 and once more in 2025. A Colombian approach with international flair sets the pace at this contemporary establishment, which serves both a la carte and an eight-course tasting menu, with particular focus on ingredients from Manuel's home Atlántico region.

What's in a name? The restaurant is named after its chef and founder, Manuel Mendoza, although he is more commonly known as Mane.

About the chef: Mendoza's parents insisted he studied business administration at university, with the young chef not completing studies in culinary arts until after graduating. However, Mendoza had long harboured a passion for gastronomy, and had been cooking traditional regional dishes such as arroz con cerdo (pork rice) with his grandma throughout his youth. His first restaurant, Cocina 33, opened 12 years ago in his home city and focuses on regional cuisine. Manuel, however, ups the ante by taking an international approach that uses mostly Colombian ingredients such as corn from Montes de María, Pacific-caught tuna and cheeses aged in Cundinamarca and Boyacá.

On the menu: One long-standing signature dish is tiradito de medregal, which has been on the menu from day one. Greater Amberjack was little appreciated in this region of Colombia, but served raw with extra virgin olive oil and ponzu, it's become a firm favourite. The crab mousse with chargrilled avocado and salsa de cazuela pays homage to local produce and is another favourite of the menu. is another favourite.
verdict: Caribbean flavours with an international approach

name: Martiny's
address: 121 E 17th Street, Gramercy Park, New York, 10003
history: Takuma Watanabe earned a devout following helming the bar programme at New York City's legendary Angel's Share. After the beloved speakeasy's original location closed in 2022, Watanabe opened Martiny's, an homage to the drinking cultures of his Tokyo birthplace as well as his Manhattan home. Occupying a carriage house in Gramercy, once owned by artist Philip Martiny, the three-storey lounge is a temple to luxury. Omotenashi, the Japanese concept of selfless hospitality, drives the experience. Guests are greeted with a warm oshibori towel and perfectly mixed drinks arrive in Kimura crystal. Martiny's eight step menu is ordered into suggested courses, akin to an omakase menu. The emphasis is on variations of Golden Age classics kicked up with premium spirits and immaculate presentation, best experienced through the ruby-hued Grand Martiny: a balance of gin, sherry, port, cognac and elderflower liqueur. When it comes to bar bites, offerings are light, but immaculate. Think wagyu tartare with XO sauce and shio kombu on fluffy milk toast, or Scotch 'Japanese' Eggs, where an ajitama soy-marinated egg comes wrapped in tsukune sausage to be dipped in a yakitori tare. Whether snacking or sipping, Martiny's delivers an opulent experience.
verdict: Takuma Watanabe earned a devout following helming the bar programme at New York City's legendary Angel's Share. After the beloved speakeasy's original location closed in 2022, Watanabe opened Martiny's, an homage to the drinking cultures of his Tokyo birthplace as well as his Manhattan home. Occupying a carriage house in Gramercy, once owned by artist Philip Martiny, the three-storey lounge is a temple to luxury. Omotenashi, the Japanese concept of selfless hospitality, drives the experience. Guests are greeted with a warm oshibori towel and perfectly mixed drinks arrive in Kimura crystal. Martiny's eight step menu is ordered into suggested courses, akin to an omakase menu. The emphasis is on variations of Golden Age classics kicked up with premium spirits and immaculate presentation, best experienced through the ruby-hued Grand Martiny: a balance of gin, sherry, port, cognac and elderflower liqueur. When it comes to bar bites, offerings are light, but immaculate. Think wagyu tartare with XO sauce and shio kombu on fluffy milk toast, or Scotch 'Japanese' Eggs, where an ajitama soy-marinated egg comes wrapped in tsukune sausage to be dipped in a yakitori tare. Whether snacking or sipping, Martiny's delivers an opulent experience.

name: Masque
address: Nit G3, Laxmi Woollen Mill Shakti Mills Lane, Off Dr. E. Moses Road Mahalaxmi, Mumbai 400011
history:
What makes it special: One of India's most forward-thinking restaurants, Masque's raison d'être is to show off the wealth of India's produce, extracting maximum flavour from local ingredients in a 10-course tasting menu served in a stylish former Mumbai textile mill. With self-taught cook and entrepreneur Aditi Dugar at the helm, Masque has won a string of accolades since opening in 2016, from the One To Watch Award in 2020 to entering Asia's 50 Best Restaurants in 2021 and achieving The Best Restaurant in India status five times in a row. In 2026, Masque was named the winner of the Art of Hospitality Award.

On the menu: With vegetarian and non-vegetarian options, Masque's seasonal degustation takes in dishes such as prickly pear with nagphani (hawthorn) and coconut malai (coconut cream), sunchoke with ghassi (a type of curry) and smoked pork with Kashmiri chilli and poha (spiced flattened rice). Other highlights are the dosa with koji and the burnt ghee with saffron and pear toast.

Changing of the guard: Varun Totlani, a Mumbai native who studied at the Culinary Academy of India in Hyderabad and the Culinary Institute of America in New York, took over the kitchen in 2022. Totlani has been with Masque since the beginning, joining as a commis chef and rising through the ranks to sous chef in 2021, and head chef in 2022, forming a great partnership with owner Dugar. With a penchant for fire, he loves the grill and is committed to showcasing India's diverse produce and techniques.

Constant innovation: In 2020, The Masque Lab opened an R&D laboratory where the team explore how ingredients age, how flavours contrast and how produce can help bridge food cultures. With 12 seats around an open kitchen, the lab doubles up as a private dining room and hosts regular experimental dinners for those who fancy tasting the future of Masque's culinary potential.
verdict: Innovative takes on Indian classics in an industrial-chic setting
chefName: and rising through the ranks to sous chef in

name: Maybe Sammy
address: 115 Harrington St, The Rocks NSW 2000, Sydney, Australia
history: When Maybe Sammy opened in 2019, it was billed as the hotel bar without a hotel around it. Inspired by the grand hotel bars of Europe, and reinterpreted and adapted for Sydney's egalitarian, irreverent culture, what Maybe Sammy did – and does to this day – is give its guests an experience with a smartly packaged combination of theatrics, attentive and fun service. Step inside its doors on an otherwise dreary stretch of street in Sydney's sandstone district The Rocks and you are enthusiastically greeted and by pink-jacketed bartenders who mix drinks theatrically, dance while they shake their tins and pour elevated takes on the cocktail canon. It's Australia's most awarded bar for the last decade, picking up accolades at home and abroad, and with good reason – the cocktails feature small touches to draw you in such as cracking through an ice cap to reveal the drink below, or serving brightly hued blue cocktails in inflatable pool toys. What its team of smart, knowledgeable bartenders and hosts ensure is that whenever you walk through those doors, you're whisked away from what's outside and you leave feeling better than you did when you walked in with a story to tell the next day to boot.
verdict: The Sydney star where every drink is a performance and the guest takes centre stage

name: Mayta
address: Av. Mariscal La Mar 1285, Miraflores 15027, Peru
history:
What’s the hype? On its 10-year anniversary in 2018, Mayta moved to new premises with a revamped interior and totally redesigned menu. Since then, its star has been ascending, entering into Latin America’s 50 Best Restaurants in 2019 and making its debut on The World’s 50 Best Restaurants list in 2022. Chef Jaime Pesaque’s refined Peruvian cuisine now draws diners from all over the globe.

On the menu: With its nine-course Mayta Experience tasting menu focusing on Peruvian ingredients, the restaurant serves dishes such as corn with chullpi (traditional Peruvian corn) and quinoa flower, or ribs with potatoes, cushuro (blue-green algae) and chincho (a Peruvian herb). Each plate is a work of art, with colourful produce front and centre, such as thinly sliced ‘ham’ made from paiche fish shaped into an intricate rose. There’s also an a la carte menu with options like Amazonian ceviche or muña (Andean mint) tart with passionfruit ice cream and white chocolate.

Preserving Peru: With great attention to sustainability, Pesaque works regularly with paiche, an Amazonian fish that needs to be eaten to prevent the destruction of other species. During the pandemic, the chef doubled down on research, working on recovering the biodiversity of the Ica (fertile desert) region of Peru with his Yachay project.

If you like this… Following the opening of Mad Burger and 500 Grados in Lima, in 2021 Pesaque launched Sapiens, showcasing open-flame techniques with vegetables, seafood, meat and grains. He also owns Suviche, Pacifico and Callao in the US, Italy and Netherlands, respectively, proving his desire to spread the joy of Peruvian cuisine knows no bounds.
verdict: Artful dishes and vibrant cocktails from a Peruvian culinary ambassador

name: Maz
address: Japan, 〒102-0094 Tokyo, Chiyoda City, Kioicho, 1-3 東京ガーデンテラス 3F
history:
Peruvian biodiversity: Maz stands at the forefront of culinary innovation, showcasing the rich biodiversity of Peru intertwined with the reverence Japanese culture has towards nature. This culinary project is overseen by Virgilio Martinez of Central in Lima, the first restaurant in Latin America to top The World's 50 Best Restaurants list in 2023. Inspired by research arm Mater Inicitiva's motto, afuera hay más (there is more outside), the restaurant pays homage to the Peruvian landscape and Japanese seasonality, crafting a distinct dining experience.

All about altitude: When guests arrive at Maz they can expect an immersive vertical expedition of Peru's various altitudes, reflecting the nation's multitude of ecosystems. The menu is presented as moments of a travel experience, with guests moving through courses with evocative names like Ocean Haze and Woods in the Highland.

Menu mainstays: On the restaurant's menu since opening night, a Peruvian traditional method for roasting potatoes underground – huatia – is adapted to incorporate Japanese produce, such as yurine (lotus root), takenoko (bamboo shoots) and sansai (wild vegetables). This method not only highlights the inherent flavours of the temporal flora but also pays homage to ancient cooking practices.

Collecting accolades: Since its opening, Maz has quickly garnered a roster of awards, debuting on the extended list of Asia's 50 Best Restaurants 2024 and earning two Michelin stars in its inaugural year. Chef Santiago Fernández, who leads the kitchen, has also been honoured with a spot in Forbes' 30 Under 30 Japan 2023, further solidifying Maz's reputation as a leader in the culinary landscape.
verdict: Combining the Peruvian landscape with Japanese seasonality in a celebration of biodiversity

name: Mecenas
address: Av. de la Paz 2133, Col Americana, Americana, 44150 Guadalajara, Jal., Mexico
history: Homemade is the name of the game at this Guadalajara bar, led by José Luis Hinostroza of Arca, Tulum, fame and head bartender Arturo Santos. Named after Roman arts patron Gaius Cilnius Maecenas, the bar draws inspiration from the arts, culture and the beauty of craft. Guided by that north star, everything is created in-house. Liqueurs at Mecenas honour the region's cane alcohol tradition, using 96% neutral cane alcohol as the base for liquids infused with aromatic botanicals and fresh fruit, such as lavender cream liquor and limoncello, while Guadalajara-based distillery Satvrnal is in charge of producing artisanal, mono-botanical spirits, featuring profiles like lemongrass and chamomile. Fortified wines, made in partnership with Discográfica winery in Valle de Guadalupe, include white and red vermouth, as well as a marigold-infused vin d'orange. Set in a reimagined garage in the heart of the city's hip Colonia Americana, the bar showcases the work of local creators and artists in a setting that is as mystical as it is sexy, with high ceilings, moody lighting and candles bringing a sense of intimacy. The absolute star of the space is the stunning rainbow of colour-coded bottles lining the bar, each hiding a unique house creation. On the menu, the beauty of each cocktail lies in its simplicity, with standouts like the Jardín de Giverny, a floral, fizzy combo of lavender liqueur, bay leaf spirit and sparkling water, and the Monje, prepared with pineapple eau de vie, lemongrass and green chartreuse. Perfect pairings come in the form of street snack-inspired bar bites, like bluefin tuna tostadas and a fried chicken sandwich with pickled charred chilies.
verdict: New Entry

name: Meet the Bund
address: 56th Floor, West Tower, Raffles City The Bund, Hongkou District, Shanghai, China
history:
Conventional and contemporary: In both venue aesthetic and menu design, Meet the Bund's concept homes in on the idea of upholding tradition while striving for innovation. This translates to a modern interpretation of customary Fujian elements – be it anything from signature plates rooted in archetypal regional dishes to brick red decor representing the indigenous Minnan houses of southern Fujian juxtaposed against a sprawling view across Shanghai's iconic skyline.

Why Fujianese cuisine? As one of China's eight ancient culinary traditions, Fujian cuisine focuses on emphasising rather than disguising the bona fide flavours of key proteins and produce. Famed for being simultaneously light yet layered, Meet the Bund aims to propel this coastal region's cultural heritage onto the world stage.

Sustainability as a core tenet: The venue's signature dish – Buddha Jumps Over the Wall – embodies the restaurant's ethos of developing sustainably by replacing the outdated use of shark's fin with a collagen-rich medley of fish maw and Australian sea cucumber to achieve the same coveted result: a soup as clear as tea yet as slickly viscous as the original.

Time for tea: One of the set menus on offer pairs courses with teas including jasmine flower tea, Wudong honey orchid dan cong, traditional smoked lapsang souchong, Jingmai Mountain ancient tree pu-erh and premium Datian beauty.

Chef stats: Fujian-native head chef Chen Zhiping began his culinary career by studying under master chef Wu Rong. He later joined the prestigious Yanyu kitchen team in Xiamen, the only Fujianese restaurant group to receive a Michelin star in China. Then, in 2019, he helped open Meet the Bund, the first Fujianese fine-dining restaurant in Shanghai.
verdict: Rediscovering the roots of Fujianese cuisine

name: Mercado 24
address: Via 5 2-24, Cdad. de Guatemala, Guatemala
history:
Short history: Located in the trendy Cuatro Grados Norte district of Guatemala City, Mercado 24 takes a fresh approach when it comes to cooking. Inspired by the diverse bounty found in the Central American capital's daily markets, the four-strong kitchen team at this casual dining spot depends totally on the availability of that day's produce – then cooks it accordingly.

Chef CV: After undertaking culinary training in Mérida, Pablo Díaz racked up a heap of experience as head chef of Manzanilla in Ensenada, Baja California. Returning to his home country in 2014, he opened Mercado 24 a year later. Díaz also runs casual temple to the Guatemalan tostada, Dora la Tostadora, and has picked up various national accolades for Mercado 24.

What's in a name? There are 23 local markets in Guatemala City and the restaurant likes to consider itself the 24th, given the focus the kitchen places on market-fresh cuisine.

On the menu: To start, go for the fish tartare or the gua bao buns with pork and kimchi. Tempting mains include grilled prawns with a local salad, sourdough bread and leeks, or pork belly.
verdict: Taking the lead from Guatemala’s daily markets

name: Mérito
address: Av. 28 de Julio 206, Barranco, Lima, 15063, Peru
history:
Tell me all about it: Combining produce from the surrounding area with flashes and memories of his country of origin, chef Juan Luis Martínez unites the cuisines of Peru and his native Venezuela at Mérito.

What to order: Try the scallops, sanky (similar to a prickly pear) and jalapeño; fish tartare with green tomato, huacatay (black mint) and matured cheese; or white fish and cocona citrus fruit. For the perfect ending to your meal, opt for the Peruvian chocolate rock, Mérito’s signature dessert. Or if it's the right time of year, make sure to order the grilled custard apple.

A note on the chef: Martínez, who hails from Caracas and studied at La Casserole du Chef, opened Mérito in 2018. The two-floor restaurant is set in the heart of the fashionable Barranco neighbourhood and is a favourite with locals. If you can, make sure to book a space at the chef’s counter.

Topped with a tipple: To enjoy a meal with a little extra panache, browse Mérito’s bespoke cocktail selection: favourites include the Cinnamon Spice, a concoction of pisco macerated with cinnamon and apple liqueur, and the Coconut Anise, made with rum, coconut liqueur and passion fruit. But don’t forget about the wine list, which is a fabulous mix of South American low-intervention bottles and Old World gems.

Other ventures: During the pandemic, Martínez created Demo, a delivery initiative serving some of the restaurant’s most popular dishes. In late 2020, he expanded the premises: Demo is still open, now as a casual café serving coffees and baked goods.
verdict: Bringing together the flavours of Peru and Venezuela
chefName: Juan Luis Martínez unites the cuisines of Peru and his native Venezuela at Mérito.

name: Mhel
address: 276 Havelock St. Toronto, ON M6H 3B9, Canada
history:
What makes it special? Mhel is the brainchild of husband-and-wife team Ji Young-Hoon and Yi Seung-min, who envisioned a restaurant inspired by the food and drinks they enjoy at home. The couple's first restaurant is an intimate escape from the bustle of Bloorcourt where Korean-Japanese small plates using seasonal local and imported ingredients are served on ceramic dishware from Korea and Japan.

What's in a name? 'Mhel is anchovy' is inscribed above a simple fish decal on the restaurant's door – referencing the Jeju dialect spoken in Dangjin, Yi's mother's hometown. More than a staple in Korean and Japanese cuisines, anchovies carry deep personal meaning for the duo. The fish evokes memories of family and traditions, reminding them of village elders who reminisced about cleaning anchovies with their families.

On the menu: Embodying the spirit of a Japanese izakaya and Korean bar, Mhel offers a sake-dominant drinks list that's peppered with refreshing sparkling tea alternatives. Here, umami-rich small plates are built around seasonal ingredients from Ontario producers like Aldergrove and Kuramoto Farms, Linton Pasture Pork, Affinity and Oroshi Fish, plus select imports including koshihikari rice. There are staples such as wild himi buri (Japanese amberjack fish) with urui (a Japanese spring mountain vegetable), Japanese spring onion, sumiso sauce and Korean radish, satoimo (taro root) simmered in homemade dashi, and purin – a velvety Japanese custard pudding made with Sheldon Creek's cream and Tamarack Farm's maple syrup.

Quaint and intimate atmosphere: The minimal and warm 60 square metre space features a wood-lined interior and seating for 30 guests, including 12 bar stools lining its open kitchen.

Owners CVs: After honing their craft in Toronto – Ji in the kitchens of Pompette and Grey Gardens, Yi at Early Bird Coffee – the duo spent six months in Seoul, with Ji at seafood-focused Ichie and Yi at Joo Ok in the Plaza Hotel.
verdict: Intimate Japanese izakaya-meets-Korean bar hangout

name: Mil
address: Vía a Moray, Maras 08655, Peru
history:
What makes it special? Reaching Mil, which is situated in Peru's breathtaking Sacred Valley, requires a 70-minute flight from Lima to Cusco, then a 45-minute winding drive up to an elevation of 3,500 metres above sea level. It's worth the journey – the dining room enjoys once-in-a-lifetime views of the Moray agricultural ruins.

Who's behind it? Virgilio Martínez and Pía León of Central (The World's Best Restaurant 2023 and member of the Best of the Best hall of fame) oversee the concept, while chef Luis Valderrama leads the kitchen and Martínez's sister, Malena, heads up the Mater Iniciativa research project, which sources ingredients for Mil, Central and Kjolle.

On the menu: A meal at Mil consists of eight courses that explore local ingredients from ecosystems at different altitudes. From the central Andes, there are potatoes, stems, chaco clay and wild chincho (an aromatic herb); from the Andean forest, there is pork belly, avocado and lupinus legume; and from the extreme altitude there is alpaca, black quinoa and tree tomato. The menu is dominated by grains, with protein represented in lamb and llama. This is the epitome of destination dining.

A journey through the extremes: Those making the trip to Mil can book an immersion package, which includes a visit to the surrounding environment to experience local life as well as a tour of Mil and Mater, including the cacao and distillation labs.
verdict: An epic exploration of Peruvian ecosystem, matched only by the restaurant’s jaw dropping setting
chefName: Luis Valderrama leads the kitchen and Martínez's sister

name: Mimi Kakushi
address: Four Seasons Resort, 23A St, Jumeirah, Jumeirah 2, Dubai, UAE
history: Dubai has become a second home to Japanese restaurant-bars, and none own their theme more than Mimi Kakushi. Located in the Four Seasons Resort, this ode to "orient nouvelle" takes its name from the popular western hairstyle that became a hit in 1920s Osaka. A niche angle, but one that unfurls to a world of cross-cultural heritage, when American fashions first crossed the Pacific, bringing with them jazz and cocktails. That confluence of east and west is where Mimi Kakushi revels, through its décor, award-winning food and cocktails. The drinks here tend to circle back on the venue's theme, its previous menu taking the form of record covers, each song with a cocktail accompaniment – and now the inspiration is movies. The latest list is influenced by the films of 1920s actor Sessue Hayakawa, with each of 11 cocktails engagingly represented – physically and visually – on movie reels. But the cocktail everyone is still talking about is the Kori Kakushi Martini, Stankovic's spin on the martini serve that almost every hotel and restaurant bar wishes it had come up with. Made with gin infused with Japanese ume and a hint of vermouth, the dry martini is pre-batched and housed in a small bottle which is frozen in a block of ice. So if you like your martinis cold, try having one carved out of ice at your table. It's a pretty cool experience; -20°C, to be exact.
verdict: Jazz-age 1920s Osaka and ice-carved martinis await

name: Mingles
address: Seoul, Gangnam-gu, Dosan-daero 67-gil, 19
history:
Why it’s worth a visit: Since opening in April 2014 in Seoul’s buzzy Cheongdam-dong district, Mingles has impressed local and international diners with refined Korean cookery that weaves together inspiration from Hong Kong and Europe. It is the perfect place to explore unsung local ingredients, from acorn jelly to bellflower root.

In the kitchen: Korean chef Mingoo Kang trained under Martin Berasategui in San Sebastian, Spain, before stints at Nobu in Miami and The Bahamas, where he took learnings that he would later apply to Korean food back on home turf.

Man on a mission: Kang and the Mingles team are on a mission to show ‘a new way of Korean cuisine’ that is driven by in-season local fish, meat and vegetables enlivened by traditional Korean fermented sauces and vinegars. At the start of the meal, guests are presented with an abundant display of produce to fully appreciate the diversity and freshness of the ingredients being used to create the 10-course tasting menu. Standouts include hanwoo, a Korean barbecue dish using high-grade beef with Jerusalem artichoke, and the signature ‘mingling pot’, which simmers morel and cabbage ssam with beef tendon and tender abalone.

What’s the vibe? Mingles is an elegant, minimalist homage to the natural world. Think warm wood, vases of fresh flowers, succulents and intricately crafted, cream-hued ceramics, a design that Kang feels best complements his dishes.

Expansions: In the summer of 2020, Kang launched his first project outside of South Korea: Hansik Goo in Hong Kong’s Central neighbourhood. This was followed by his first European venture in 2024 with the launch of Sétopa (a portmanteau of Seoul to Paris), helmed by chef Linda Lee and created with Kang and fellow Korean chef Chang-ho Shin.
verdict: In-season, en vogue Korean with global influence
chefName: Mingoo Kang trained under Martin Berasategui in San Sebastian

name: Mírate
address: 1712 N Vermont Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90027, United States
history: Mírate, a multi-level, skylit and foliage ensconced oasis, is a refreshing addition to the Los Feliz area in Los Angeles, with beverage director Maxwell Reis's expertise in all things agave spirits front and centre. Pre-Hispanic, fermented beverages such as tepache, tejuino and nitro pulque dot the menu while the bottles behind their two bars feature a curated selection of ethically made spirits. The extensive collection includes private barrel Oaxacan rum and exclusive batches of mezcal and destilado de agave thanks to the friendships Reis has formed with producers. Variations on classic cocktails include the fan favourite margarita called El Guero, named after Mariscos El Guero in Ensenada and made with aguachile, nopales granita, coconut, avocado-washed tequila and garnished with cucumber-kelp caviar. Its canned paloma, Tu Compa, is made with house-made 'squirt,' blanco tequila, pulque, junmai ginjo sake and granada vallet but poured into a glass painted with pulque. Southern California's bounty of produce informs the drink menu by giving it a tropical slant, with fruit such as loquats, passion fruit, pineapple and guava appearing in the bulk of the cocktails. Don't miss out on its vast selection of Mexican spirits including bacanora, sotol and raicilla made with ancestral methods, which are meant to be sipped neat. The vibe at Mírate is always lively, providing an atmosphere accommodating to locals and tourists alike whether during brunch, happy hour or dinner. After entering into the list in 2024, Mírate's success story continued in 2025, jumping 34 places to earn a duo of titles as The Best Bar in West USA and winner of the Nikka Highest Climber Award 2025.
verdict: Mírate, a multi-level, skylit and foliage ensconced oasis, is a refreshing addition to the Los Feliz area in Los Angeles, with beverage director Maxwell Reis's expertise in all things agave spirits front and centre. Pre-Hispanic, fermented beverages such as tepache, tejuino and nitro pulque dot the menu while the bottles behind their two bars feature a curated selection of ethically made spirits. The extensive collection includes private barrel Oaxacan rum and exclusive batches of mezcal and destilado de agave thanks to the friendships Reis has formed with producers. Variations on classic cocktails include the fan favourite margarita called El Guero, named after Mariscos El Guero in Ensenada and made with aguachile, nopales granita, coconut, avocado-washed tequila and garnished with cucumber-kelp caviar. Its canned paloma, Tu Compa, is made with house-made 'squirt,' blanco tequila, pulque, junmai ginjo sake and granada vallet but poured into a glass painted with pulque. Southern California's bounty of produce informs the drink menu by giving it a tropical slant, with fruit such as loquats, passion fruit, pineapple and guava appearing in the bulk of the cocktails. Don't miss out on its vast selection of Mexican spirits including bacanora, sotol and raicilla made with ancestral methods, which are meant to be sipped neat. The vibe at Mírate is always lively, providing an atmosphere accommodating to locals and tourists alike whether during brunch, happy hour or dinner. After entering into the list in 2024, Mírate's success story continued in 2025, jumping 34 places to earn a duo of titles as The Best Bar in West USA and winner of the Nikka Highest Climber Award 2025.

name: Mirror Bar
address: Radisson Blu Carlton Hotel, Bratislava, Hviezdoslavovo námestie 3, 811 02 Bratislava, Slovakia
history: Within Bratislava's historic Radisson Blu Carlton Hotel is a lounge bar that has become the epicentre of the new wave of Slovakian cocktail culture. Under the stewardship of bar ambassador Stanislav Harcinik and Peter Marcina, Mirror Bar has found a confluence between timeless classicism and an embracing of innovation and theatrics that borders on science fiction. Even before a sip has been taken, stepping into the Mirror Bar lounge is a transportive experience. Forest-green walls and emerald velvet furnishings are accented by deep wooden panels and hanging plants. There are cabinets of curiosity displaying miniature bonsai trees beneath a lofted skylight, all set around the bar's centrepiece – a full-size tree created by florist Róbert Bartolen. It all combines to give the impression of the surrealist fantasy of a Victorian-era natural scientist. The setting plays on the three elements that define the Mirror Bar concept – Classy, Art, and Nature. Now on the third edition of its menu, named The Essence of Design, the cocktails at Mirror Bar eschew minimalism – serves here are theatrical articles of maximalism with imaginative glassware made in collaboration with local artisans. Try the Lantern of Infinity, a clarified rum-based cocktail with goji and rosehip, red miso butter caramel and rice milk served within a four-dimensional mirrored cube, or Lux – a gin-based cocktail with passion fruit, sherry, rue berry liqueur and prosecco served under a bonsai tree from the bar's own botanical lab.
verdict: Art, nature and bohemian elegance collide in historic Bratislava

name: MO Bar Shenzhen
address: 5001 Huanggang Road, Upper Hills, Shenzhen, China
history: Perched high above the bustling cityscape of Shenzhen on the 79th floor of the Mandarin Oriental, MO Bar Shenzhen is pouring a liquid tribute to Chinese cultural heritage. The aptly dubbed menu, Colours of China, follows the ethos of the 24 Solar Terms (节气) – a calendar divided into 24 periods used to mark the changes in micro seasons, climate, and phenology that's commonly associated with Traditional Chinese Medicine. The 16 handcrafted cocktails showcased within the menu are correspondingly split between spring, summer, winter and autumn. The menu itself is a visual and tactile experience, featuring interactive acrylic pages. Guests can take in ingredient details alongside the stories that begot them, such as the Lutinus – a savoury, tomato-forward sipper beckoning the arrival of spring with house-made tomato vodka and smoky whiskey. MO Bar's ethos extends beyond exceptional drinks; it embodies a dedication to self-reinvention and collaboration within the industry. The bar team, led by the accomplished Tiger Chang, continuously pays homage to China's five-millennia history while embracing the dynamic spirit of contemporary Shenzhen. This commitment is reflected in their seasonal menu rotations, ensuring that guests are always treated to fresh narratives with every visit.
verdict: New Entry

name: Modernhaus
history: Founded in May 2024 by Mirwansyah 'Bule' of The Union Group (the Jakarta F&B industry giant behind the acclaimed The Cocktail Club, Union, Loewy and more), Modernhaus invites guests into its stylish living room, replete with warm, low lighting, coffee tables and mixed sofa seating. In fact, it's a space more akin to entering a friend's home than a traditional bar. The name Modernhaus cleverly plays on the dual meaning of 'haus' – both a nod to the mid-century modern architectural design of the venue and a reference to 'thirst' in Bahasa Indonesia – encapsulating the essence of what this bar aims to provide. With a focus on locally sourced ingredients and a commitment to achieving zero-waste, Modernhaus embraces an ingredients-first philosophy that celebrates the rich biodiversity of Indonesia. The bar's interior is a stunning homage to modern design, with the focal point being a custom-made communal island bar counter that breaks the barrier between bartender and patron, enhancing the cosy, convivial atmosphere. The cocktail menu at Modernhaus brings nature indoors, divided into four categories that make up the anatomy of a plant – roots, fruits, leaves, and flowers – with each dram crafted to showcase local botanicals. The menu incorporates fermentation techniques – like homemade makgeolli and fruit wines that employ local berries and spirits such as sopi or arrack – and sustainable practices, including upcycled ingredients, to minimise environmental impact. Signature drinks like Pohpohan, made from refined juniper mint liqueur, champagne lactic, limeade cordial, and locally-sourced pohpohan leaves offer a refreshingly vegetal experience that is undeniably Indonesian. Here, the emphasis is on savouring the moment, enjoying expertly crafted cocktails, and feeling at home in a space designed for connection and comfort.
verdict: New Entry

name: Moebius Milano
address: Via Alfredo Cappellini, 25, 20124 Milano MI, Italy
history: Located in a former textile warehouse in central Milan, Moebius is redefining what a night out can be. Founded by Lorenzo Querci in 2019, the venue resists being categorised. Part cocktail bar, part bistro, part cultural hub, Moebius offers a succinct blend of food, design, hospitality and ambience. The aesthetic of Moebius draws inspiration from the sci-fi surrealism of Jean Giraud (Moebius), reimagined by Studio Q‑Bic. The space fuses raw iron, warm wood and natural elements, with a 700‑year‑old Andalusian olive tree spanning two floors and anchoring the space to form a striking centrepiece. At its heart is the cocktail bar, led by Giovanni Allario, which offers a seasonal menu blending culinary techniques with the craft of bartending. Unique reinterpretations like the Pesto Martini (vodka, white balsamic, house pesto), Peach Vodka Soda (vodka, peach, basil, bubbles) and the indulgent Seed Negroni (with pumpkin seeds and cream) are crowd favourites. The drinks are familiar, pared back and built to go down easy as the night picks up. The venue also houses two distinct dining areas: a casual ground-floor tapa bistro adjoining the bar, perfect for shared, globally influenced plates and Moebius Sperimentale, a fine‑dining glass-enclosed restaurant elevated on the first floor. This fusion of thoughtful design, meticulously crafted drinks, culinary excellence and warm, attentive service shapes Moebius into an intimate yet expansive cultural landmark in the city of Milan.
verdict: A Milanese hotspot that blends cocktails, culture and cuisine

name: Mon Lapin
address: 150 Rue Saint-Zotique Est, Montreal, QC H2S 1L8, Canada
history:
Marc-Olivier Frappier and Jessica Noel

Humble beginnings: Long a favourite on the Canadian fine-dining scene, Mon Lapin began life in 2018 as a 30-seat wine bar. The location, Montreal's Little Italy neighbourhood of countless trattorias, was a surprise, as the cuisine offers a sophisticated French-Italian take on local ingredients, many sourced at the city's famous Jean-Talon market a few blocks away. A hit since day one, Mon Lapin doubled in size in 2020.

Dynamic duo: Chef Marc-Olivier Frappier and sommelier Vanya Filipovic are a husband-and-wife team that understands the collaborative power that food and wine can have. Working alongside them is co-chef Jessica Noël, who joined forces with Frappier to put forward the creatively prepared small plates Mon Lapin is known for. The menu changes daily, offering dishes like honeynut squash with pig's head and camelina, salt cod tonnarelli with courgette, cantaloupe, chartreuse and tomato ice cream, and the signature croque-pétoncle (a pressed scallop sandwich).

A natural fit: Sommelier and wine importer Filipovic has assembled a winning wine list, filled not with pages of Pétrus and Cristal, but with sought-after low intervention wines from producers such as Jean-François Ganevat, Athénaïs de Béru and Giuseppe Rinaldi, as well as grower champagnes galore – wines that have won this restaurant a spot at the top of many sommeliers' must-try lists.

Open to all: Montreal's food scene is anything but pretentious, and Mon Lapin is the epitome of the casual-on-the-outside, brilliant-on-the-inside dining the city is famous for. Service may seem laid-back, yet everyone on the floor is schooled at pairing Frappier's creations with Filipovic's meticulously selected wines. Visitors to North America's largest French-speaking city will not only eat well here but can also sample wines from the burgeoning Quebec oenological scene, making for a unique experience for local cuisine-seekers.
verdict: Natural wines and creative plates with casual brilliance
chefName: Marc-Olivier Frappier and sommelier Vanya Filipovic are a husband-and-wife team that understands the collaborative power

name: Mono
address: 5/F, 18 On Lan Street Central, Hong Kong
history:
Switching sides: Within a few months of opening in late 2019, Mono quickly switched its identity from a contemporary French restaurant to a champion of modern Latin American cuisine. Born in Venezuela, with Colombian, Argentinian and Italian heritage, chef Ricardo Chaneton is one of the few cooks in Hong Kong and Mainland China delivering the fare of his home continent.

Serious chef CV: Chaneton has worked with some of the world's best, including Quique Dacosta in Spain and Mauro Colagreco at Mirazur in France (part of the Best of the Best hall of fame), where he rose to head chef. He moved to Hong Kong in 2016 to oversee French restaurant Petrus, before departing to open Mono.

What's on the menu? Inspiration for Chaneton's dishes stretches the length and breadth of the Americas. The menu turns to South America with dishes like the Andean salad with acai and cashew, before hopping up to Mexico with the seasonal taco (the current incarnation features duck foie gras, a 21-ingredient mole and coffee oil). Other dishes make the most of prized East Asian ingredients, such as the botan ebi with sofrito and coriander and the abalone with chipotle and almond.

Hot for chocolate: Forget bean to bar, Mono makes its own chocolate from scratch, using cacao pods from Ecuador. Instead of a traditional dessert, Chaneton educates diners about every step from pod to plate, with samplings of cacao pulp, nibs, chocolate paste, mousse and bonbons.

The atmosphere: Mono is upscale yet homely, with the best seats at the long kitchen counter. The rotation of timeless jazz on the sound system is astutely paired to the dining experience, with Chaneton's collection of more than 360 vinyl records – many inherited from his father – bringing warmth and intimacy to each meal. For a taste of what's in store, you can find Mono's Spotify playlist on its website.
verdict: A taste of South America in bustling Hong Kong
chefName: Ricardo Chaneton is one of the few cooks in Hong Kong and Mainland China delivering the fare of his home continent.

name: Moon Rabbit
address: 927 F. St. NW, Washington, DC 20004, United States
history:
Short history: In celebration of his Vietnamese heritage and Cajun upbringing, chef-owner Kevin Tien opened a rebooted Moon Rabbit in downtown DC in 2024. The acclaimed restaurant had been situated inside the InterContinental Hotel on DC's waterfront since 2020, but the hotel shuttered the restaurant in 2023. The surprise closure paved the way for Moon Rabbit's triumphant rebirth less than eight months later.

What's cooking? Tien's modern Vietnamese cuisine centres on seasonal ingredients prepared with precision, taking diners on a culinary journey from land to sea. Highlights include the mochi beignet and a dish made with Szechuan-spiced snapper, pepper, plantain, herbed freekeh and charred caulilini. Desserts also shine, notably the green curry sponge cake with avocado sorbet, soursop mousse, fish sauce caramel and finger lime.

Say more about the chef: Born into a military family in Galveston, Texas and raised in Lafayette, Louisiana, Tien made his name in DC with Himitsu, the 24-seat, Japanese-inspired restaurant he helped open in 2016. Further afield, Tien's culinary skills have taken him around the world. In 2023, the US Department of State recruited Tien to join its now-closed American Culinary Corps, a cadre of more than 80 American chefs who cooked at programmes and functions in the US and across the globe. Among his most impressive guests is Kamala Harris – Tien cooked for her on more than one occasion during her tenure as vice president.

Honours galore: Tien isn't the only creative force at Moon Rabbit with culinary accolades. Bar director Thi Nguyen was the winner of the 2024 Michelin Guide Washington DC's Exceptional Cocktails Award for crafting a cocktail programme that amplifies Vietnamese flavours; and pastry chef Susan Bae was the winner of the first North America's Best Pastry Chef Award in 2025.
verdict: Cutting-edge Vietnamese dishes in tune with the seasons

name: Moonrock
address: No. 13號, Alley 42, Lane 22, Chenggong Rd, North District, Tainan City, Taiwan 704
history: Located in Tainan's serene North District, Moonrock is a low-profile cocktail bar with high appeal that invites guests to escape earth's orbit, entering a lunar state of mind shadowed by artistic storytelling. The name itself embodies the venue's essence: 'Moon' evokes the mystery and romance of the night, while 'rock' signifies a laid-back, steady rhythm. Every aspect of Moonrock – from its carefully curated lighting and spatial design to the thoughtfully selected indie music – reflects the passions of its founder, A-Liao, and a community of artists and creators that support him. The bar's seasonal menu, Moon Shapes, is a testament to its refined and introspective spirit, drawing inspiration from photography via a visual and emotive narrative. Partnering with local creatives, each cocktail becomes a vessel for cultural exchange and shared imagination. Libations showcase local Taiwanese ingredients, such as prickly ash seeds, makao (mountain pepper), and native cacao, expertly crafted using experimental textural methods, like granita and espuma to elevate each drink's sensory profile. Expanding beyond the boundaries of flavour and smell, cocktails involve collaborations in design, music, light, and other artistic expression. Here, savouring a drink is just as much about emotional connection as it is taste. Moonrock's food menu complements its cocktail offerings, featuring approachable East-meets-West bar bites designed by Chef Kedy of Jaimiba. Think foie gras ravioli and fried tripe – comfort plates that enhance the drinking experience without adhering to rigid pairing rules. With a two-storey inviting layout akin to a historic Taiwanese mansion, outfitted in dark wood panelling and abundant foliage, plus a well-stocked bar spotlighting a collection of in-house made spirits, Moonrock is a haven for cocktail enthusiasts and those seeking an artistic expedition.
verdict: New Entry

name: Mosu
address: 4 Hoenamu-ro 41-gil, Yongsan District, Seoul
history:
A new phase: Mosu may only have been around since 2015, but it's been through a fair few transformations – and picked up a slew of glittering awards – since then. Founded in San Francisco, California by Korean-American chef Sung Anh, it relocated to Seoul in 2017, and a separate Hong Kong branch opened in 2022. Following that, the Seoul restaurant closed temporarily in 2024, in anticipation of one further reinvention: a move to the city's vibrant Yongsan-gu neighbourhood in 2025.

Conjuring memories: Sung Anh trained at renowned kitchens including The French Laundry and Urasawa before stepping out on his own to launch Mosu, whose name derives from the Korean word for cosmos, a flower Anh remembers from his childhood garden in Seoul. As the nostalgic title indicates, classic Korean flavours are at the centre of Mosu's menu, elevated with modern, global techniques and contemporary plating; think of it as the story of Anh's life, told through food.

What's on the menu? Dishes change with the seasons to highlight the freshest of ingredients from local producers, but signature courses include the abalone taco (flame-grilled abalone served in a crispy soyabean shell), toasted acorn noodles with butter and black truffles, and caramel ice cream with fried onion: a surprisingly complementary sweet-and-salty dessert that's creamy and crunchy in equal measure.

And the ambience? Step inside and you'll find yourself ensconced in warm tones and intimate, tranquil vibes, with professional, knowledgeable staff catering to your every need. Luxurious without being ostentatious, Mosu's minimalist interior and low lighting foster an intimate atmosphere designed to heighten the personal nature of the tasting menu experience.
verdict: From Korea to the West and back again

name: Mother
address: 874 Queen St W, Toronto, ON M6J 2V1, Canada
history: Fermentation is the heart and soul of Mother, a smart bar on a chic stretch of retail and restaurants that back on to the park in Toronto's perennially cool Trinity-Bellwoods neighbourhood. Named after the starter used to make vinegar, Mother is renowned for its wildly inventive drinks programme, based largely on funky house-made ingredients from the cellar's fermentorium and inspired by seemingly anything that captures founder and 'thirsty dreamer' Massimo Zitti's interest, from tomato soup to the movie Dune. The drinks are an important part of the draw, but crowds also flock to this dark and intimate space to bask in the candlelight, groove to an artfully curated funk soundtrack and drink in the truly impressive showmanship behind the bar. The cocktail list includes its well-established barrel-aged drinks programme, a generous number of zero-proof options, classics and the headliner section: Mirror Mirror. The layout makes it possible to trace the bar's progression over the years, from original hits like Toasted Chai Piña Colada, made with caramelised yogurt, coconut sorbet and lacto-fermented pineapple and ginger, to contemporary drinks like Fading Silhouette, a warming concoction of amaretto, kombucha, plum and black cardamom. Each creation at Mother may be utterly unique, but, together, the drinks tell a story of a bar that's deeply committed to experimentation and discovering fresh ways to raise the art of the cocktail.
verdict: Fermentation is the heart and soul of Mother, a smart bar on a chic stretch of retail and restaurants that back on to the park in Toronto's perennially cool Trinity-Bellwoods neighbourhood. Named after the starter used to make vinegar, Mother is renowned for its wildly inventive drinks programme, based largely on funky house-made ingredients from the cellar's fermentorium and inspired by seemingly anything that captures founder and 'thirsty dreamer' Massimo Zitti's interest, from tomato soup to the movie Dune. The drinks are an important part of the draw, but crowds also flock to this dark and intimate space to bask in the candlelight, groove to an artfully curated funk soundtrack and drink in the truly impressive showmanship behind the bar. The cocktail list includes its well-established barrel-aged drinks programme, a generous number of zero-proof options, classics and the headliner section: Mirror Mirror. The layout makes it possible to trace the bar's progression over the years, from original hits like Toasted Chai Piña Colada, made with caramelised yogurt, coconut sorbet and lacto-fermented pineapple and ginger, to contemporary drinks like Fading Silhouette, a warming concoction of amaretto, kombucha, plum and black cardamom. Each creation at Mother may be utterly unique, but, together, the drinks tell a story of a bar that's deeply committed to experimentation and discovering fresh ways to raise the art of the cocktail.

name: Ms. Maria & Mr. Singh
address: 2nd Floor of Gaggan Anand Restaurant, 68 Sukhumvit 31, Sukhumvit Road, Klongton-Neu, Wattana, Bangkok 10110
history:
Fairytale fusion: This irresistibly fun, intentionally gaudy restaurant tells the tale of two fictional characters: Ms. Maria and Mr. Singh, a Mexican woman and an Indian man who meet and fall in love. The moral of the story? Indian food and Mexican cuisine are a match made in comfort-food heaven, their union giving rise to a family of dishes spilling over with flavour, colour and a serious helping of joy. Pretentious dining, this is not – just bring an open mind and prepare to be swept off your feet.

Who's the storyteller? Gaggan Anand, a master of playful, boundary-pushing Indian cuisine whose flagship Bangkok restaurant, Gaggan, has topped Asia's 50 Best Restaurants list multiple times. At Ms. Maria & Mr. Singh, he collaborates with chefs Hernán Crispín Villalva and Rydo Anton to create Indian-Mexican fusion dishes that bring together distinct flavour traditions in the most surprising, soul-swelling of ways.

Let's hear some examples: Standout dishes include soft-tortilla pork vindaloo tacos amped up with pineapple and chillies, butter chicken enchiladas served with chutney, and keema quesadillas loaded with spices and cheese. The desserts are equally mouthwatering: think churros with cardamom chocolate, and tres leches cake flavoured with rose and pistachio.

What's the vibe? Just like its eccentric menu, Ms. Maria & Mr. Singh's interior is bold, brash and full of personality. Decorated in warm orange and bright blue (inspired by cultural motifs from Jodhpur and Oaxaca) and decked out with neon signs, rattan furniture and an array of arty curios, the space is quirky without being overwhelming, with upbeat music adding to the party atmosphere.
verdict: A culinary love story for the ages

name: Myoujyaku
address: Japan, 〒106-0031 Tokyo, Minato City, Nishiazabu, 3 Chome−2−34 B1F
history:
Culinary philosophy: At Myoujyaku, the culinary philosophy behind the 14-course, French-leaning omakase menu revolves around three guiding principles: harmony, flavour and purity. Chef Hidetoshi Nakamura and his team are dedicated to bringing out the innate potential of each ingredient, focusing on seasonal vegetables and seafood sourced from the rich landscapes of Edo Tokyo and beyond. This commitment to and gratitude for nature ensures that every dish is a reflection of the wilderness, waters and winds that nurture said ingredients.

Keep it simple: Found on a quiet residential street in Nishiazabu, Myoujyaku's interior – designed by Tetsu Kikima – offers a serene setting. The main dining room features an intimate nine-seat counter, allowing guests to observe the artistry of the chefs at work.

Water is life: Nakamura emphasises the importance of water, utilising Miyazu Gulf submarine spring water, setting his approach apart from conventional techniques that rely heavily on kombu and katsuo. By stripping down to the essentials, Nakamura allows the natural flavours of the bounty of Japan's landscapes to shine through, affording diners enhanced awareness of the need for environmental protection of the area. The opening course, Greetings from Myoujyaku, highlights seasonal vegetables cooked solely in seawater – a technique known as ushioni – showcasing the purity of nature's bounty. This method is echoed in the handmade soba noodles served with a dipping sauce made from the same pristine 'mother water', sourced from Kyotango.

Drinks accompaniment: Complementing the food is an impressive drinks menu curated by the team of wine and sake sommeliers, with more than 30 varieties of sake alongside carefully selected wines and teas.
verdict: Delicately handled ingredients reveal the richness of Japan’s bounty
chefName: Hidetoshi Nakamura and his team are dedicated to bringing out the innate potential of each ingredient

name: Naar
address: VPO Darwa, sub tehsil kishangarh Kasauli, District Solan Himachal Pradesh 173206
history:
Lofty ambitions: Accessed via vertiginous mountain roads high up in the Himalayan foothills, Naar is the ultimate example of destination dining: eating here is an immersive experience brought to life by soul-stirring views, endemic ingredients and expert storytelling. A labour of love, the name translates as 'Fire', representing not only chef Prateek Sadhu's burning desire to bring the traditions and flavours of Himalayan cuisine to a global audience, but also the elemental spirit behind his food.

Uncovering deep roots: Chef Sadhu worked in some of the world's most respected kitchens (Alinea, The French Laundry and Le Bernardin, to name a few) and co-founded the award-winning Masque in Mumbai before embarking on a journey of culinary discovery across India in 2022. His travels brought him to the Himalayan Belt, which encompasses his homeland, Kashmir – and it was here that, upon delving into the region's biodiversity and intricate web of food traditions, the idea for Naar was conceived.

From peak to plate: Naar's multi-course tasting menu is designed to whisk diners away on a gastronomic tour through the Himalayas, from Kashmir and Ladakh to Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh and beyond. Crafted with local produce, time-tested techniques and a dash of forward-thinking creativity, dishes are informed by the region's six distinct seasons: pre-winter, winter, spring, summer, monsoon and autumn.

What to expect: Timely, provincial creations such as Himalayan trout with 'dirty toast' (smoked or cured trout with chewy khambir bread), brined pork with a range of condiments (Himachali apples, Sikkimese bamboo shoot pickles, apricot jam and hemp seed chutney) and pine nut ice cream lavished with pine oil and fermented pine syrup.
verdict: A beacon of Himalayan food culture
chefName: Prateek Sadhu's burning desire to bring the traditions and flavours of Himalayan cuisine to a global audience

name: Nae:Um
address: 161 Telok Ayer St, Singapore 068615
history:
Heritage flavours, contemporary execution: If you speak Korean, Nae:um's mission is clear from the get-go. The name, which translates as 'a fragrance that evokes memories', reflects the philosophy behind this fine dining restaurant, helmed by South Korean chef Louis Han. Reinterpreting the nostalgic flavours of his home country via Western culinary techniques and surprising creative twists, Han crafts exquisite dishes that feel both familiar and refreshingly modern at the same time.

For example? Rustic memilmyeon (cold buckwheat noodles) reimagined as haute cuisine, dressed with white kimchi, perilla oil and cured roe sauce, or dry-aged, pan-seared turbot paired with a buttery sauce inspired by maeuntang, a spicy fish stew.

A story-driven series: Nae:um's seasonal nine-course tasting menus are framed as 'episodes', each designed to tell the tale of a place, an aspect of Korean culture, or one of Han's own personal experiences. Past themes have included 'Jeju Blossoms' (starring ingredients from Korea's Jeju Island), 'Han River Sunset' (capturing a stroll along Seoul's iconic river) and 'Front Yard Barbecue' (conjuring the excitement and aromas of alfresco family dining).

Visually captivating fare: Tucked behind a traditional shopfront on the edge of Singapore's Chinatown, Nae:um is a minimalistic sanctuary of calm, cocooned in creamy colours and warm lighting. After all, why let the decor do the talking when each ethereal plate served is a canvas of its own? An open kitchen in the corner of the intimate space also draws the eye, allowing guests to observe the artistry of Han and his brigade de cuisine from their tables.
verdict: South Korean memories manifested with cutting-edge precision
chefName: Louis Han. Reinterpreting the nostalgic flavours of his home country via Western culinary techniques and surprising crea

name: Narisawa
address: Japan, 〒107-0062 Tokyo, Minato City, Minamiaoyama, 2 Chome−6−15
history:
Local legend: Born into a family of confectioners, Yoshihiro Narisawa left home at 19 and spent eight years training in some of Europe’s most venerated kitchens, including those of the late Paul Bocuse and Joël Robuchon. In 1996, he returned home to Japan and opened La Napoule in Kanagawa Prefecture. Seven years later, he moved to a space in Tokyo’s non-touristy district of Minami Aoyama and formed Les Créations de Narisawa. After eight years of service, he renamed the restaurant simply Narisawa.

Enduring quality: Now, after two decades as one of Tokyo’s finest dining destinations, chef Yoshihiro Narisawa’s eponymous restaurant in Tokyo’s upmarket Aoyama district needs little introduction. After topping the inaugural Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants list in 2013, Narisawa has been a stalwart of the Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants ranking ever since and in 2025, is making a re-entry to The World’s 50 Best Restaurants list.

Satoyama cuisine: Chef Narisawa channels the traditional satoyama farming system of premodern Japan – flatland cultivation, husbandry of the nearby forests and hunting-foraging in the mountains. The stunning Temari dish is a recreation of a traditional toy, here made with root vegetable strips wrapped around a crab and scallop dumpling. In true omakase style, the full menu will pivot every day depending on availability of ingredients, but draws inspiration from Japanese, Chinese and French cuisines. There are vegetarian and vegan menus available on request too.

To drink: Narisawa is one of the best places in the world to appreciate the finest of Japanese winemaking, with pinot noir from Nagano, riesling from Iwate and aged Bordeaux-style blends from Yamagata. Of course, there’s sake, too.

A light retouch: In 2023, the restaurant celebrated its 20th anniversary, using the opportunity to renovate the premises with a few modern touches. While the restaurant was closed, Narisawa took to the road for an extended residency in Singapore.
verdict: Modern and sustainable Japanese cooking from a Tokyo dining mainstay

name: Native
address: 52A Amoy St, Singapore 069878
