It's no surprise really, given Demers' pastry pedigree. Craving more confections? On Fridays and Saturdays, the duo lay on an afternoon tea experience with tea pairings instead of wine.

Signature servings: The menu changes with the seasons, but there's one dish that's always on the menu: wood fire-grilled mushrooms topped with a puff-pastry arlette and a bay leaf and juniper sabayon. Besides that, past delicacies have included the likes of a savoury sheep's milk cheesecake with tomatoes, chilli and marigolds, and a dessert of warm Komuntu chocolate mousse, pistachio parfait and moist chocolate cake soaked in coffee.

What's the vibe? To allow the food to speak for itself, the small space is minimalist and modern, with the boundary between kitchen and dining room almost non-existent. As glasses chime and cutlery clinks, a soundtrack of local artists heightens Sabayon's commitment to its community; 95 per cent of the songs played are by Quebecois musicians.
verdict: The art of quiet precision
chefName: extraordinaire Patrice Demers and sommelier Marie-Josée Beaudoin at the helm

name: Saison
address: 178 Townsend St. San Francisco, CA 94107, United States
history:
Saison 2.0: From humble Mission District pop-up to world-renowned restaurant under founding chef Joshua Skenes, Saison was an early pioneer of upscale open-hearth cooking and elements like sauces made with bones and livers of animals. Since opening in 2009, it has continued to accumulate accolades. Saison is in a new era under chef-owner Richard Lee, who has been evolving this monumental restaurant with heart and warmth as it pushes steadily towards two decades in service.

Chef CV: Lee's impressive resume includes five years as sous chef at Eleven Madison Park, where he developed a deep respect for seasonality and produce. Swiftly working his way up the ranks at Saison from executive sous chef to chef de cuisine and finally executive chef.

On the menu: Chef Lee is a San Francisco native and first-generation Chinese-American who showcases northern California's superb ingredients, farmers, ranchers and fishers. He weaves in his heritage and a vivid East-meets-West, international ethos on ever-evolving, seasonal menus that heavily employ open-cooking in front of diners. Playfulness is also a strong value, exemplified in bites like duck liver pistachio stroopwafels.

Wine masters: Saison co-founder, winemaker and sommelier Mark Bright has long been a trailblazer in Burgundy wines, while a team of sommeliers and beverage and service director Molly Greene serve from a massive wine collection, select spirits and cocktails. They dive deep into Old and New World wine, as well as wines from their own Saison Winery, helmed by Bright. And, yes, they still have one of the most impressive Burgundy wine collections in the nation.
verdict: An open-hearth Bay Area legend continues to innovate

name: Salmon Guru
address: Calle de Echegaray, 21, 28014 Madrid, Spain
history: Owner Diego Cabrera is often asked to explain his bar's name. While Guru was inherited from the previous tenant, Salmon was chosen because the fish swims upstream to spawn. Cabrera wanted to create a bar that pushed boundaries under the motto, "Only dead fish go with the flow." This guiding philosophy has proved successful: Salmon Guru is now a global brand with outposts in Dubai and Milan, where the latter was named the best new bar in Italy at the Roma Bar Show. The Madrid flagship, however, remains the salmon's natural habitat. This particular kind of fish thrives in strange waters – the hallmarks of Salmon Guru are a high-energy atmosphere, loud music and very bold visuals. The main room plunges clients underwater, while the walls of the second room explode with comic book and manga-style illustrations. The last room has a louche Chinatown feel, reflecting Asia's significant influence at Salmon Guru. Unsurprisingly, the bar's inventive spirit is evident in the drinks. Many of the cocktails emerge from Guru Lab, Cabrera's nearby research and development department. The Jardín de Dos Mundos, served in a heart-shaped double vessel, represents Madrid as a meeting place of cultures. It allows you to share a pickled onion and fried quinoa dirty martini and a pisco and pesto gimlet. For more cautious patrons, the iconic Old School Funny, an Iberian negroni aged for five years in a dynamic solera, remains a must-try.
verdict: High energy bar with boundary-pushing fusion cocktails

name: Sastrería Martinez
address: Av. Mariscal La Mar 1263, Miraflores 15074, Peru
history: One of the finest speakeasy entrances out there, the experience at Sastrería Martinez starts at a tailor shop door on a nondescript Lima street. Inside, it's full theme, with a tailor, tape measure in hand, sewing machine to their side, who – assuming they like the cut of your jib (they will) – shows you to the bar door. What lies beyond is one of the greatest scene changes in the drinks world: a stunning cocktail bar with poser tables and booths, illuminated by a glowing back bar and soundtracked by a band playing Latin American classics. If he's in, debonair owner Diego Maceda, gussied up in his signature pinstripes, will take you to your table. This is his bar, his vision – to bring Lima its first speakeasy – but of course, this is a hidden bar with a real sense of place. The drinks may have a classical structure – perhaps even borrowing familiar names – but the flavours are outside of the usual bar-world ecosystem, Lima's too. It's only recently that gastronomic curiosity has seen chefs and bartenders explore beyond Peru's coastal regions to the rarer ingredients of the country's highlands and, further still, its Amazon rainforest. There's so much to try, but a pisco drink is a good start. The Huaca Pietra also combines wine, vermouth, coca leaf, passion fruit, yacon honey, limón sidra and grapefruit bitter and is off-dry and refreshing. Or for something that blends two of Peru's biomes, there's the Cochinilla with rum, red prickly pear from the desert, cocona from the Amazon, along with beetroot liqueur, sanky cordial, lime and cacao bitters.
verdict: Lima speakeasy serving classic drinks with a Peruvian accent

name: Satan's Whiskers
address: 343 Cambridge Heath Rd, London E2 9RA, UK
history: In a bar landscape populated by ever-more esoteric themes, Satan's Whiskers is the antidote, standing plainly and proudly as a cocktail bar, nothing less, nothing more. In focusing on the fundamentals of the bar experience, Kevin Armstrong's now 12-year old east London bar has become a beacon of how bars could – and to some, should – be run. So expect the lighting and hip-hop soundtrack to be perfectly pitched, no matter where you're sitting. And if that's near the bar, behold some of the best drinks-making in the industry. This is fifth-dan bartending, where speed meets accuracy. That means ice-cold cocktails delivered to your table within little more than five minutes. And because everything here is made fresh to order – not pre-batched before service as has become the modern trend – drinks can be personalised. So as much as the Satan's Manhattan is one of the best renditions around, if you want it made your way, the bartenders will do it without blinking. The daily printed cocktail list rotates through many of the bar's 900-strong list of classics, but whatever you choose, it'll be a soft landing. For something fun, the very unserious Miami Vice (a strawberry daiquiri and piña colada sharing a glass in swirled pastel-coloured perfection) is serious business here. But the drink to drink is the East 8 Hold-Up (vodka, Aperol, pineapple, lime juice) a neo-classic invented by Armstrong himself. You'll have to ask the staff to tell you the story of its name – it's not repeatable here.
verdict: In a bar landscape populated by ever-more esoteric themes, Satan's Whiskers is the antidote, standing plainly and proudly as a cocktail bar, nothing less, nothing more. In focusing on the fundamentals of the bar experience, Kevin Armstrong's now 12-year old east London bar has become a beacon of how bars could – and to some, should – be run. So expect the lighting and hip-hop soundtrack to be perfectly pitched, no matter where you're sitting. And if that's near the bar, behold some of the best drinks-making in the industry. This is fifth-dan bartending, where speed meets accuracy. That means ice-cold cocktails delivered to your table within little more than five minutes. And because everything here is made fresh to order – not pre-batched before service as has become the modern trend – drinks can be personalised. So as much as the Satan's Manhattan is one of the best renditions around, if you want it made your way, the bartenders will do it without blinking. The daily printed cocktail list rotates through many of the bar's 900-strong list of classics, but whatever you choose, it'll be a soft landing. For something fun, the very unserious Miami Vice (a strawberry daiquiri and piña colada sharing a glass in swirled pastel-coloured perfection) is serious business here. But the drink to drink is the East 8 Hold-Up (vodka, Aperol, pineapple, lime juice) a neo-classic invented by Armstrong himself. You'll have to ask the staff to tell you the story of its name – it's not repeatable here.

name: Sazenka
address: 4 Chome-7-5 Minamiazabu, Minato City, Tokyo 106-0047, Japan
history:
Common ground: Chef Tomoya Kawada cooks Chinese cuisine infused with a Japanese sensibility, rooted in the two countries' shared history and adoration of tea drinking. His elegant restaurant, located inside a former diplomat's residence in Tokyo's upmarket Minami-Azabu district, has just 12 seats, plus two private rooms.

What's in a name? 'Sa' refers to tea and is found in the word for tea ceremony, sado. The origins of tea ceremony are in deep meditation, or 'zen'. Together they form the portmanteau 'sazen'. This phrase teaches that while the process may be different, what is imagined by tea and zen is one and the same.

Story behind the fusion: Kawada fell in love with Chinese food at the age of five. Determined since then to become a chef, he worked at a Chinese restaurant in Tokyo for 10 years. In 2011, he worked at the noted Japanese restaurant Nihonryori RyuGin, where he learned the techniques and spirit of Japanese kaiseki from iconic chef Seiji Yamamoto. Two years later, he moved to the island of Taiwan to help launch RyuGin's Taipei branch, Shoun RyuGin. After returning to Japan, he opened Sazenka in February 2017.

On the menu: The seasonal tasting menu features carefully selected Japanese ingredients used to honour the traditions of Chinese cuisine. The botan ebi shrimp with Shaoxing rice wine and the clarified pheasant soup with wontons are ideal small bites, followed by the spiciness of the luxurious simmered dried abalone, or the tangy fried pork with Sichuan accents. To finish on a sweet note, the apple with osmanthus jelly is great closer.

Pairing: The food is paired with an eclectic selection of drinks, either alcoholic (including wine, sake and Shaoxing wine) or the wide selection of the finest Chinese, Taiwanese and Japanese teas.
verdict: Chinese-Japanese fusion with a focus on tea
chefName: Tomoya Kawada cooks Chinese cuisine infused with a Japanese sensibility

name: Scarfes Bar
address: Rosewood London, 252 High Holborn, London WC1V 7EN, UK
history: The scene at Scarfes is hotel bar seventh heaven. The entrance alone is one of the most arresting in hospitality as the antique revolving doors transport you from High Holborn into a swish, leader-bound lounge of bookcases, fireplaces and spherical chandeliers – in here, guests feel every bit the British aristocrat. To your left flank is an impossibly long hardwood bar, tended by bartenders bedecked in Scarfes' signature white jackets and tartan trousers. Wrapping around it are plush sofas, armchairs and a grand piano, which attracts some of the city's best musicians. Wherever you find yourself, you'll feel like you landed the best seat in the house – every spot offers something different: a cosy nook under namesake cartoonist Gerald Scarfe's artwork, a ringside stool at the bar, or nestled in among the musicians. Director of bars Andy Loudon took the reins of the place last year and he and his team have delivered their first menu. One Drawn Out Sip is inspired by Gerald Scarfe's memoirs – entitled Long Drawn Out Trip – with cocktails inspired by Scarfe's work in stage, film and political satire. Try the Retail Therapy (gin, basil, yuzu, clarified almond, champagne) or, for something you can get your teeth into, the Toothless Grin (Rémy Martin 1738, medjool dates, evaporated beetroot, citra hops). Alternatively, just peer up at the back bar to Scarfes' impressive cannon of Scotch – there aren't many better places in the world to drink one.
verdict: Gentleman’s club stylings meets fine drinking in London

name: Schmuck
address: 97 1st Ave, New York, NY 10003, United States
history: If you thought NYC couldn't possibly fit in another achingly cool cocktail bar, you'd be wrong, because here stands Schmuck. Opened in early 2025 by Moe Aljaff and Juliette Larrouy in Manhattan's East Village, Schmuck quickly became one of the most talked-about new bars in NYC. And it's not hard to see why. First there's the deliberately laid-back, house-party-style vibe, which makes every patron feel as though they are hanging out in artists' warehouse home, from the slouchy floor cushions to the grunge-chic crimson walls and eclectic art collection (spot the animal sculptures littered around the space). Secondly, there's the distinctly divided spaces to sip in: The Living Room with its lounge-style space with couches and a central cocktail bar and The Kitchen Table, a smaller room with a long communal table designed for large groups and loud conversation. But, underpinning it all is a tight drinks programme of clever takes on classic flavours with a distinctly savoury, food-driven point of view. The menu gravitates from the intriguing with the BBL Brie (tequila, lacto beets, berries, walnuts, Brie) to the mind-bending with the Piña Colada Pickleback, which manages to be both nothing like and the essence of a piña colada at the same time, and arrives in two short glasses, one of coconut fat-washed whisky and another of homemade pickled pineapple brine.
verdict: New Entry

name: Selva
address: C. Macedonio Alcalá #403-int. 6, Ruta Independencia, Centro, Oaxaca, 68000
history: If one were to distil and bottle the essence of Oaxaca, it would taste like a Selva cocktail. The bar's mid-century modern decor is offset by selva and agave plants outside and painted onto the stuccoed walls, with elements of indigenous art complementing the design. Under beverage director Alexandra Purcaru, the establishment also aims to celebrate local flora and fauna in the glass. Each item celebrates a different market, plant or community found within the region, such as the Miahuatlán (mezcal, sherry, amaro, artichoke liqueur, shiitake mushroom), inspired by the mountainous town in southern Oaxaca. Selva's food offering is worth the trip itself. Fresh ingredients and unique combinations shine on the seafood-oriented menu through dishes such as tuna tostadas with avocado, sesame vinaigrette and soy sauce, or delectable shrimp tlayudas with chintextle. The kitchen also offers a large selection of vegetarian options, including exemplary plates of coffee-roasted beetroot with cream cheese, pistachio and smoked avocado leaf oil. Regardless of one's preferences, Selva offers an oasis of genuine Oaxacan hospitality, from the beautiful, feline-filled courtyard to the team's cat-like instincts for the perfect drink.
verdict: If one were to distil and bottle the essence of Oaxaca, it would taste like a Selva cocktail. The bar's mid-century modern decor is offset by selva and agave plants outside and painted onto the stuccoed walls, with elements of indigenous art complementing the design. Under beverage director Alexandra Purcaru, the establishment also aims to celebrate local flora and fauna in the glass. Each item celebrates a different market, plant or community found within the region, such as the Miahuatlán (mezcal, sherry, amaro, artichoke liqueur, shiitake mushroom), inspired by the mountainous town in southern Oaxaca. Selva's food offering is worth the trip itself. Fresh ingredients and unique combinations shine on the seafood-oriented menu through dishes such as tuna tostadas with avocado, sesame vinaigrette and soy sauce, or delectable shrimp tlayudas with chintextle. The kitchen also offers a large selection of vegetarian options, including exemplary plates of coffee-roasted beetroot with cream cheese, pistachio and smoked avocado leaf oil. Regardless of one's preferences, Selva offers an oasis of genuine Oaxacan hospitality, from the beautiful, feline-filled courtyard to the team's cat-like instincts for the perfect drink.

name: Semma
address: 60 Greenwich Ave, New York, NY 10011, United States
history:
Defiantly south Indian: Semma is part of the Unapologetic Foods group (the team behind acclaimed restaurants Adda and Dhamaka) – and it's clear this is a place that takes the 'unapologetic' formula seriously. A haven of hyper-regional south Indian cuisine (particularly from chef Vijay Kumar's native Tamil Nadu), this sought-after spot doesn't tailor its bold, spicy dishes to meet US expectations; instead, it makes stars of lesser-known ingredients and traditional techniques rarely seen in this corner of the world.

A taste of what's on offer: Semma's full-throttle dishes come as small, medium and large plates to encourage sharing, dipping and scooping. Chef Kumar's signatures include gunpowder dosa – a crispy rice and lentil crêpe filled with spicy potato masala and served with sambar lentil stew – and mulaikattiya thaniyam, a beloved childhood snack consisting of sprouted mung beans, coconut and smoked chilli. If you're after something meltingly meaty, try the ghee-roasted lamb chops with vegetable pulao and salna, a hot and spicy gravy.

All about the atmosphere: Wood-clad and cocoon-like (a design choice inspired by the houseboats of Kerala), the dining room is small, extra-cosy even, with closely set tables and warm lighting adding to the sense of intimacy. Of course, with a conversational crowd and a constant stream of plates arriving at each table, it can get noisy – so much so that you won't notice the music playing in the background – but that's all part of the charm.

Want in? Reservations can be hard to come by. To stand the best chance of securing one, you'll need to be ready and waiting online when tables are released 15 days in advance. If you miss out, however, there's always a fallback: 12 full-service bar seats are held for walk-ins each evening.
verdict: Transportative south Indian cooking

name: Septime
address: 80, Rue de Charonne, Paris 11, France
history:
Perfectly Parisian: Septime is a super-cool restaurant on an ultra-cool street run by cool people and frequented by cool customers. The food is dictated by the seasons and served without pretention.

What dishes can I expect? One of the biggest charms of this neo-industrial bistro is its fair pricing: $85 for five courses at lunch and $135 for seven courses at dinner. Chef Bertrand Grébaut’s modern French food has been luring diners from around the world since the rustic restaurant’s debut more than a decade ago. Expect thoughtful, ingredient-driven plates with global touches, such as delicate plates of cepes and girolles with peppercorn sauce, or young leeks in a mussel and pimenton butter topped with wafer-thin slices of lardo.

More on the chef: Graphic designer-turned-chef Bertrand Grébaut, formerly of Alain Passard’s iconic Arpège, helms the kitchen, while his school friend and former economist Théo Pourriat runs the dining room and selects the wines. Plan in advance: Due to its limited number of oversubscribed seats, booking a table at Septime is not a simple task; the restaurant accepts reservations three weeks in advance, online only.

Other projects: Should you be unable to get into Septime, Grébaut and Pourriat also command a growing number of nearby restaurants: seafood-focused Clamato, natural wine bar Septime La Cave, as well as artisanal, seasonal pastry shop Tapisserie. Further afield, the duo owns a charming countryside hotel two hours’ away from Paris, called D’Une Île.

Eco credentials: Hyper-sustainable Septime was also the winner of the Sustainable Restaurant Award in 2017, and Grébaut and Pourriat recently released Septime, a book that aims to tell the everyday story of their restaurants, including inspiration, recipes and more.
verdict: Super seasonal, ultra-cool modern French cooking

name: Seroja
address: 7 Fraser Street, Rochor, Singapore, 189356
history:
The backstory: Since opening in 2022, Chef Kevin Wong's Seroja has experienced a meteoric rise, including entering the 2023 list of Asia's 50 Best Restaurants as the highest new entry. Showcasing cuisine from the Malay Archipelago, Wong uses locally sourced produce from both Singapore and Malaysia to shine a spotlight on the rich flavours of Southeast Asia.

Name and space: The 24-seat Seroja – which means Lotus Flower in the Malay language and also refers to the street, Jalan Seroja, where the chef's family home is – offers counter seats as well as tables. The former allows a full view of the kitchen team in action.

And the food? The multi-course Nusantara menu offers a selection of dishes featuring native spices and herbs, including the red seabream with sarawak white pepper gulai and lemongrass coconut broth. Chef Wong's signature is the mangrove wood-charred scallops with laksa leaf sauce paired with the in-house baked roti paung (Terengganu butter buns). Use the soft sweet buns to mop up the remaining sauce at the end. In keeping with sustainable actions (Seroja retains a Green Michelin star), all trimmings are re-imagined as elements in non-alcoholic beverages.

Rise to stardom: Wong, previously of the acclaimed Meta in Singapore, received the Michelin Young Chef Award in 2023. Cooking the cuisine of his culture has been a dream, which he has made reality in lightning speed.
verdict: Platforming the unique flavours of Malaysia and Singapore
chefName: Kevin Wong's Seroja has experienced a meteoric rise

name: Service Bar
address: 926-928 U St NW, Washington DC, 20001
history: Since 2016, thirsty patrons have made Service Bar their go-to local in The District for three great reasons: the famous fried chicken, the lively, unpretentious ambience and outstanding cocktails delivered at reasonable prices. The brainchild of seasoned DC industry vets Chad Spangler and Glendon Hartley, the no-frills, laid-back neighbourhood bar offers a refreshing alternative to the more serious (and pricey) cocktail bars the city has to offer. Easy-going drink combos with quirky names keep things light and fun while staying conspicuously delicious – from the $9 happy hour cocktails to more complex but still whimsical drinks such as the Sweater Weather (whisky, chai, apple, honey, sherry, angostura bitters) and Suffering Bastard (bourbon, gin, ginger, mint, bitters), there is an option for every mood. And for times that call for a keenly priced beer or glass of rosé, the menu has you covered on that front as well. Small groups can reserve the Snug Room for special occasions, but for everyday drinking, Service Bar is one of the most sublimely casual drinking destinations in DC.
verdict: Since 2016, thirsty patrons have made Service Bar their go-to local in The District for three great reasons: the famous fried chicken, the lively, unpretentious ambience and outstanding cocktails delivered at reasonable prices. The brainchild of seasoned DC industry vets Chad Spangler and Glendon Hartley, the no-frills, laid-back neighbourhood bar offers a refreshing alternative to the more serious (and pricey) cocktail bars the city has to offer. Easy-going drink combos with quirky names keep things light and fun while staying conspicuously delicious – from the $9 happy hour cocktails to more complex but still whimsical drinks such as the Sweater Weather (whisky, chai, apple, honey, sherry, angostura bitters) and Suffering Bastard (bourbon, gin, ginger, mint, bitters), there is an option for every mood. And for times that call for a keenly priced beer or glass of rosé, the menu has you covered on that front as well. Small groups can reserve the Snug Room for special occasions, but for everyday drinking, Service Bar is one of the most sublimely casual drinking destinations in DC.

name: Sézanne
address: 1 Chome-11-1 Marunouchi, Chiyoda City, Tokyo 100-6277, Japan
history:
Need to know: To dine at Sézanne is to experience the epitome of culinary sophistication. Opened in July 2021 on the seventh floor of the Four Seasons Hotel Tokyo at Marunouchi with British chef Daniel Calvert at the helm, it immediately wowed diners with its immersive neo-French style infused with meaningful tributes to Asia’s rich culinary cultures.

The experience: Walk through the buzzy Maronouchi Bistro – whose menu is also curated by Calvert – to reach the doors of Sézanne’s elegant dining room, designed by Hong Kong artist André Fu. Large windows afford it plenty of natural light; later, Tokyo’s skyline swoops in to provide a mesmerising nightscape. Each of the comfortable seats enjoys a view of the glass-walled kitchen, from which your culinary wonders will emerge.

Tell me about the menu: Chef Calvert’s cooking, while firmly rooted in a neo-French ethos, is enriched by his experiences across Asia. It’s in the unique combinations resulting from these encounters that his style stands out. The 12-course tasting menu features the likes of bouillabaisse with saffron from the Saga Prefecture, and fugu from the Yamaguchi Prefecture cooked on the bone with fugu shirako (pufferfish milt) and roasted kegani sauce (horsehair crab).

Team effort: Calvert – whose CV includes leading Belon in Hong Kong to No.4 in Asia’s 50 Best Restaurants, as well as manning the pass at Epicure in Paris and Per Se in New York – never misses a chance to share Sézanne’s success with his team. This doesn’t only include executive sous chef Ashley Caley, sommelier Nobuhide Otsuka and restaurant manager Vivian Chen, but also his producers, whose names are proudly presented on the menu.

For bubbles lovers: True to the town from which it borrows its name, Sézanne features an extensive list of champagnes for all budgets and tastes.
verdict: Utterly delicious journey from Europe to Asia conquers hearts and palates

name: Sikwa
address: C. 41, Los Yoses, San José, Costa Rica
history:
Pablo Bonilla and Yerling Aviles

Why should I go? Having travelled around Costa Rica to get to know the Central American country's diverse gastronomic heritage from its Indigenous communities, chef Pablo Bonilla interprets traditional ingredients and culinary techniques, as well as recipes that have been passed down through generations orally at Sikwa. The first restaurant in the country to ever be voted into the list, Sikwa is known as boundary-pushing and trailblazing in Costa Rica's culinary scene.

About the chef: Born and raised in Costa Rica, Bonilla relentlessly traversed his home country, getting closer to Indigenous peoples such as the Bribis and Cabécares to document them for his pioneering research project. The endeavour later took on a physical form in the shape of Sikwa, which opened in 2018 – the chef prefers to call it a research centre for Costa Rican gastronomic education, rather than a restaurant.

What's in a name? Sikwa means a non-Indigenous person or foreigner in Bribri, a Chibchan Indigenous language that is spoken in the Isthmo-Colombian region of Central America.

Giving back: The restaurant project operates its own circular economy, with Indigenous farmers and producers forming part of the production chain, selling their ingredients not only to Sikwa but also to other restaurants in San José.

On the menu: Kick things off withshrimp tartare (served with pico de gallo ponzu and a green plantain hashbrown) or the hen empanadas, made from purple corn and green plantain dough. For mains, there's a green curry made with the catch of the day, charcoal-grilled beef with a white bean pureé and cacao mole and a pork flank steak with green papaya kimchi and chestnut, among other tempting dishes. There's also a list of signature cocktails, such as the agua de sapo (ginger beer, rum and raw sugar cane) and the maiz dorado (corn whiskey, bone marrow, dry vermouth and a saline solution).
verdict: Preserving Costa Rica’s Indigenous culinary heritage
chefName: Pablo Bonilla interprets traditional ingredients and culinary techniques

name: SingleThread
address: 131 North St. Healdsburg, CA 95448, United States
history:
What's the story? Led by husband-and-wife team Kyle and Katina Connaughton – a chef and farmer respectively – SingleThread is a much-acclaimed destination restaurant in northern Californian wine country.

Japan meets Cali: Chef Connaughton and his team present an exquisite tasting menu with a strong Japanese influence, beginning with myriad small snacks, before moving through 10 further courses of kaiseki-style dining (like a Hokkaido scallop with a citrus kosho sauce). SingleThread, which also doubles as a five-bedroom boutique hotel, operates based on the concept of omotenashi, namely going above and beyond to anticipate guests' needs.

The farm factor: Katina runs the couple's nearby 24-acre farm, from which most of the restaurant's produce is sourced, including rare fruits and vegetables rarely seen outside Japan. It will come as little surprise that the couple spent some years living and working in Japan, as well as in the UK under Heston Blumenthal at The Fat Duck. Sustainability runs as a thread through the entire operation, from agricultural practices to food waste, community engagement to sourcing and even storytelling.

Breakfast bonus: Guests lucky enough to stay overnight also have the opportunity to sample SingleThread's unusual, but near-legendary, morning fare.

Doing the double: Having been named the One To Watch by The World's 50 Best Restaurants in 2018, less than two years after opening, SingleThread has established itself as one of the finest dining institutions in the US. In 2025, it pulled off an impressive double win, taking the Sustainable Restaurant Award and The Best Restaurant in West USA – and it retains the latter accolade for the second year running in 2026.
verdict: Super-sustainable Japanese-influenced cooking in Sonoma

name: Sip & Guzzle
address: 29 Cornelia St, New York, NY 10014, United States
history: Bipartite bar Sip & Guzzle is the latest collaboration between New York's finest Steve Schneider and Asian bar magnate Shingo Gokan. A sister bar to SG Club in Tokyo, Sip & Guzzle continues the story of the first samurai to have visited America in the 19th century, bringing to liquid life their trans-Pacific experiences. The ground floor space is Guzzle and this is where you'll find Schneider – a bartender to his core, holding court like he once did so famously at Employees Only. With its lively, tavern-like vibe, this is the place to quaff – guzzle even – Japanese beers, highballs and classically driven cocktails. The drink to drink is the Miami Vice Negroni – the traditional recipe as you know it twisted with an infusion of strawberries and coconut washed before being served over a large clear ice cube. Before you leave Guzzle, you must try the Bikini – billed as the world's thinnest sandwich and made with comté and ham, it's also one of the most delicious. Now it's time to quieten things down and head downstairs. As the name Sip suggest, Gokan's basement bar is more about slower, considered drinking. It's somewhere to get to know someone – and what they're drinking. That could be any of a raft of Japanese-accented crafted cocktails on offer, but the Tomato Tree is the stand out. It's a non-mover on the menu by popular demand, made with tomato water, dill-infused gin, shochu, mastiha, St-Germain and lemon. It's savoury, delectable, and (don't say it out loud) totally guzzle-worthy.
verdict: A bar of two halves from a Japanese-American powerhouse duo

name: Sips
address: C/ de Muntaner, 108, 08036 Barcelona, Spain
history: Opened in 2021 and named The World's Best Bar in 2023, Sips continues its reign as a top destination for cocktail innovation. Its success is the result of decades of experience: the bar is the brainchild of two legends in their fields. Since his time at Artesian, Simone Caporale has been at the helm of bars that have achieved the top spot on The World's 50 Best Bars list five times, while Marc Álvarez honed his craft for more than 10 years with the Adria brothers of El Bulli fame. The bar's island design, which deliberately eschews expectations for what a bar should look like, remains as eye-catching as ever. Drinks are a spectacle in themselves, showcasing intricate presentation and bold flavors. The Nixtamil is an experience: a red fruit reduction is licked from a small mirror, preparing the palate for a mixture of bourbon and corn and miso distillate. Sips is also well-known for its playfulness with classics: their Tommy's adds strawberry and yellow aji. Never content to stand still, the duo launched a bar within a bar called Esencia. With seating for just 14 patrons, Esencia offers a tasting menu of intricately curated 'sips,' exploring the limits of liquid creativity. This intimate, multi-sensory journey allows guests to experience the bar's most experimental creations. This latest innovation has kept the bar at the forefront of the cocktail world, ensuring it remains a must-visit for enthusiasts seeking the next level of drinking.
verdict: An immersive cocktail journey from two of the world's best

name: Smoke & Bitters
address: Pehebiya Rd, Dickwella, Matara, Hiriketiya
history: A scenic two-and-a-half-hour drive away from Sri Lanka's capital city of Colombo, tucked away within a coconut grove on the stunning Pehebiya Beach in Hiriketiya, is the island's prized possession: Smoke & Bitters, a bar and smokehouse that has put this area on the global cocktail map. It's a tropical paradise popular with locals and tourists for its easy-going vibe and great food and drink. The cocktail programme is committed to championing the region by staying focused on local spirits and ingredients, with house-made syrups and tinctures at the forefront of the menu. One of the few drinks remaining from the bar's original menu, Pamuditha Aiya is a blend of rum and arrack, with a dash of absinthe, plus some Sri Lankan narang (a sour orange-like fruit). The bar, headed by two of the island's finest, Don Ranasinghe and Lahiru Perera, is testimony to Sri Lankan hospitality and warmth. The smokehouse menu is equally impressive and makes the place a weekend destination for many. The decor is sustainable and full of island character, with coconut tree pillars holding up antique ceiling tiles and hand-drawn motifs batiked onto fabric and coated with resin to form the surface of the tables. Smoke & Bitters is the kind of place you'll want to keep coming back to for the food, the drinks, the sunsets and the smiles. In 2025, Smoke & Bitters is once again named The Best Bar in Sri Lanka, sponsored by Noam.
verdict: A scenic two-and-a-half-hour drive away from Sri Lanka's capital city of Colombo, tucked away within a coconut grove on the stunning Pehebiya Beach in Hiriketiya, is the island's prized possession: Smoke & Bitters, a bar and smokehouse that has put this area on the global cocktail map. It's a tropical paradise popular with locals and tourists for its easy-going vibe and great food and drink. The cocktail programme is committed to championing the region by staying focused on local spirits and ingredients, with house-made syrups and tinctures at the forefront of the menu. One of the few drinks remaining from the bar's original menu, Pamuditha Aiya is a blend of rum and arrack, with a dash of absinthe, plus some Sri Lankan narang (a sour orange-like fruit). The bar, headed by two of the island's finest, Don Ranasinghe and Lahiru Perera, is testimony to Sri Lankan hospitality and warmth. The smokehouse menu is equally impressive and makes the place a weekend destination for many. The decor is sustainable and full of island character, with coconut tree pillars holding up antique ceiling tiles and hand-drawn motifs batiked onto fabric and coated with resin to form the surface of the tables. Smoke & Bitters is the kind of place you'll want to keep coming back to for the food, the drinks, the sunsets and the smiles. In 2025, Smoke & Bitters is once again named The Best Bar in Sri Lanka, sponsored by Noam.

name: Smyth
address: 177 North Ada St. 101, Chicago, IL 60607, United States
history:
Chef CV: Karen Urie Shields and John Shields met working in the kitchen at what was then Chicago's most exacting restaurant: Charlie Trotter's. They were offered the opportunity to open Trotter's in Las Vegas, then, perhaps, the highest profile gig around. Instead, they moved to rural Virginia and re-imagined Town House restaurant with the use of locally grown and foraged produce, before returning to Chicago to open Smyth and The Loyalist, Smyth's more casual downstairs neighbour. The moniker is an homage to the years spent in Smyth County, Virginia, the place where they honed their culinary ethos.

Labour of love: Smyth is all about partnership: of the couple at the helm (Karen on pastry and John on savoury), but also between ingredients, instinct and technique. The warm bittersweet chocolate and maitake mushroom tart is a frequently commented on example, something diners feel surprised to find they love. But it shouldn't come as a surprise that the Shields really know what they're doing.

Farm focus: The Shields partner with farms across the US, ensuring the highest quality ingredients land on diners' plates. Marrying both consistency and creativity, dishes on the multi-course tasting menu are rooted seasonality and the flavours of the Midwest, with intriguing pairings and unique takes on ingredients all part of the experience. Expect the unexpected: trout belly doughnut with kumquat; caviar with green walnut; and a foie gras and rice pudding-based dessert.

On the list: On the drinks menu, expect plenty of small-batch natural wines from all over the world. The offering is seasonal, moving deftly between lighter-bodied reds in the summer months and more textured, weightier cuvées in cooler times.

Mover and shaker: In 2023, Smyth became one of just 14 restaurants in the US to hold three Michelin Stars, which it has retained in the years since. In a culmination of the team's myriad achievements since opening, Smyth is named The Best Restaurant in North America, sponsored by S.Pellegrino & Acqua Panna, as part of North America's 50 Best Restaurants 2026.
verdict: Inventive dining with a progressive farm-to-table approach

name: Soka
address: No.210, A cross, 1st Main Rd, 2nd stage Indiranagar, Stage 2, Domlur, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560071, India
history: Helmed by chef Sombir Choudhary — whose Goa-based venture Jamming Goat is a local favourite — and bartender Avinash Kapoli, Soka is a space that showcases the city's stories and secrets through craft, technique, and unique atmosphere. Spread across 531 sq ft, Soka is styled like a cozy izakaya, with earthy décor, rattan chairs, flickering wall lamps, and a striking installation by Goan artist Siddharth Kerkar. Here, the cocktails are the highlight, with a carefully curated, story-centric menu that elevates drinking into art form. The Mofo Don, a spicy tequila concoction with a pink hue from red cabbage and a cilantro-sprinkled rim, is a playful nod to the bar's cabbage vendor. The Cheese Cherry Pineapple offers a grown-up twist on a classic pub snack, blending rum with hints of local flavours like jasmine, coconut, and bananas with all elements sourced from local markets and prepared with sous vide, clarification, and blending. Complementing the drinks are 10 inventive "nano plates"— small bites with Indian influences, like the Soka Fried Chicken with raw mango chimichurri or the classic South Indian snack: Mutton Pepper Fry. Sombir and Kapoli's playful, imaginative touches turn familiar tastes into surprising, memorable experiences that will make you want to come back for more.
verdict: New Entry

name: Somni
address: 9045 Nemo St. West Hollywood, CA 90069, United States
history:
Awakening a sleeping giant: Reborn after the closure of its original iteration at the SLS Hotel – which shuttered during the pandemic – the new and improved Somni 2.0 ranks among North America's most exclusive and decorated restaurants. The vision of Barcelona-born chef Aitor Zabala, who earned his wings at a number of Spanish modernist eateries (including the venerable El Bulli), its name means 'dream' in Catalan. Cross the threshold into its converted-bungalow premises just off Santa Monica Avenue, and you'll immediately understand why.

An otherworldly air: From the whimsical giraffe sculpture that greets guests on arrival to the restaurant's cocoon-like secret courtyard – dimly lit, lined with trees and flecked with floating, cloud-like artworks – Somni is built on a sense of the unexpected and slightly surreal. Inside, a palette of soft whites and glossy surfaces add to the trance-like ambience, while the curved counter, which seats just 14 people, offers up-close views of the chefs' culinary sorcery. As each dish is served, a wall-mounted rainbow bull's head, a subtle Spanish motif salvaged from the original location, watches over proceedings.

Tongue-in-cheek temptations: Each of the belt-looseningly extensive tasting menu's carefully constructed courses is designed to surprise and delight; for example, caviar is served on top of a fish-shaped savoury meringue layered with nori powder and a smoked butter cream, and beef tenderloin tartare is concealed within a tempura-style fried shiso leaf crowned with purple flowers. Desserts include an eye-popping spread of jewel-like mignardises, as well as a cheese course presented under a cow-shaped cracker (how else?).

And to drink? Somewhat unusually, Somni does not offer wines by the glass. Instead, a wine, non-alcoholic or hybrid 'maridatge' pairing is de rigueur. Bottles of wine are available, but it is important to Zabala that diners embrace the 'go big or go home' attitude, as the pairings help to build the cultural dialogue between Spain and California.
verdict: A multi-sensory dream sequence

name: Sons & Daughters
address: 2875 18th St. San Francisco, CA 94110, United States
history:
A new generation: Sons & Daughters has undergone something of a transformation in recent years. Opened as a monument to New American gastronomy in 2010, it gained a Michelin star under Teague Moriarty in 2012 – and 12 years later, with British chef Harrison Cheney at the helm, it secured its second. Now a temple of New Nordic cooking, this fine-dining destination is proof that the Scandi ideals of hyper-seasonal ingredients, preservation techniques and minimalist aesthetics can stand out in a place as sun-soaked and abundant as San Francisco.

From the UK to the Bay: Cheney worked at a number of venerable culinary institutions before taking up the mantle at Sons & Daughters: he had stints at The Ledbury and The Square in London, gained experience at San Francisco's Quince and served as head chef at New-Nordic bastion Gastrologik in Stockholm before making his grand West Coast return.

Californian bounty, north European lens: Sons & Daughters' multi-course tasting menu places fresh, locally sourced seasonal produce front and centre, backed by an ingenious cast of pickles, ferments and cured and smoked ingredients. Previous creations have included the likes of cured California trout with chilled fish bone broth and herbed oil, rutabaga noodles dressed with pork fat and a buttery sabayon, and applewood-roasted quail with crisp skin and a fermented blueberry glaze.

A change of scene: Sons & Daughters doesn't just taste different these days; it looks a lot different, too. The restaurant moved from its original Nob Hill location to a larger Mission District space in 2025, and it's all the better for it. Warm, wood-panelled and welcoming, a little like a Scandinavian cabin, the modern dining area wraps around a stage-like open kitchen so you can watch the chefs at work.
verdict: Quintessentially Californian New Nordic cuisine

name: Sorn
address: 56 Soi Sukhumvit 26, Klongton Khlong Toei, Bangkok 10110, Thailand
history:
A potted history: Supaksorn 'Ice' Jongsiri, Sorn's chef-patron, grew up in close contact with his grandmother’s southern cooking. Together with his own personal vision, Chef Ice transforms the flavours and memories of his encounters with diverse southern Thai cultures into a one-of-a-kind innovative cuisine. His thought process is mind-bending and his flavouring explosive, with no compromise on chilli heat and spice.

What should I expect? Sorn is allegedly the most difficult restaurant to book in Thailand and one of the reasons it’s so sought after is its impeccable service. The front-of-house team is trained specifically to communicate the stories and memories that the chef puts into every dish. With each bite, you will be inspired by Ice’s love and passion for the south.

Don’t wait to tuck in: Every dish served at Sorn is highly temperature sensitive. It’s something the chef pays special attention to and his kitchen brigade delivers every dish at an incredible tempo – so, don’t take too long photographing them. By controlling the temperature of the dishes, Sorn delivers a sensory-driven sequence that adds a new dimension to Thai cuisine.

Standout dish: Kan Chu Piang (gems on crab stick) is Sorn’s version of Kan Chiang Pu (blue swimmer crab leg), arguably the most fought over part of the crab at any Thai dinner table. In the nation’s households, this part is reserved for the most senior or the most beloved of the family. At Sorn, you become that favourite child for a day. The dish is a marriage of the two most valued parts of the crab: crab roe and swimmer leg. The plump and juicy meat is served chilled and coated with crab roe paste inside a little granite mortar, which has a fiery yellow chilli sauce dip inside.
verdict: Genre-bending, spice-saturated southern Thai gastronomy
chefName: Ice transforms the flavours and memories of his encounters with diverse southern Thai cultures into a one-of-a-kind inno

name: Steirereck
address: Steirereck, Am Heumarkt 2A im Stadtpark A-1030 Vienna, Austria
history:
The dining room: Much like chef Heinz Reitbauer’s cuisine, the restaurant’s interior combines the historic with the uber-contemporary. The original building dates from 1904, but the striking mirrored glass extension has walls that raise up in the summer months to create semi al-fresco dining in Vienna’s green Stadtpark. The blonde wood and concrete interior is airy, stylish and sophisticated.

World-leading service: Steirereck has long been a feature at the upper end of The World’s 50 Best Restaurants list – and while that’s undoubtedly down to the chef’s vision and skill, it’s also a reflection of the outstanding and ever-evolving hospitality that is proffered, all orchestrated by doyenne of the dining room, Birgit Reitbauer. Her warmth, attention to detail and deep knowledge is reflected across the team and has created an atmosphere that attracts global gourmets as well as locals.

Austria on a plate: Chef Reitbauer, who took over the family restaurant almost 20 years ago, has forged a unique culinary identity, which is heavily research-driven, sustainable and, of course, delicious. It’s also fundamentally Austrian, with numerous unknown or long-forgotten ingredients brought to the fore, including rare breeds of meat and fish and near-extinct fruit and vegetable varieties. Herbs and vegetables are grown on the building’s rooftop alongside its own beehives. Dishes on the exquisite tasting menu (there’s also an a la carte option) include pheasant with yacon, gooseberry and kale; young artichoke with bergamot, nettle and chicken velouté; and kohlrabi with oats, apple and parsley.

Trolley action: This restaurant – and its customers – love a well-laden trolley. The bread trolley shows off the creativity of Austrian bakers, with 25 varieties on offer. There are also aperitif trolleys, a tea trolley, a cheese selection to die for, one national and one international digestif trolley and, before Christmas, one for vanillekipferl – the traditional Austrian crescent-shaped biscuit.
verdict: A history lesson in Austrian gastronomy delivered with contemporary flair

name: Sublime
address: 12 Calle 4-15, Guatemala City 01014, Guatemala
history:
What's the concept? Sublime portrays Guatemala's essence through a tasting menu conceptualised by chef Sergio Díaz with Jocelyn Degollado, an anthropologist with an understanding of how food intertwines with the country's iconic events.

The dining experience: Díaz's long experience as a corporate chef is crucial to understanding the professionality of his approach to food and hospitality. The 12-course tasting menu usually begins with dishes inspired by pre-Columbian times, passes through Spanish colonial syncretism or mestizaje, and ends with the flavours of the present, inviting diners to distil their own reflections on the future. There is also an a la carte menu worth exploring.

What's the vibe? Sublime, named The Best Restaurant in Guatemala for four years in a row, is located in a beautiful house in a high-end neighbourhood of Guatemala City and features a stylish vintage cocktail bar named Cocktail Lobby. The venue is host to different spaces, from outdoor tables to private dining rooms, with guests often able to enjoy a direct view of the semi-open kitchen. The walls are covered in contemporary art by recognised Guatemalan artists.

About the chef: Díaz is a creative, passion-driven chef with a positive attitude that builds comradeship among the local food community. Born into a family of restaurateurs in the Guatemalan highlands, he fell in love with cooking when he was young and this deep-rooted passion keeps him aiming higher and higher.

Bonus point: Sublime also includes a terrace with a casual but well-executed concept called Patio San Román, a popular brunch spot among the locals.
verdict: A thoughtful interpretation of Guatemala’s history and culinary musings on its future

name: Sühring
address: 10 Yen Akat Soi 3, Chongnonsi, Yannawa, Bangkok 10120
history:
What's it all about? Twin brothers Mathias and Thomas Sühring meld flavours, techniques and experiences accumulated in their native Germany, as well as the Netherlands, Italy and Thailand, under one modish roof. The result is a sophisticated – and at times playful – seasonal haute cuisine menu with strong German identity.

What’s the vibe? Step through the luscious front garden to a 1970s villa renovated by the twins. It’s a blissful and calming breath of fresh air in Thailand’s energetic capital. Some tables overlook the open kitchen while others enjoy the tropical landscape courtesy of the glass-ceilinged atrium.

Typical dishes: European influence is welcomed in a city known for racy spices, and strong plates on the menu include scallop with pumpkin and kelp; lobster with vanilla, persimmon and hazelnut; Kagoshima A5 wagyu with kintoki carrot and oxtail; and spaetzle egg noodles topped with Périgord truffle, inciting plenty of German culinary nostalgia (although the truffle is somewhat of an indulgent touch).

Worth noting: Sühring now forms part of the Relais & Châteaux league of top establishments, and has held on to two Michelin stars since 2018. Embrace the full experience with the wine pairing comprising glasses from the Rheingau in Germany and Kremstal in Austria, or a juice pairing for something a little bit different.
verdict: Cool and collected contemporary German cuisine in bustling Bangkok

name: Superbueno
address: 13 1st Ave., New York, NY 10003
history: Ignacio 'Nacho' Jimenez is an icon of the New York City hospitality scene and his bar, Superbueno, represents the two decades he's spent in Gotham as well as his Mexican heritage. The East Village hotspot is frequently packed with a swanky, distinctly New York crowd. However, the casual, celebratory atmosphere is decidedly Mexican – think a festive, airy vibe punctuated by primary colours and neon lights. The bartop is lit by the signature, swinging globe lights, but true illumination comes from head bartender Kip Moffitt's inventive creations. Technical mastery meets traditional Mexican ingredients here to deliver bright, delicious cocktails. Marquee creations include the Salted Plum & Tamarind Milk Punch (charanda, ayuuk, salted plum and tamarind, lojana tea, milk) and Mole Negroni (mole fat-washed mezcal, amari blend, sweet vermouth, xocolati bitters). True to the bar's inclusive philosophy, the list also includes Mexican beers and wines as well as an impressive line-up of non-alcoholic drinks. The many regular guests enjoy rotating slushies and tepache on tap, while chef Cyed Adraincem's food line-up keeps them coming back with elevated classics such as the bar's signature Birria Grilled Cheese (braised beef, Mexican cheese blend, cilantro lime mayo, cotija, lime, bolillo bread; served with consommé). The cantina's most impressive offering can't be found on any menu – it is defined by warm hospitality. Nacho can frequently be spotted welcoming guests, whether that entails pouring water or pouring shots. Coupled with the elite drinks programme, the overwhelming sense of feeling welcomes encapsulates the very best of Mexican-American hospitality.
verdict: A joyful homage to Mexican-American culture

name: Svanen
address: Karl Johans gt. 13, 0154 Oslo, Norway
history: Historians argue that the first cocktails weren't sold in bars but by apothecaries; their bitter curatives blended to remedy all manner of olde-worlde ailments. And so, the stunning scene at Oslo's Svanen – a repurposing of a mint-condition 19th-century Oslo pharmacy – speaks directly to mixology's origin story. When Yunus Yildiz first laid eyes on the not-long closed neo-classical site, the epiphany came quickly. The decorative antique mahogany, the vintage glass cabinets, marble columns, original tiled floors and Wilhelm Krogh-painted panelled ceilings... a film-maker's budget couldn't craft a more perfect pharmaceutically-themed cocktail bar. Into the medicine dressers went spirits and tucked into drawers of the apothecary cabinets went botanicals: on the counters emerged some of the finest cocktails around. The transition was seamless – this cultural heritage site preserved, in a sense, in alcohol. Yildiz and Karel Varga resisted the temptation to go too conceptual with the drinks – mercifully themeless, the cocktails stand on their own two feet. The standout is Stolen Apples, made with rum, gin, green apple juice, lapsang souchong tea, ginger and shiso. And the name Svanen? It means 'swan' and there was little choice to adopt it. This was the Swan Pharmacy, the business of which dates to 1628, moving into the Karl Johans Gate in 1896. And besides, you can't miss the decorative swans throughout the building. There's an ugly duckling too (Den Grimme Ælling), though this is Yildiz's own addition. Juxtaposing the old with the new, the basement bar has one foot in the future.
verdict: A vintage pharmacy transformed into a forward-thinking cocktail bar

name: Table by Bruno Verjus
address: 3 rue de Prague, 75012 Paris, France
history:
A chef of many hats: Bruno Verjus, Table’s 65-year-old chef-patron, doesn’t only look a little like the grinning Cheshire Cat from Alice in Wonderland, but also had many lives before opening his restaurant a decade ago: medical student, entrepreneur in China, photographer, author, radio commentator, even food critic. His motto? “Our food choices shape our world.”

What’s it like inside? Table (pronounced in the French way) features a long counter designed like a wave that creates private nooks, plus an open kitchen. You can follow every gesture, see every plating, including the Colours of the Day – a daily changing dish of the most seasonal herbs and vegetables – and witness how Verjus serves line-caught monkfish torched with bay leaves, or prepares a donburi dish with blonde lentils with pig’s ear in a crab broth.

Signature dish? Imagine a tartlet topped with melted dark chocolate from Peru mixed with caper-infused cream. Add some Ossetra caviar on top… et voila! And make sure to devour the mellow homemade madeleine, which is even better dipped in Kardamili olive oil, as suggested.

Tying it together: The undeniably warm and accessible atmosphere is the product of Verjus and his personable team, who speak flawless English and are always happy to chat about suppliers, any of their stunning wines and, of course, the food. It’s a combination that saw the restaurant achieve the rare feat of entering the top 10 on its first appearance in The World’s 50 Best Restaurants list in 2023, and maintaining in 2024 and 2025.
verdict: The hottest table in Paris from a self-taught chef

name: Tan Tan
address: R. Fradique Coutinho, 153 - Pinheiros, São Paulo - SP, 05416-010, Brazil
history: An intimate yet buzzing spot in the trendy Pinheiros district of São Paulo, Tan Tan effortlessly blurs the lines to serve up both smart cocktails and delicious Asian dishes. And, in a metropolis with the largest Japanese population outside of Japan, it sets the bar high. A half-and-half concept divided between bar and restaurant as well as Brazilian and Asian flavours conceived by chef and owner Thiago Bañares, on one side, the open kitchen showcases the chefs' precise, well-orchestrated movements, while on the other, the bartenders' workstations blend seamlessly into a beautiful wooden counter. The space is framed by a plush inviting sofa, although those after a more casual experience might prefer to take a seat streetside. The cocktail list's narrative – devised by head barman Caio Carvalhaes – is updated annually. And here comes the science bit: new for 2025 is Pour-Hibition, an inclusive concept challenging relationships with alcohol while addressing the global decline in its consumption. Tan Tan's drinks list also features non-alcoholic cocktails identified by their ABV rather than being segregated. Order the Dirty Collins and its wink towards Brazil made with white cachaça, tequila, olive and Tahiti lime and savour it to a pumping hip-hop soundtrack; other cocktails have thought-proving names such as Sinner, Hypocrisy and Libertine. As for dishes, start with oysters in ponzu sauce before moving on to a katsu sando or aka teba (fried chicken wings with homemade sweet chilli sauce.)
verdict: Half bar, half restaurant, all fun at this Brazilian-Asian hotspot

name: Tanière3
address: 7 Rue du Don-de-Dieu, Québec, QC G1K 3Z6, Canada
history:
Restaurant history: Founded in 1977, La Tanière has been pushing the boundaries of culinary excellence as a leader in contemporary wild Quebec cuisine. Having undergone ownership changes over the years, it continued to deepen its commitment of using locally harvested and foraged ingredients and implemented modern cooking techniques from the early 2000s until its closure in 2015. Relaunched under the leadership of owners Roxan Bourdelais and chef François-Emmanuel Nicol in 2019, the restaurant was renamed Tanière3 to acknowledge its new iteration. Originally calling the outskirts of Quebec City home, the lauded stalwart relocated to the historic stone vaults in Old Quebec, a Unesco World Heritage site.

Unmistakeably Quebec: Tanière3 celebrates Quebec's rich culinary heritage with a hyper-seasonal, terroir-driven tasting menu of 12-to-18 courses. From field, forest and estuary, each dish showcases the province's natural bounty. Think silky Quebec scallops crowned with wild lake sturgeon caviar from Lac-St-Pierre, or s tuna from Chaleur Bay with prickly ash pepper sauce, marinated wild blackberries and basswood foam.

Daring drinks: A culinary innovator, Tanière3 was one of the first in the province to serve non-alcoholic pairings. Curated by barman Simon Faucher, expect dandelion-like coltsfoot used as a syrup, infusion and bitters then topped with an edible 'cloud' made from the same ingredients for a welcome drink.

Dining in multiple acts: Beyond the plate, it's the hours-long, immersive journey which transforms the dining experience into a multi-sensory adventure. First, a personal code is used to enter the restaurant's unmarked entrance. Then, the evening progresses through a series of captivating spaces from the Bar Vault to the Main Dining Room or Chef's Counter, wrapping up in the Dessert Vault.

Unparalleled service: Dedicated to providing polished and attentive service, Tanière3's knowledgeable staff embody the very best of Québécois hospitality's warmth, friendliness and professionalism.
verdict: Boreal forest flavours with warm Quebecois hospitality

name: Tatiana by Kwame Onwuachi
address: 10 Lincoln Center Plaza, New York, NY 10023, United States
history:
A life-long love story: Kwame Onwuachi grew up surrounded by food. His Jamaican mother, a chef, introduced him early to the tastes of the Caribbean, while time spent with family in Nigeria rooted him in the rituals of traditional home cooking. Back in the Bronx, a mix of influences – from Italian restaurants to Chinese takeaways – further shaped his palate. At Tatiana by Kwame Onwuachi, named for his beloved older sister, he draws on all these eclectic influences to create a menu that serves as a heartfelt tribute to his youth.

What's on the menu? A selection of small and large sharing plates bring big flavours to the table. Think short rib pastrami suya (a homage to Jewish delis and West African spices), curried goat patties with green seasoning aioli and mango chutney, and an egusi-inspired tortellini dish featuring Dungeness crab, Nigerian red stew and parsley. The standout signature? Braised oxtails with Thumbelina carrots, chayote squash, rice and peas – a comfort-food classic executed with finesse.

A night at the theatre: There's a real sense of spectacle at Tatiana, from the open kitchen to the floor-to-ceiling windows that run along the restaurant's perimeter, inviting observers to take in the show – so it makes sense that it's located within Lincoln Center, a cultural complex that's also home to the New York Philharmonic, Metropolitan Opera, New York City Ballet and the prestigious Juilliard School.

Loud and proud: A fine-dining spot without the associated stiffness, Tatiana is cool, contemporary and unapologetically high-energy. Matching the unbridled joy of its dishes, there's a convivial, almost party-like atmosphere that's hard to resist, with a playlist of hip-hop and R&B wafting through the air as diners pass plates and sip cocktails.
verdict: Food to spark your memory and pull at your heartstrings

name: Tayēr + Elementary
address: 152 Old St, London EC1V 9BW, UK
history: Now in its sixth year, Tayēr + Elementary feels as fresh as the day it opened while also becoming the blueprint for progressive cocktail bars. The influence of this bar is evident in every cocktail capital the world over. From its exposed, industrial aesthetic, ingredient-led minimalism and constantly evolving drinks list, the philosophy refined by co-founders Monica Berg and Alex Kratena has become bartending scripture. In this east London hotspot, every element of the traditional cocktail bar has been forensically analysed, from design and atmosphere to service and drinks creation, and reimagined into a bar that still feels like stepping into the future, even after more that five years in operation – testament to the ability of its founders to remain a step or two ahead of the curve. As the name suggests, Tayēr + Elementary is a bar of two parts. Elementary is an industrially designed diner-style front bar. A casual, neighbourhood drinking experience where the draught cocktails form the bar's core and weekly-changing seasonal and collaborative menus are served in seconds alongside the own-label wine and beer. The bar's One Sip Martini with a blue cheese olive has become an almost pre-requisite jumping off point. Through a partition is Tayēr. Here the drinking experience meets the chef's table. Dominated by its centrepiece, an enormous hexagonally-divided bar station, the daily-changing produce-driven menu is as much a celebration of ingredients – often esoteric, always hyper-seasonal – as the myriad techniques wielded to extract their flavour.
verdict: London’s produce-driven, hyper-seasonal bar of the future

name: The Bar in Front of the Bar
address: Petraki 1, Athina 105 63, Greece
photoUrl: https://www.theworlds50best.com/bars/best-in-the-world/filestore/jpeg/The Bar in Front of the Bar-cocktail_W50BB25-website-profile.jpeg
history: There's something uniquely Athenian about The Bar in Front of the Bar. This is a city where people spend a lot of time drinking al fresco, sitting on the sidewalks listening to the buzzing music of loud sound systems – and that's exactly what you'll find here. But many other things make this place unique. The brainchild of Alexandros Tselepis, Konstantinos Theodorakopoulos and Simeon Papanikolaou, The Bar in Front of the Bar was initially a quirky pop-up that covered up work being done on a more traditional bar behind it, in short, a scaffold made interesting. Rumble in the Jungle, the bar behind the bar, is now open and operated by the same team. The Bar in Front of the Bar remains as the first layer of a unique concept. The street bar (with zero indoor seating) is instantly recognisable by its minimalist design and a large, ever-changing digital sign that delivers humorous messages to passersby; "We serve humans too" is probably the most famous. While this is a bar that doesn't take itself too seriously, the cocktails are well thought-out and follow a philosophy of zero waste and constant reinvention. The eight signature drinks change daily and are produced from scratch using the leftovers from the Rumble in the Jungle prep process. The focus is on classical structures and fresh, seasonal ingredients. A vibrant bar in one of the world's most vibrant cocktail cities, The Bar in Front of the Bar offers something new with every visit.
verdict: Congenial street-side drinking with a zero-waste focus

name: The Bellwood
address: 41-31 Udagawacho, Shibuya, Tokyo 150-0042, Japan
history: Opened in 2020 by award-winning bartender Atsushi Suzuki in Tokyo's eclectic Shibuya neighbourhood, The Bellwood has since steadily earned its reputation as one of the best bars in Asia. The bar's concept is inspired by the café culture of late Meiji to Taisho-era Japan, a time in history when Western and Japanese sensibilities began to merge and create a unique experience that straddled both worlds. This revival of the wayō-setchū spirit successfully introduces historical storytelling charm into the modern context through an intimate and innovative cocktail experience. Suzuki's impressive bartending pedigree and years of valuable experience working in New York and Japan have left an indelible mark on his approach to cocktails at The Bellwood. The drinks programme borrows inspiration from the kaiseki tradition, where courses such as sakizuke (a small appetiser designed to awaken the palate), yakimono (grilled dishes typically showcasing seasonal ingredients), and gohan (rice, traditionally served toward the end of the meal) are thoughtfully reimagined as cocktails. Each course takes on the form of a cocktail that draws on both Japanese and Western spirits, carefully chosen to complement the essence of the original dish. The result is a refined and immersive cocktail experience that honours the structure and philosophy of the time-honoured tradition of Kaiseki. Tucked away at the back, the unmarked Bell Sushi counter serves a 12-piece omakase where each nigiri fuses traditional technique with global influences – a refined yet playful take on sushi.
verdict: Tokyo hotspot where cocktail kaiseki rewrites the drinking rulebook

name: The Cambridge Public House
address: 8 Rue de Poitou, 75003 Paris, France
history: Combining the convivial charm of a traditional English pub with the meticulous precision of a modern cocktail bar, The Cambridge Public House, founded in 2019 by notable Parisian bartender Hyacinthe Lescoët and his partners, has quickly become a standout venue in Paris. The bar's hybrid model offers a welcoming atmosphere where patrons can enjoy everything from craft beers and natural wines to delicious cocktails. The food menu bridges the French-British divide with bar snacks like homemade meat and veggie pies and sausage rolls, all prepared with French ingredients. This duality lives on in the drinks. The Cambridge takes great pride in its perfectly poured Guinness and their reinterpretation of the Pimm's, currently made with Alsatian wine, St. Germain and gin, is a menu evergreen. Other hits wear their French influence on the sleeve or in the names: Cigarette After Sex has been a standard since the bar's opening. What drew Lescoët to the pub model is the sense that they are communal institutions, and the bar strives to lead its community's push for sustainability and social responsibility. This commitment is documented in an annual Environmental, Social and Governance report, which ensures transparency and accountability. Last year, the bar became the first in the world to achieve B Corp certification, a rigorous distinction that recognises their very high standards. To share its knowledge, the bar also launched Shaken Leaf, a website dedicated to promoting and educating about sustainable practices in the hospitality world.
verdict: Where an English pub's soul meets Parisian cocktail mastery

name: The Chairman
address: 3rd Floor, The Wellington, 198 Wellington St, Central, Hong Kong
history:
A singular restaurant: The Chairman is a phenomenon precisely because it doesn’t seek to be: no theatrics, no PR tactics, no Instagram-focused plating – this is a restaurant where the food comes first. Its quietly innovative rewriting of classic Cantonese cooking comes in the form of entirely new recipes that are built on the bedrock of China’s culinary history. As a result, it has built a fiercely loyal local clientele, which now combines with international visitors aplenty. Don’t be fooled by the smartly suited waiters, this is one of the most welcoming spots in Hong Kong.

Settled in a new home: After building its unmatched reputation in a relatively non-descript location in Central, owner Danny Yip relocated the restaurant post-pandemic to a larger, smarter but still relaxed space in The Wellington nearby. Here, Yip’s cerebral taste for art and literature is more evident in the interior design, which also features leafy plants, an eclectic array of art pieces and low-lit lamps.

The duo behind it: The Chairman is the creation of Yip, a chef turned successful tech entrepreneur turned restaurateur, who learned his trade in Australia. He is now a revered figure back home in HK and beyond. However, the role of long-time head chef Kwok Keung Tung should not be underestimated. The duo has formed a unique partnership which sees them deep-dive into ingredient research and experiment with new recipes and cooking methods for months at a time, with duly memorable results.

House specials: Sichuan peppercorn stewed oxtail has become a new favourite (though nothing matches The Chairman’s steamed flowery crab dish for popularity), along with lighter bites such as delicately pickled rose buds with crispy raw lotus roots, lemon-spiked and seared local scallops scattered with rice noodles and spring onions and perfectly stir-fried seasonal vegetables zinging with XO sauce.
verdict: Legendary Central favourite rewriting the rules of Cantonese cuisine
chefName: turned successful tech entrepreneur turned restaurateur

name: The Keefer Bar
address: 135 Keefer St, Vancouver, BC V6A 1X3, Canada
history: Located within Vancouver's historic Chinatown, The Keefer Bar has been an institution of the city for more than a decade. Where expansion and modernisation has eroded away some of Chinatown's history, the bar uses its space to spotlight its history through a unique drinks programme that is inspired by old apothecaries. Keefer's selection of 'prescriptions', aka cocktails, are split into rotating seasonal options and menu mainstays. Around Lunar New Year, expect drinks like the milk-clarified Pink Pony Club, made with gin, bamboo liqueur, herbal liqueur, strawberry tea, lemon and hojicha. From the greatest hits 'Alumni' selection, the Antidote fully leans into the medicinal concept, combining Canadian whisky, yuzu sake, lemon and Nin Jiom (traditional Chinese herbal remedy). The classics are just as honed, with the Forager's Martini a fantastic rendition on the original, imbued with house-pickled vegetables and served at a cool -20 degrees. Don't miss out on its happy hour, where all bar snacks and dim sum are half price, and a selection of cocktails are just $8 a pop. Rising 21 places from its 2025 ranking, The Keefer Bar claims the title of The Best Bar in Canada, sponsored by Monkey Shoulder.
verdict: Located within Vancouver's historic Chinatown, The Keefer Bar has been an institution of the city for more than a decade. Where expansion and modernisation has eroded away some of Chinatown's history, the bar uses its space to spotlight its history through a unique drinks programme that is inspired by old apothecaries. Keefer's selection of 'prescriptions', aka cocktails, are split into rotating seasonal options and menu mainstays. Around Lunar New Year, expect drinks like the milk-clarified Pink Pony Club, made with gin, bamboo liqueur, herbal liqueur, strawberry tea, lemon and hojicha. From the greatest hits 'Alumni' selection, the Antidote fully leans into the medicinal concept, combining Canadian whisky, yuzu sake, lemon and Nin Jiom (traditional Chinese herbal remedy). The classics are just as honed, with the Forager's Martini a fantastic rendition on the original, imbued with house-pickled vegetables and served at a cool -20 degrees. Don't miss out on its happy hour, where all bar snacks and dim sum are half price, and a selection of cocktails are just $8 a pop. Rising 21 places from its 2025 ranking, The Keefer Bar claims the title of The Best Bar in Canada, sponsored by Monkey Shoulder.

name: The Pine
address: 7535 County Rd 9, Creemore, ON L0M 1G0, Canada
history:
Gather round: The Pine is a little restaurant that knows how to tell a tall tale or two. The passion project of husband-and-wife team Jeremy and Cassie Austin – the former handling the duties of head chef, the latter overseeing operations and responsible for the service philosophy – it's a restaurant that charts the couple's travels around China, each dish a poignant portrayal of a memory of a place. Although inspiration may come from far-flung points, the ingredients are all as locally sourced as possible, with much fresh produce delivered straight from the family's farm.

East meets West: After graduating from chef school, Jeremy Austin spent time working at Antica Corte Pallavicina, a decorated restaurant in Italy's Emilia-Romagna region, where he experienced the rigours of farm-to-table cooking first hand. Cassie was by his side, and the pair later moved on together to China to continue their culinary education – all the while gathering ideas and influences that would later shape The Pine.

What can I expect? Attempt to view The Pine's tasting menu on its website, and you'll be met with a series of colourful hieroglyphs. But, hey – there's no need to go into detail when, in the restaurant itself, each course is delivered with a card inscribed with a personal story. General manager and beverage director, Jeremiah Tomas, leads front-of-house service and is instrumental to the level of excellence guests experience. Previous dishes on the menu have included a tea egg soaked in star anise and black tea, stuffed with a mustardy devilled filling and crowned with caviar, and a sando: a corn-based, ice cream sandwich.

The space: Housed within a converted gas station, this contemporary 24-seat spot is designed around an open kitchen, with a chef's counter offering up-close views of culinary craft in motion. Light wood, clean lines and high ceilings act as the blank page upon which the multi-course menu's plot unfolds.
verdict: An immersive culinary journey through China

name: The Public House
address: Section 4, Xinyi Road, Da’an District, Taipei, 10681
history: Inspired by British pubs, The Public House was founded by William Wu, who previously worked at renowned Taipei bar Le Petite Chambre. Don't be deceived by the name, however. Drinks here are a far cry from the ones you'd typically find in an English boozer. Take for instance the deceptively simple gin & tonic, which is elevated with hand-carved ice and the precision one would expect of traditional East Asian-style of bartending. The bar also houses a range of signature cocktails, with its most adored serve, simply titled The Public 1, featuring gin, paochong tea, osmanthus syrup and chardonnay wine. Other creations from the menu, such as the Autumn Mosaic, made with rum, tea, ginger lily and fermented apple, are just as heavy of a hitter. The bar – which is open until 4am – serves food such as fried chicken and risotto until late, and if you stick around after 2am, you might get to taste the delicious (and free) chicken soup.
verdict: Inspired by British pubs, The Public House was founded by William Wu, who previously worked at renowned Taipei bar Le Petite Chambre. Don't be deceived by the name, however. Drinks here are a far cry from the ones you'd typically find in an English boozer. Take for instance the deceptively simple gin & tonic, which is elevated with hand-carved ice and the precision one would expect of traditional East Asian-style of bartending. The bar also houses a range of signature cocktails, with its most adored serve, simply titled The Public 1, featuring gin, paochong tea, osmanthus syrup and chardonnay wine. Other creations from the menu, such as the Autumn Mosaic, made with rum, tea, ginger lily and fermented apple, are just as heavy of a hitter. The bar – which is open until 4am – serves food such as fried chicken and risotto until late, and if you stick around after 2am, you might get to taste the delicious (and free) chicken soup.

name: The Savory Project
address: 4 Staunton Street, Soho, Central, Hong Kong
history: Following the success of Hong Kong's most accoladed bar, Coa, comes the co-founding team of Jay Khan and Ajit Gurung's freshest concept – The Savory Project – situated in the pulsing heart of Hong Kong's Soho district. Since opening the bar in May 2023, the duo has been tearing up the cocktail rulebook after taking note of an increased demand by guests requesting fewer sweet libations. Distinct from Coa, which focuses on all things agave, The Savory Project – as the name suggest – acts as a salute to unconventional flavours that accentuate savoury, earthy and umami tastes while dialling back sweet or fruity notes.
