By incorporating ingredients such as beef jerky, fungi, corn husks, Japanese white soy sauce and brine, the establishment is embracing a contemporary notion of culinary cocktails – or cocktails inspired by food recipes that involve ingredients typically found in the kitchen pantry rather than poured over ice. The venue's focal point is a custom-made hexagonal bar, an intentional design element that removes the barrier between bartender and curious guest to offer a more engaging experience, augmented by warm earth-toned mixed materials in marble, metal, wood and stone. Visitors can find the likes of Mala Punch – Sichuan peppercorn, mint, passionfruit, fermented bean, gin – and others christened Biryani, Gari Gari, and Peppercorn. Taking the concept a step further, the menu is divided between Hedonism (for those imbibing) and Temperance (for those abstaining), creating an equitable space for drinkers and non-drinkers alike, supporting another growing trend for non-alcoholic cocktails.
verdict: Following the success of Hong Kong's most accoladed bar, Coa, comes the co-founding team of Jay Khan and Ajit Gurung's freshest concept – The Savory Project – situated in the pulsing heart of Hong Kong's Soho district. By incorporating ingredients such as beef jerky, fungi, corn husks, Japanese white soy sauce and brine, the establishment is embracing a contemporary notion of culinary cocktails – or cocktails inspired by food recipes that involve ingredients typically found in the kitchen pantry rather than poured over ice. The venue's focal point is a custom-made hexagonal bar, an intentional design element that removes the barrier between bartender and curious guest to offer a more engaging experience, augmented by warm earth-toned mixed materials in marble, metal, wood and stone. Visitors can find the likes of Mala Punch – Sichuan peppercorn, mint, passionfruit, fermented bean, gin – and others christened Biryani, Gari Gari, and Peppercorn. Taking the concept a step further, the menu is divided between Hedonism (for those imbibing) and Temperance (for those abstaining), creating an equitable space for drinkers and non-drinkers alike, supporting another growing trend for non-alcoholic cocktails.

name: The St. Regis Bar
address: Rajawali Place, Jl. H. R. Rasuna Said No.4 Blok Kav. B, Kuningan, Jakarta, Indonesia
history: Located within The St. Regis Jakarta, this polished bar brings New York swagger and Indonesian elegance into the same frame. Inspired by the King Cole Bar in Manhattan, the venue is built around a glowing circular bar beneath a trumpet chandelier, with a jazz programme that gives the space its pulse. Designed by Alexandra Champalimaud, the room is equal parts glamour and comfort, and the perfect place for quiet martinis or late-night conversations under dim lights. The menu, New York-Inspired Cocktails 2.0, is a second act of sorts – reinterpreting timeless classics through Jakarta's lens. Signature drinks like the Lawyer's Sour Deal balance tropical flavours with bittersweet complexity, while each cocktail references NYC neighbourhoods from Manhattan to the Upper East Side. Live jazz performances and rituals like the 'Violet Hour' toast – when the sky outside turns purple and the bartenders start throwing martinis – complete the experience. This is a destination that captures the spirit of two cities at once – refined, rhythmic and quietly ambitious.
verdict: New Entry

name: Three X Co
history: A pioneer of KL's speakeasy movement, Three X Co continues to evolve without losing sight of its roots. Hidden behind a barbershop in Bangsar Shopping Centre, the bar blends chinoiserie elegance with easy-going charm – its signature mural of a Chinese opera diva with martini and cigar in hand still watching over the crowd. It's a place that respects cocktail tradition but isn't afraid to mess with it, led today by head bartender Amanda Wan, a World Class Malaysia champion and seasoned industry voice with stints in Hong Kong, New York and Singapore under her belt. The 2024 menu Indi V Duality is a celebration of self-expression, offering drinks that are both playful and precise. One section explores classic forms with a twist; the other, original creations built on unexpected contrasts. Each drink is tied to a persona – like the irreverent Whimsy Wabbit (a lychee martini twist accompanied by a jade jelly rabbit) or the bold Naughty Nana, which mixes bourbon and banana liqueur with Chinese Tie Guan Yin tea – inviting guests to discover their own bar alter ego.
verdict: Re-Entry

name: Tlecān
address: Av. Álvaro Obregón 228-Local 2, Roma Nte., Cuauhtémoc, 06700 Ciudad de México, CDMX, Mexico
history: Agave distillates are the lifeblood and beating heart at Tlecān, a discreet and intimate spot hidden away in Mexico City's trendy Roma Norte that is instantly noticeable for the invariable queue lining up outside. Translating as the Náhuatl word for 'place of fire', step over the threshold and come face to face with an impressive carved stone Disc of the Death that pays tribute to the country's pre-Hispanic roots; Tlecān is an authentic hub for Mexican culture and head bartender and co-owner Eli Martínez Bello one of its finest ambassadors. Naturally, mezcal is a protagonist, although Martínez Bello – who was named Altos Bartenders' Bartender as part of North America's 50 Best Bars 2025 – is both passionate and relentless in her search to source hard-to-find distillates directly from craft producers all over the country. Lesser-known spirits include bacanora, sotol, pox and raicilla available by the shot or used in one of Tlecān's creative concoctions that usually bear a twist; whatever you order will inadvertently be an education in agave. A house favourite is the Paloma Blanca, a refined classic made with carbonated and clarified grapefruit juice, lime juice, mezcal and volcanic salt, that's topped with soda water; enjoy it to a special curated cumbia playlist. For a twist on the classic Piña Colada, give the Pulque Colada a whirl: pulque (fermented sap of the maguey plant) plays out with pineapple milk punch, coconut water and espadín mezcal with a coconut oil fat wash. Should you find the drinks list overwhelming, the friendly agave-loving bar team is always happy to chime in with expert opinion.
verdict: An authentic hub of Mexican culture driven by agave distillates

name: To Infinity & Beyond
address: No. 13號, Lane 160, Section 1, Dunhua S Rd, Da’an District, Taipei City, Taiwan 106
history: To Infinity & Beyond is a cosmic expedition through the universe of flavours, textures, and innovative mixology. Opened in December 2019, the venue takes inspiration from the realms of space and science to create a drinking experience that is as imaginative as it is delicious. The ethos of the bar revolves around the idea that each cocktail is a small universe, filled with endless possibilities waiting to be explored. The team, led by 2021 World Class Taiwan Bartender of the Year, Mars Chang, infuses a scientific approach into their craft, utilising advanced techniques such as distillation and centrifugation to extract flavours and create liquid art that is at once layered and complex. The result is a menu that transcends traditional cocktail categories, focusing instead on textural differences: creaminess, clarification, and carbonation – classifications that allow consumers to easily understand contemporary cocktails. The atmosphere at To Infinity & Beyond further enhances this adventurous spirit, evoking a sense of escapism from Earth's orbit (and life's pressures that go along with it). Upon entering through space-themed automatic doors, guests are transported into a futuristic setting akin to stepping aboard a spaceship. The décor is equal parts sleek and psychedelic, with a layout that encourages interaction. Signature sippers, such as (EARTH)²⁰²⁵ encapsulate the primal essence of Mother Earth via ingredients like pine needles, pink peppercorns, and woody palo santo to mirror the natural elements of soil and forest. Otherworldly Σ(stars) = GALAXY sees backlit ingredients like red guava and jasmine-scented tea, mimicking the cosmos in visual aesthetic. A new entry to the list in 2025, To Infinity & Beyond is continuously reimagining Taipei's cocktail culture, making it a must-visit destination for both cocktail buffs and beginners alike.
verdict: New Entry

name: Torrisi
address: 275 Mulberry St. New York, NY 10012, United States
history:
'Italian', in inverted commas: Torrisi may officially be an Italian restaurant – but it's simultaneously as New York as can be, drawing inspiration from the city's abundance of red-sauce joints, Jewish delis, Chinatown restaurants and West Indian patty shops to create a menu that could only possibly make sense in the Big Apple. A tangle of apparent contradictions, it's playful yet precise, traditional yet modern, showy yet stylish; so it's no surprise that it has food lovers clamouring to come and judge it for themselves.

Putting on the ritz: Housed inside the iconic Puck Building on Mulberry Street in the city's trendy Nolita neighbourhood, this opulent eatery (from the Major Food Group that brought you Carbone, Sadelle's and Dirty French, among others) isn't shy about making a statement. All vaulted ceilings, geometric marble floors, aquamarine velvet booths and overflowing flower arrangements, its extravagance somehow serves to make its dishes seem even more inviting.

Must-try dishes: Speciality starters include Don's scungilli toast – marinated scungilli (conch) prepared by chef Don Grazio DeMonico, served on toasted bread – and cucumbers New Yorkese, an Italian American-inspired riff on deli pickles. In the mood for pasta? Plump for the cavatelli with spicy Jamaican beef ragu. Or the chicken alla griglia, with its super crispy skin and juicy meat, is also always a hit. For dessert, you can't go wrong with Torrisi's unique take on affogato, made with layers of vanilla ice cream, fudge sauce and mascarpone cream topped with coffee granita. A hot ticket: Unsurprisingly, given the restaurant's immense popularity, it can be tricky to get a table – they're released 30 days in advance, and the best time slots tend to get snapped up quickly. For those willing to try their luck, a limited number of bar seats are held for walk-ins each evening, where the full menu is available (perhaps save for a few specials).
verdict: Little Italy? No. This is BIG Italy

name: Tres Monos
address: Guatemala 4899, C1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina
history: Straddling the corner of a leafy street in the cosmopolitan Palermo district of Buenos Aires, Tres Monos is the small indie bar that makes a big impact. In Argentina, bars have tended towards the classical, glamorous side of cocktails, while Tres Monos went the other way. The name translates to Three Monkeys and, orchestrated by its trio of owners – Sebastian Atienza, Charly Aguinsky and Gus Vocke – there is plenty of monkeying around. The scene here is dark, lit barely by neon, soundtracked by rock and scrawled in graffiti – a theme that now extends to a larger space out back named El Garage. But, of course, one doesn't get recognised as one of Argentina's best bars by dive vibes alone – this is a bar with brains. If you care to ask over the bar and the roaring music, you'll find that this is an Argentinian bar that speaks the native language. Great thought and energy has gone into local sourcing, with the bar even partnering with producers to create its own-label products – such as a bourbon-style whiskey with local grains, liqueurs, sake and wines, including sauvignon blanc and pét-nat sparkling. You're in a dive, so it's perfectly fine to keep things simple and order a glass of any of the aforementioned, but cocktails are very much in its wheelhouse too. The latest menu features Julep de D10S, a mix of Tres Monos house Licor del Norte (a liqueur of three Argentinian herbs), amaros, strawberry miso and grapefruit. And there's a decent chance that the person serving you has also graduated from the bar's own La Escuelita academy, which trains those from disadvantaged barrios in hospitality skills. Just another way in which this dive bar runs deeper.
verdict: A dark and divey rock bar showcasing Argentina's liquid larder

name: Trescha
address: Murillo 725, C1414 Cdad. Autónoma de Buenos Aires, Argentina
history:
What makes it special? In a city where tasting menus are often eschewed for casual dining, one young chef is determined to serve haute cuisine. Trescha opened in 2022 in the trendy Villa Crespo neighbourhood of Buenos Aires in a beautiful casona (mansion), and serves a 16-course menu to just 10 diners seated at a kitchen counter.

About the chef: Tomás Treschanski followed his passion for the culinary arts and trained in London before undertaking various stages at the likes of Azurmendi, Barrafina, Frantzén, 108 and Boragó. Aged just 25, he opened Trescha with the aim of following the fine-dining ideals of those restaurants, but using mostly Argentine ingredients.

Behind the scenes: Treschanski takes research and development seriously and has a dedicated team working in the Test Kitchen, the heart of the project, to develop the ever-changing menu.

What's the concept? An immersive experience, dinner starts at the bar before moving to the chef's counter, where the kitchen team moves seamlessly as one. Dessert is taken on the outdoor patio, while the wine cellar is world class.

On the menu: Focusing on a key ingredient per course, Treschanski takes things to a new level. Paso Lengua, for example, takes tongue to new extremes: braised and served with aged chimichurri, onion, endives, pollen and black tongue mortadella in a rye sandwich with sweet chilli, quince, huacatay mint and 35-year-old balsamic vinegar; and puff pastry brioche with pollen.
verdict: Fine dining reimagined through Argentine ingredients and contemporary ideas
chefName: is determined to serve haute cuisine. Trescha opened in

name: Trèsind Studio
address: St. Regis Gardens Entrance B - The Palm Jumeirah - Dubai
history:
The mastermind: Learning his trade at lauded New Delhi dining institution Indian Accent before moving to Dubai, chef Himanshu Saini has become the standard-bearer of modern Indian gastronomy in the UAE. After opening Trèsind in 2014 and the fantastically experimental and immersive Trèsind Studio in 2018, both to critical acclaim, he has quickly gained a reputation as one of India’s hottest culinary exports.

What’s the food like? Titled ‘Rising India’, its latest tasting menu pays homage to the nation's 5,000-year history and the plurality of produce that has long stocked its larder. Dishes on the menu are grouped into areas of the subcontinent: stop at the Deccan Plateau for some tender coconut kushiyaki with hearts of palm and yuzu rasam, before later swinging by the Northern Plains and Himalayan Mountains for a course of king oyster noodles with black fungus XO sauce and shoyu of morel mushrooms.

A touch of theatre: Dining at Trèsind Studio, now located on The Palm Jumeirah, can be a dramatic experience. As you progress through the tasting menu, expect to be relocated to different areas of the restaurant, each carefully curated to match the plates in front of you. With just 20 seats available, its already intimate experience may be rapidly dialled up as the dining room plunges into darkness for a course or two.

Leading hospitality: Taking inspiration from the popular Indian phrase ‘atithi devo bhava’, directly translated as ‘the guest is god’, astute hospitality lies at the core of Trèsind Studio’s operation. Every service executed by its accomplished team is a masterclass, as they expertly manoeuvre in tandem with each meal's ever-changing scenery and ambience with relative ease.
verdict: Landmark, theatrical, cutting-edge Indian cuisine
chefName: Himanshu Saini has become the standard-bearer of modern Indian gastronomy in the UAE. After opening Trèsind in

name: True Laurel
address: 753 Alabama St, San Francisco, CA 94110, United States
history: You'll find True Laurel in the heart of San Francisco's Mission District: a cozy but buzzy cocktail destination with two bars, indoor tables and outdoor patio dining. Chef Te'sean Glass's dinner and brunch menus filled with stellar bar food complement bar director's Nicolas Torres's creative cocktail menu of science-forward, complex drinks that incorporate local flora you didn't know existed, whether that's overgrown weeds, foraged conifers or root vegetables from local farmers. Its menu, which is full of visually stunning and often crystal-clear cocktails, changes day-to-day due to the hyper-seasonality of its ingredients. Expect to find creations made out of unique applications such as roasted sunchokes, wild oxalis weeds, anise hyssop distillate and yoghurt whey-fermented lemonade. Hardly anything goes to waste here, with seeds and peels factored into the drinks and providing nuanced notes on the palate – just a few of the reasons the bar was bestowed with the Ketel One Sustainable Bar Award in 2024. This year, it adds to its trophy cabinet with the title of The Best Bar in West USA 2026, sponsored by St-Germain. Service is warm and knowledgeable but not overbearing, with just the right amount of background knowledge imparted with each dish and drink. Reservations are also available, including tasting bar takeovers where parties of six to ten get the entire bar to themselves for two hours. Enjoy each glass within the confines of an artfully crafted space, where every component has been custom designed to make locals feel right at home as much as they are intended to make visitors feel as though they've stepped into a true cocktail destination.
verdict: You'll find True Laurel in the heart of San Francisco's Mission District: a cozy but buzzy cocktail destination with two bars, indoor tables and outdoor patio dining. Chef Te'sean Glass's dinner and brunch menus filled with stellar bar food complement bar director's Nicolas Torres's creative cocktail menu of science-forward, complex drinks that incorporate local flora you didn't know existed, whether that's overgrown weeds, foraged conifers or root vegetables from local farmers. Its menu, which is full of visually stunning and often crystal-clear cocktails, changes day-to-day due to the hyper-seasonality of its ingredients. Expect to find creations made out of unique applications such as roasted sunchokes, wild oxalis weeds, anise hyssop distillate and yoghurt whey-fermented lemonade. Hardly anything goes to waste here, with seeds and peels factored into the drinks and providing nuanced notes on the palate – just a few of the reasons the bar was bestowed with the Ketel One Sustainable Bar Award in 2024. This year, it adds to its trophy cabinet with the title of The Best Bar in West USA 2026, sponsored by St-Germain. Service is warm and knowledgeable but not overbearing, with just the right amount of background knowledge imparted with each dish and drink. Reservations are also available, including tasting bar takeovers where parties of six to ten get the entire bar to themselves for two hours. Enjoy each glass within the confines of an artfully crafted space, where every component has been custom designed to make locals feel right at home as much as they are intended to make visitors feel as though they've stepped into a true cocktail destination.

name: Tuju
address: R. Frei Galvão, 135 - Jardim Paulistano, São Paulo - SP, 01454-060
history:
Location, location, location: Housed in a strikingly imposing three-story building in the bustling heart of São Paulo in the upscale Jardim Paulistano neighbourhood, Tuju stands out with its seamless blend of inventive cuisine and exceptional service. The airy, modern space – with decor blending natural materials and lush gardens – enhances the service, elevating hospitality across three distinct areas: a bar-patio on the ground floor, the main dining room on the second floor and a terrace for post-dinner moments. In each space, meticulous attention to detail makes all the difference in making guests feel welcomed and cared for.

The vibe: Led by chef Ivan Ralston, the restaurant's 10-course menu is strictly seasonal, changing every three months so that every ingredient is used when at its peak. Ethical concerns are paramount here, and the team works closely with local fruit and vegetable producers and uses only Brazilian meat raised on farms with strong social and environmental credentials.

Great plates: Dishes such as crab with cod and carrot, aubergine with peanut and coffee and fig with tarragon and sobrasada allow the ingredients to truly shine. Each plate at Tuju reflects São Paulo's rich and varied local produce, executed with precision and flair. Less than a year after reopening – following a three-year closure after its time in Vila Madalena – the restaurant earned two Michelin stars.

Award-winning hospitality: Serving only one-third of the number of guests compared to the former venue, the new Tuju focuses on outstanding service as its secret ingredient. This meticulous effort earned Tuju the prestigious Gin Mare Art of Hospitality Award 2024 for dining excellence and reinforced its goal to create a warm, welcoming atmosphere for its guests.
verdict: Precise and personalised cooking reimaging Brazilian fine dining

name: Uliassi
address: Via Banchina di Levante 6, 60019 Senigallia, Ancona, Italy
history:
Stunning surrounds: Sitting like a pearl between the marina and the beach, Uliassi is set in a beautiful white wooden structure with an iconic summer balcony overlooking the sea. The location is home to head chef Mauro and maître d' sister Catia Uliassi, who welcome guests to this unique restaurant with open arms.

Classic and contemporary: Uliassi’s cuisine is now a well-known reference for gourmets from all over the world. It manages to move seamlessly between classical and innovative plates, all at a super-premium level. This is thanks to the Uliassi Lab: a creative effort involving the entire team, which gathers to come up with new, exciting ideas during the winter months.

Marrying land and sea: The uniqueness of Uliassi’s cuisine is found in the chef’s study of marine flavours and how they meet – and ‘contaminate’ – meat-based products. It’s part of a sea-land exchange typical of the Marche regional tradition. Thanks to a careful balancing act between ingredients, consistencies, techniques and temperatures, every dish manages to surprise and delight.

What dishes to expect: After the timeless appetisers (regular guests would revolt if chef were to phase them out), which include a foie gras wafer and kir royale, dishes arrive including seared cuttlefish, cheek lard oil, chard, honey and anchovy sauce; and pasta with tomato sauce ‘Alla Hilde’ – an ode to perfumer Hilde Soliani, Every year the team adds 10 new courses presented along with a Classic menu.

Keeping it local: The Uliassi siblings were born and raised in the area and opened the restaurant in 1990.
verdict: Innovative, memorable Italian cooking with a view to match
chefName: Mauro and maître d' sister Catia Uliassi

name: Vender
address: No. 118, Wuquan West 4th St, West District, Taichung
history: After a successful spell bartending in Singapore, Summer Chen returned to her native Taiwan to open Vender with her partner, Darren Lim. Chen liked the idea that vending machines provide daily essentials everywhere, so she based her bar on this premise. Indeed, to enter the premises, guests need to insert a coin in the vending machine at the entrance. Once inside, everyone is greeted by a small welcoming portion of a Singapore Sling as a nod to Chen's recent past, before selecting from vending machines selling bottled cocktails and beers alongside capsules denoting the cocktails. Vender's menu features pockets containing the main ingredients used in each signature cocktail – which are named after different types of vending machines – allowing guests to explore each element to inform their final choice. Drinks feature produce, herbs and spices from Southeast Asia, and the food offering also nods to the region with dishes such as bak kut teh (Hokkien pork rib broth) and chili crab. The Cigar Vending Machine cocktail is inspired by Chen's first taste of Cuban cigars and features dark rum, red wine syrup, fresh lemon juice, egg white, aromatised wine and chocolate bitters, and is smoked using Taiwanese cypress wood chips. Vender is once again named The Best Bar in Taiwan, sponsored by Naked Malt.
verdict: After a successful spell bartending in Singapore, Summer Chen returned to her native Taiwan to open Vender with her partner, Darren Lim. Chen liked the idea that vending machines provide daily essentials everywhere, so she based her bar on this premise. Indeed, to enter the premises, guests need to insert a coin in the vending machine at the entrance. Once inside, everyone is greeted by a small welcoming portion of a Singapore Sling as a nod to Chen's recent past, before selecting from vending machines selling bottled cocktails and beers alongside capsules denoting the cocktails. Vender's menu features pockets containing the main ingredients used in each signature cocktail – which are named after different types of vending machines – allowing guests to explore each element to inform their final choice. Drinks feature produce, herbs and spices from Southeast Asia, and the food offering also nods to the region with dishes such as bak kut teh (Hokkien pork rib broth) and chili crab. The Cigar Vending Machine cocktail is inspired by Chen's first taste of Cuban cigars and features dark rum, red wine syrup, fresh lemon juice, egg white, aromatised wine and chocolate bitters, and is smoked using Taiwanese cypress wood chips. Vender is once again named The Best Bar in Taiwan, sponsored by Naked Malt.

name: Vesper
address: 10/15 Convent Road, Silom, Bangkok
history: European-inspired Vesper – one of the OG Bangkok sipping spots with a decade of history along Convent Road under its belt – is serious about cocktails. Accented by leather furnishings and forest green counters, the establishment has continued to evolve, most recently under bar manager and British transplant Tom Hearn, who built his career at the American Bar at The Savoy and other lauded London establishments. Hearn recently launched the People of Vesper Vol. 3 menu, honouring the roster of VIPs that have been part of the bar's long history. The lineup of classic cocktails and new creations, plus the Mediterranean-leaning tapas menu, represent the bar's polyglot origins in an unpretentious approachable manner to the everyday drinker. Where the signature Vesper – of which there are three variants to choose – is one that will forever remain on the menu, the White Matcha Negroni supercharges this timeless tipple with pistachio matcha and white chocolate for the ultimate nightcap. Firmly established as a bastion of Asia's cocktail scene, this year Vesper is named the recipient of the Rémy Martin Legend of the List Award 2025.
verdict: European-inspired Vesper – one of the OG Bangkok sipping spots with a decade of history along Convent Road under its belt – is serious about cocktails. Accented by leather furnishings and forest green counters, the establishment has continued to evolve, most recently under bar manager and British transplant Tom Hearn, who built his career at the American Bar at The Savoy and other lauded London establishments. Hearn recently launched the People of Vesper Vol. 3 menu, honouring the roster of VIPs that have been part of the bar's long history. The lineup of classic cocktails and new creations, plus the Mediterranean-leaning tapas menu, represent the bar's polyglot origins in an unpretentious approachable manner to the everyday drinker. Where the signature Vesper – of which there are three variants to choose – is one that will forever remain on the menu, the White Matcha Negroni supercharges this timeless tipple with pistachio matcha and white chocolate for the ultimate nightcap. Firmly established as a bastion of Asia's cocktail scene, this year Vesper is named the recipient of the Rémy Martin Legend of the List Award 2025.

name: Viceversa
address: 398 NE 5th St, Miami, FL 33132, United States
history: Viceversa signifies a swapped order. At this Miami destination, Italian expat Valentino Longo mashes up the refined hospitality of his homeland with the joyful exuberance of the Florida coast. An illuminated crescent backbar fuses Miami's signature decor with Italian futurism. The room's velvet green, marble tables and cherrywood trim conjure Longo's Roman youth; the stylish crowd would be at home in a Fellini film. However, Viceversa's inspiration is a different Italian artist, Fortunato Depero. His 1920's style of vibrant colours and fractured geometry decorate the wall, menus and even the cocktails themselves. The menu is grounded in the Futurists' 'Polibibita' philosophy of cocktails that present unexpected, contrasting flavours, along with playful visuals. Such combinations are presented in creations like Little Tonico (white port, elderflower liqueur, sherry, olive brine, Mediterranean tonic) and the Forza! (bourbon, bergamot, vermouth, blackcurrant). Valentino pays homage to his roots with a wide selection of negroni riffs, vermouths, sherry and amari. Apertivo also drives chef Justin Flit's acclaimed culinary menu of shareable plates. Flit leans on simple, flavourful preparations of immaculately sourced, fresh ingredients. The raw bar is popular, boasting several types of oyster alongside preparations like bluefin tuna with scallion broth and tomatillo. Rounding out the Italian angle, the kitchen's pizzas rank among the state's best. But, the pizzas rotate seasonally because at Viceversa, the only certainties are elite hospitality and a surprise to the taste buds.
verdict: New Entry

name: Villa Torél
address: México 3 Km 94, 22766 Villa de Juárez, B.C., Mexico
history:
Alfredo Villanueva and Fernando Zavala

What is it about? Villa Torél was born in 2019 as the restaurant of the Santo Tomás vineyard, one of the oldest in the Baja California region and in all of Mexico, located in San Antonio de las Minas. It is a restaurant with no fine dining pretensions, focusing instead on a short, well-thought menu served in a lovely venue, showing utmost respect to the producers and neighbouring Valle de Guadalupe food projects.

Who's in the kitchen? Born in Mexico City, chef Alfredo Villanueva moved to Monterrey when he was young and lived there for more than 20 years. He is well-known in the Mexican food scene as a chef of great values, a creator of memorable flavours and a master of rice and fire. He moved to Ensenada with his wife Denise to open Villa Torél, creating a place where food doesn't only gather people around the table, but also generates community and positive opportunities for everyone involved. He calls his concept 'proximity cuisine'.

The setting: A beautiful terrace with views of the vineyards and mountains complement the food experience, which is always relaxed with dishes made for sharing. The luxury of being in contact with nature and a fun atmosphere are both main ingredients for Villa Torél.

Bonus point: In 2022, the team came up with the concept for Liernes, a combination of the Spanish words for Monday (lunes) and Friday (viernes). It's an experience that sees different chefs from Baja California and other parts of the country come to Villa Torél to celebrate by throwing a party to start the week.
verdict: Hyper-local produce takes centre stage in a picturesque setting
chefName: Alfredo Villanueva moved to Monterrey when he was young and lived there for more than

name: Virtù
address: 1 Chome-2-1 Ōtemachi, Chiyoda City, Tokyo 100-0004
history: High above the city in the Four Seasons Hotel Tokyo at Otemachi, Virtù pairs skyline views with a warmth that keeps the luxury from feeling stiff. Keith Motsi and his polyglot team bring humour and ease to a room of brass details, Art Deco flourishes and floor-to-ceiling windows looking out over eastern Tokyo and the Skytree. Fluent in nine languages, the staff can guide you through the nuances of a cognac or simply let you sink into the view. The drinks carry the same blend of polish and personality. Most of the menu mixes French spirits with Japanese produce, like the Smoked Ume Fashioned, made with homemade rye-and-brandy umeshu, Japanese whisky and hinoki bitters. A new range goes all-in on Japanese ingredients, from a garibaldi brightened with local amaro to a martini built on Japanese gin, vodka and vermouth. A map in the menu pinpoints where ingredients come from, whether that's a small sake brewery or a local orchard. Service runs on quiet coordination, with details about guests – birthdays, anniversaries, favourite drinks – passed along invisibly so the welcome feels personal without being scripted. It's that mix of care, craft and comfort that won Virtù the Michter's Art of Hospitality Award in 2024, and that keeps it from feeling like any other luxury hotel lounge in the city.
verdict: Franco-Japanese cocktails served with award-winning hospitality

name: Vyn
address: Höga vägen 72, 272 92 Simrishamn
history:
What makes it special: Vyn (meaning ’view’ in Swedish) is as much about the place as it is about the food of one of Sweden’s best chefs. It starts with the trip from Copenhagen or Stockholm to southern Sweden, to Daniel Berlin’s homeland of Skåne: a region of dreamy pastoral landscapes, picturesque villages and, on arrival, stunning views of the Baltic Sea. It’s a place to relax, immerse yourself in the beauty of nature, and enjoy life.

About the chef: After closing his previous restaurant in September 2020 due to family reasons, the news of Daniel Berlin reopening at a different place at the end of 2023 caused a stir in the culinary world. Admired as much for his integrity and authenticity as for his exquisite skills in the kitchen, Berlin draws inspiration from the fields, forests and coastlines of Skåne – guided by a profound personal connection to the region.

Nature at its core: Rooted in sustainability and local tradition, Berlin’s roughly 16-course menu reflects a nose-to-tail philosophy, built on ingredients that are foraged, grown and hunted from nearby landscapes and shores. Guided by the seasons and shaped by nature, the menu reflects the coastal setting, with warm king crab, butter-poached langoustine and local favourite Kalix löjrom (vendace roe) being frequent highlights. His hot and cold scallop, pan-fried with cold metal discs on top and served in a dill sugar kelp and caramelised apple sauce, is as simple as it is genius.

A place to stay: Adjacent to the restaurant is a boutique hotel with 15 sunlit guest rooms featuring locally handmade beds and glass sculptures by Swedish artist Ellen Ehk. The cosy food and wine bar has a relaxed, communal Scandi flair, offering lighter in-season dishes and a delicious, hearty breakfast for overnight guests.
verdict: Southern Sweden on a plate with a knockout view

name: Wana Yook
address: 5, 15 Phaya Thai Rd, Thanon Phaya Thai, Ratchathewi, Bangkok 10400, Thailand
history:
A Thai staple, reimagined: Housed in a magnificent colonial residence dating back to the reign of King Rama VI, Wana Yook takes tradition seriously – but that certainly doesn't mean it's afraid of innovation. At the heart of its philosophy is khao gaeng, a beloved Thai comfort food concept that literally translates as 'rice with curry.' However, under the discerning eye of chef Chalee Kader, this humble pairing sheds its street-food status, transformed through premium ingredients, inventive techniques and immaculate presentation.

Style and sophistication: All high ceilings, tall windows and curated art, Wana Yook's beautifully restored interior is elegant yet comfortable, with stained-glass accents and period furniture adding to the sense of history and grandeur. Guests begin with welcome drinks in the relaxed lounge before making their way upstairs to the dining room, where rich wooden furniture, silk cushions and moody lighting set an intimate, inviting stage for the tasting menu experience.

What does it entail? Chef Kader's 12-course menus evolve with the seasons, consistently showcasing different rice varieties from across Thailand alongside imaginative interpretations of regional staples. For example, instead of the usual chilli dipping sauce, the tod man pla fish cakes come with a caviar-like topping of cucumber and shallots, and a dish of crab and tiger prawns is served with a tai pla curry like you've never seen it before.

Finishing on a high note: Pleasingly, classic Thai sweets get a modern makeover at Wana Yook, too. An abstract version of piak poon (a confection made with rice flour and palm sugar) is finished with coconut husk dust, pandan leaves and meringue, while another standout dessert pairs toddy palm (a sweet, fragrant fruit) with an aromatic bael-fruit pandan tea.
verdict: A fine dining take on khao gaeng

name: Wild Blue
address: 4005 Whistler Way, Whistler, BC V8B 1J1, Canada
history:
Best of the Northwest: A triple-threat collaboration between veteran restaurateur Jack Evrensel, hospitality expert Neil Henderson and Alex Chen, winner of the inaugural Iron Chef Canada competition, Wild Blue has become one of Whistler's most acclaimed fine-dining destinations since it opened its doors in 2022. A must-visit for outdoor-enthusiast tourists and locals alike, it serves a menu centred around seasonal, sustainable Pacific Northwest fare – in particular, sparklingly fresh seafood.

Tell me more: Popular sea-sourced creations on the a la carte dinner menu include a quintessentially British Columbian take on linguine alle vongole (made with sweet, plump Manila clams and their briny brother, geoduck) and sablefish with roasted fish bone sauce and an umami-rich preparation of turnip, radish, maitake mushroom and sesame. Meanwhile, the grill offers veal chops, pork chops and a seductive assortment of steaks, and the raw bar showcases an embarrassment of riches from the deep – including oysters, chilled lobster and seafood towers.

And then? Wild Blue's desserts are as considered as its mains, from elevated comfort-food indulgences like sticky toffee pudding with walnut nougatine, salted caramel ice cream and toffee sauce, to a selection of house-made sorbets and an elegant pear gaufrette: a honey wafer served with poached pears, bergamot gelée and jasmine diplomat cream. Plus, each one can be paired with a recommended wine or vermouth to make that finishing note even sweeter.

A stylish affair: Located right in the heart of pedestrian-friendly Whistler Village, Wild Blue has all the contemporary elegance you'd expect from a sophisticated big-city restaurant. All dark wood panelling, velvet seating and intimate low lighting, with an impressive wall of wine and open kitchen views to draw your gaze, it's a little like a modern, fine-dining take on a classic North American steakhouse.
verdict: Celebrating the Pacific Northwest’s rich bounty

name: Wing
address: 29/F The Wellington, 198 Wellington St, Central, Hong Kong
history:
The backstory: What grew out of an invite-only, midnight-supper test kitchen at chef-owner Vicky Cheng’s debut Hong Kong venue, Vea, became his ‘boundary-less’ Chinese fine-dining establishment, Wing, located just one floor below. With a 20-year background in French classical cooking, Cheng never planned to open a Chinese restaurant, but after returning to his birthplace of Hong Kong, he found himself gravitating towards local tastes.

Why Wing? Drawing inspiration from Cheng’s Chinese name, Wing represents hope and perseverance – sentiments that reflect the chef’s pride for China’s heritage coupled with a desire to share the untold stories of its abundant culinary history; an expedition diners traverse through the seasonal tasting menu.

Tradition made new: Wing’s fare serves as an homage to the eight great Chinese cuisines, innovatively respecting tradition through a no-rules contemporary interpretation. Think dry-aged crispy Cantonese chicken – a recipe that took more than 80 trials to perfect; smoked pigeon with sugarcane; and a pork belly-like slab of braised sea cucumber enveloped in a puffed spring roll. Don’t forget the fruit trolley service for the final sweet treat.

World-leading service: In addition to its explorative, ground-breaking food offering from the kitchen, Wing is just as hot when it comes to the front-of-house operation. Service at Wing comes with all the slickness and luxury of modern fine-dining restaurants, thanks to a hawk-eyed team who are in-tune with each diner’s tastes and ready to help shape their experience accordingly. For those who revisit Wing, notes on their tastes will be resurfaced ahead of their arrival to ensure their upcoming experience is not an exact replica of their previous meal, with new dishes in tow. Thanks to this, Wing is named the winner of the Gin Mare Art of Hospitality Award 2025.
verdict: An innovative, expansive approach to Chinese fine dining

name: Yakoboku
address: 5−21 1F 夜香木, Minamitsuboimachi, Chuo Ward, Japan, 860-0848 Kumamoto, 〒
history: The southern Japanese city of Kumamoto is known for its towering castle, deep shochu culture and an inexplicably popular cartoon bear mascot, but until recently, never for cocktails. That changed when Shinya Koba returned home after five years in Singapore to open Yakoboku in 2020. Situated in a former ryokan in the city's old Minamitsuboi-machi entertainment district, the bar stands out from its neighbours with a dramatic cypress front wall that rotates open to the street. Inside is a long camphor counter, just 15 seats, and a room that's all wood and restrained, ambient-lit minimalism. Koba's cocktails are deeply rooted in local culture. His Kyushu Old Fashioned employs homemade amaro, hinoki wood and an infusion of haze tree wax – a material used by Kumamoto's candle artisans, now a dying art he wants to help sustain. A Trinidad Sour is reimagined with Kyushu shiranui citrus from a local farm; a Naked & Famous gets a Japanese twist with ume-infused chartreuse. And you can't be a Kyushu bar without serving shochu, so Koba makes a pistachio-accented twist on an espresso martini, with shochu as the low-abv base. The name of the bar refers to night jasmine – a flower that only releases its scent after dark. It's a fitting metaphor for an establishment that flourishes, elegantly, after sundown. There's no concept-heavy framing, no Instagram bait. Just drinks made with care, balance, and a strong sense of place.
verdict: New Entry

name: Yum Cha
address: La Herradura 2722, Providencia, Santiago, 7530001
history:
Origin story: Chef Nicolás Tapia launched Yum Cha after travelling to China and journeying through the country's tea regions, discovering for himself the Cantonese tradition of yum cha (to drink tea), which inspired the chef to open a restaurant centered on the ancient leaves. The restaurant embodies reflection, curiosity and connection, drawing from Chinese and Chilean culinary traditions while embracing an artisan's mindset: extracting depth from simplicity, valuing skill over luxury, and celebrating freshness as the ultimate indulgence.

What sets it apart: A leading figure in Santiago's fine-dining evolution, Tapia balances technical discipline with quiet innovation. His approach celebrates the artistry of service and the meditative act of dining – an invitation to pause, taste and rediscover the beauty of restraint. With fewer than 20 seats, Yum Cha offers intimacy without pretension. Diners can choose from four pairing paths: tea, Chilean wine, a blend of both, or a tri-pairing that also incorporates Japanese sake, each designed to mirror the kitchen's calm precision.

On the menu: Yum Cha revolves around a six or 10-course pescatarian tasting menu in which each dish pairs with a different tea, from Chinese oolong to Taiwanese red. The menu changes three or four times a year, guided by the teas that inspire each course. Two signature dishes remain constant:pescado crudo with green mango, herbs and chilli oil, and pescado al vapor with cabbage and Sichuan pepper. Desserts follow three playful rules: one must be fresh (often a Japanese kakigori), another celebrates seasonal fruit, and the third reinterprets a nut or seed. Mostly lactose-free and low in sugar, they show that balance and pleasure can coexist.

Location and experience: Set in a quiet residence in Providencia, Yum Cha features two discreet dining rooms and a bar facing the open kitchen. The atmosphere is serene, service is measured and reservations are essential.
verdict: Tea-led dining fusing Chinese and Chilean gastronomy
chefName: Nicolás Tapia launched Yum Cha after travelling to China and journeying through the country's tea regions

name: Zest
address: B1 26 KR 55 Dosan-daero 55-gil, Gangnam-gu, Seoul, Korea
history: Pride in provenance is what drives Zest's cocktail programme, leaning on ingredients rooted in Korea's biodiverse landscape. Zest advocates for local producers and community-driven practices, sourcing vegetables and herbs through contract farming, while harvesting 'city honey' from the bar's own beehives in Seoul. These hyper-local elements are skillfully integrated with carefully selected global spirits and liquors from Korean micro-distilleries and craft breweries, forming the backbone of Zest's notable cocktail programme. At the heart of this vision is co-founder Dohyung 'Demie' Kim, winner of the Altos Bartenders' Bartender Award 2024, a prestigious peer‑voted honour within Asia's 50 Best Bars community. Kim and his partners lead the bar's ethos of minimal waste and thoughtful precision. The drinks at Zest are designed to be sustainable, trying as far as possible to utilise the ingredients in their entirety. To honour every part of the Hallabong for example, the team squeezes the fresh juice for the Jeju Garibaldi, repurposes the peels to infuse the house gin, and gives the leftover pulp a new lease of life, either as a cordial or fermented and used in the bar's signature Pulp Sauerkraut. With no back-bar display and a chic, minimalist aesthetic, Zest embraces the Korean philosophy of 'beauty in emptiness.' When it comes to technique and presentation, the bar takes a disciplined approach. Every drink is built with intention, avoiding all forms of excess including garnish, unless it truly belongs. The focus is on clean flavours, balance, and precise methods that highlight the quality of the ingredients. House-made elements are used with purpose, and the same philosophy guides the service and the space: efficient and restrained.
verdict: South Korea’s terroir distilled into minimalist, hyper-seasonal cocktails

name: ZLB23
address: The Leela Palace, Kodihalli, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560008, India
history: A stroll down a winding garden path, a trot through a bustling hotel kitchen and a short ride in a service lift: this brief journey, albeit confounding, is well worth it, because it delivers you to this Kyoto-inspired speakeasy in the heart of Bengaluru. In the age of Instagram, it's hard for a bar to remain a secret, especially when it's in an iconic city hotel such as The Leela Palace Bengaluru. Yet, ZLB23, complete with heavy velvet curtains and crystal chandeliers, manages to transport guests straight to the Prohibition era. On the menu of this vibey, underground bar – where the jazz bands are boisterously fun – is a selection of small bites and well-crafted cocktails. The team, led by bar manager Suchismita Roy and head mixologist Rajib Mukhergee, whips up inspired takes on classic cocktails such as the Kyoto Collins, made with whisky, lapsang souchong and aromatised wine. Other cocktails take a more adventurous route, such as the Shoyu Ramen, which reimagines the iconic noodle dish into a mixture of tequila, sake, shiro miso and shochu. While cocktails take centre stage, ZLB23 also boasts an impressive list of hard-to-find whiskeys and other spirits from around the world. The food is no afterthought, with the culinary team crafting a tightly edited curation of nibbles that pair delightfully with the sips. The hickory-smoked duck, for instance, is pan-seared to perfection with miso glaze, truffle passion fruit emulsion and crispy pangritata.
verdict: A stroll down a winding garden path, a trot through a bustling hotel kitchen and a short ride in a service lift: this brief journey, albeit confounding, is well worth it, because it delivers you to this Kyoto-inspired speakeasy in the heart of Bengaluru.
